U9 / Novice.sit: It's okay to stop trying if the other team is winning by a lot.
U9 / Novice.why: You always play hard no matter the score. Games can change fast, and the habits you build when it's tough are the habits that make you better. Never stop competing.
U9 / Novice.tip: Down by a lot? Doesn't matter. Keep going. Hard work is always worth it.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your teammate scores a goal, you should celebrate with them even if you didn't help on the play.
U9 / Novice.why: Hockey is a team sport. Celebrating together builds team spirit. Your teammate's goal is your team's goal — every player matters, even when you're not the one who scored.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate scored? Celebrate with them. That's what teams do.

U9 / Novice.sit: When the referee blows the whistle, you should stop playing immediately.
U9 / Novice.why: The whistle means play is dead. Anything you do after the whistle doesn't count and could get you a penalty. Stop right away, every time.
U9 / Novice.tip: Whistle = stop. Immediately. No exceptions.

U9 / Novice.sit: Only the best player on the team should shoot the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Every player should shoot when they have a chance. The more shots your team takes, the more chances you have to score. You don't have to be the best player to put the puck on net.
U9 / Novice.tip: Got a chance to shoot? Shoot. Every player. Every shot counts.

U9 / Novice.sit: Everyone on the team needs to help on defense — not just the defensemen.
U9 / Novice.why: Defense is everyone's job. Forwards backcheck. Centers cover the middle. When the whole team defends, the other team has a much harder time scoring.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defense isn't just for D. Everyone helps. The whole team defends together.

U9 / Novice.sit: Every player on the team should always skate directly to wherever the puck is.
U9 / Novice.why: If everyone goes to the puck, the rest of the ice is empty. Smart players spread out and find open ice. Only one or two players need to be near the puck — everyone else should be open for a pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Not everyone goes to the puck. Spread out. Find open ice. Be ready for a pass.

U9 / Novice.sit: Making mistakes during practice and games is part of getting better at hockey.
U9 / Novice.why: Every player makes mistakes — even the pros. Mistakes are how you learn what works and what doesn't. The important thing is to keep trying and learn from them.
U9 / Novice.tip: Made a mistake? Good. Now you know. Try again. That's how you get better.

U9 / Novice.sit: You enter the offensive zone with speed. A defender is square in front of you, but your linemate is wide open on the left wing. What do you do?
opts: Try to deke around the defender one-on-one.
Pass to your open linemate immediately.
Dump the puck in deep and chase after it.
Slow down and wait for more teammate help.
U9 / Novice.why: The defender is committed to you — which means your teammate is open. Defender takes you = give it up.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defender takes you + teammate is open = pass. Almost always.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. The other team has it and is skating the other way. You're the nearest forward. What's your first move?
opts: Stay in the offensive zone in case.
Immediately turn and skate back.
Skate slowly back and watch.
Go to the bench as fast as you can.
U9 / Novice.why: The instant the puck changes hands, your job changes. You go from attacker to backchecker immediately.
U9 / Novice.tip: Turnover = turn around. Don't wait. Go now.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in the offensive zone without the puck. Three players including you are all crowded near the puck carrier. What's the smart move?
opts: Get as close to the puck as you can so you're ready to grab a loose one.
Find open ice away from the puck — near the net or at the blue line.
Stand still right where you are and call loudly for a pass to come.
Crash the crease hard and try to screen the goalie from that spot.
U9 / Novice.why: Clustering around the puck is one of the most common mistakes at this age. Open ice is where plays are made.
U9 / Novice.tip: Where's the open ice? Go there. That's where the play will come.

U9 / Novice.sit: You can see a defender coming at your teammate who has the puck but can't see behind them. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing and let them figure the play out on their own.
Yell 'man on!' so your teammate knows pressure is coming.
Skate over fast and try to get between them and the defender.
Wait until the check happens and then chase down the loose puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Communication is a decision. 'Man on' gives your teammate a split-second to protect the puck.
U9 / Novice.tip: See something your teammate can't? Tell them. Loud and clear. That's hockey.

U9 / Novice.sit: You catch a pass in the slot, 15 feet from the net. The goalie is leaning slightly right. You have a clear shooting lane to the left. What should you do?
opts: Pass out to a teammate on the perimeter.
Shoot quickly to the left where the goalie is out of position.
Stickhandle and wait for a much better angle.
Turn and skate back out.
U9 / Novice.why: When you catch the puck in the slot with a shooting lane and an out-of-position goalie, shoot immediately. Every second you wait, the goalie adjusts.
U9 / Novice.tip: Slot + open lane + quick release = shoot. Don't think. Shoot.

U9 / Novice.sit: You lose a faceoff in your own zone. The other team's center has the puck. What's your immediate priority?
opts: Argue with the referee.
Go pressure the puck carrier immediately.
Skate back to the bench.
Stand still and watch the play unfold.
U9 / Novice.why: Lost faceoffs happen — what matters is the reaction. Going immediately to pressure the puck forces a mistake or delays the play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Lost the draw? Go hard immediately. The puck is right there.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your defenseman picks up the puck in your own corner. As a winger, where should you be going?
opts: Stand in the corner with the defenseman and help.
Get up the wall to give the D an outlet option.
Skate straight to center ice and call for a pass.
Stay close to the net in case of a loose rebound.
U9 / Novice.why: Breakout positioning means getting to spots that give your defenseman real options. As a winger, that's typically up the wall.
U9 / Novice.tip: D has the puck in the corner? Get up the wall. Give them an out.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team has the puck and is skating toward your net. You're a forward behind the play. What's your job?
opts: Stand at center ice and wait right away.
Skate back hard to give your D support.
Skate to the other team's end.
Yell at your teammates to handle it.
U9 / Novice.why: When the other team has the puck and is coming your way, your job is to turn around and get back — always.
U9 / Novice.tip: Other team going the other way? Turn around and go back hard.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has the puck in the offensive zone. You don't have it and there's no one near you. Where's the smartest place to go?
opts: Stand still at center ice.
Get to the front of the net.
Skate into the nearest corner.
Head back toward the blue line.
U9 / Novice.why: Standing in front of the net without the puck is one of the highest-value things a player can do. It creates a screen, puts you in rebound position, and forces a defender to deal with you.
U9 / Novice.tip: No puck? Go to the net. Someone will find you there.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're a defender and an opposing player is skating toward you with the puck, 20 feet away. What's right?
opts: Skate straight at them as fast as you can and try to knock the puck loose.
Back up and maintain a steady gap while angling them toward the boards.
Turn around and skate backward immediately all the way back to your crease.
Stand still and hold your ground.
U9 / Novice.why: Gap control means not giving up too much — but also not closing too fast. Skating straight at a puck carrier lets them pull the puck past you.
U9 / Novice.tip: Don't rush a puck carrier. Control your gap. Make them go where you want.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in your own zone. A forechecker is right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to skate out yourself past the forechecker.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice.
Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
U9 / Novice.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate takes a shot from the point. The goalie makes the save but the rebound bounces out to the slot. You're 10 feet away. What do you do?
opts: Wait to see where the puck bounces and then go.
Skate hard to the rebound and shoot immediately.
Pass the rebound to a teammate further from the net.
Back off to give the goalie more space to cover it.
U9 / Novice.why: Rebounds are the highest-percentage scoring opportunities in hockey. The player who gets there first and shoots first wins most of those battles.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rebound in the slot? Go get it. Shoot first, think later.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your goalie makes a save and has the puck. The other team has forecheckers coming in. Your winger is breaking back toward the goalie. What should the goalie do?
opts: Hold the puck under the glove and freeze it for a faceoff play.
Pass to the winger breaking back — beat the forecheck.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice to the far end.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far side winger.
U9 / Novice.why: A goalie with the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U9 / Novice.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Start the breakout fast.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is attacking 3-on-2. Where should the three forwards position themselves?
opts: All three forwards skate together in a tight group up the middle.
Spread out across the ice so the two defenders can't cover everyone.
Two forwards go left wing and the third goes right wing.
One attacker goes in alone while the other two hang back at center ice.
U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out on a rush forces the defenders to make an impossible choice. A tight group lets two players cover all three.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush attack: spread out. Make the defenders choose.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck in the offensive zone and is pressured from behind. You're five feet away with no defender on you. What do you do?
opts: Watch to see what they do and trust the play to develop on its own.
Get open and call for the puck — give them an easy outlet.
Skate down into the corner to help them battle the puck free.
Go park in front of the net and wait for a pass or rebound there.
U9 / Novice.why: When a teammate is pressured, your job is to get open and give them an easy option. The call helps them know you're there.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate pressured? Get open and let them know. Be the easy option.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your defenseman has the puck behind the net. Both wingers are covered. The other defenseman is open on the far side. What's the best option?
opts: Force a pass to a covered winger anyway.
Walk out from behind the net alone and skate.
Pass across to the open defenseman to reset the breakout.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and change.
U9 / Novice.why: When your outlets are covered, the D-to-D pass behind the net resets the play. It switches the point of attack and gives the other side time to get open.
U9 / Novice.tip: Outlets covered? Go D-to-D. Reset and rebuild.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. The puck is in the left corner. You're the right winger. Where's your best spot?
opts: Go into the left corner to help your teammate win the puck.
Stay on the right side near the net — the weak side.
Skate back to the blue line and be ready for a point shot.
Skate back to center ice so you're ready for a fast break.
U9 / Novice.why: As the weak-side winger, your job is to stay available on the opposite side. Pucks go cross-ice constantly — you need to be ready.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck in the far corner? Stay on your side near the net. That's where the cross-ice pass goes.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just got the puck in your own zone. The other team's forecheckers are still in your end. What do you do with the puck?
opts: Shoot it down the ice to the far end as fast as you can.
Skate it out calmly — the forecheckers are out of position and you have time.
Wait behind your own net for the forecheckers to get tired and regroup.
Pass it back to your goalie and let them freeze it for a faceoff.
U9 / Novice.why: When the other team's forecheckers are caught deep in your zone, they're out of position. That's your window to carry the puck out with speed.
U9 / Novice.tip: Forecheckers caught deep? Carry it out. You have time.

U9 / Novice.sit: You receive a pass at the blue line on the power play. A penalty killer is closing fast. The slot is open. You have one second. What's right?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
One-time it or shoot immediately into the open slot.
Pass the puck back to the other point.
Skate into the zone yourself with it.
U9 / Novice.why: One second on the power play point with the slot open is a shooting opportunity — not a thinking opportunity. The PK player closing on you takes that lane away in two seconds.
U9 / Novice.tip: Point with an open shot? Release it fast. The lane closes quick.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in the offensive zone with the puck. You have a shooting lane but your teammate is streaking to the net completely unmarked. The goalie hasn't seen them yet. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you already have the lane open.
Pass to your unmarked teammate cutting to the net.
Dump the puck into the corner.
Hold the puck and stickhandle to wait for a better play.
U9 / Novice.why: An unmarked teammate cutting to the net with the goalie unaware is a higher-percentage chance than your open shot. The selfless play is also the smart play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate cutting to the net unmarked? Give it up. That's the better chance.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1 against you. The puck carrier has a teammate to their right. What's the most important thing you do?
opts: Rush hard at the puck carrier to try to poke the puck away before the pass can be made.
Take away the pass — stay in the middle, force the shooter, and let the goalie handle the shot.
Chase the trailing player and leave.
Back up all the way to your own crease and let the goalie handle the two attackers alone.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender's job is to take away the pass. If you rush the puck carrier, the pass kills you. Force the shot — your goalie is ready for that.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 as D: take away the pass. Make them shoot. That's your job.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is forechecking. Two forwards are already deep in the offensive zone. You're the third forward. Where do you set up?
opts: Skate deep into the zone with the other two forwards and forecheck hard.
Stay at the top of the zone near the blue line as the safety.
Head to the bench for a quick change.
Skate down to the half-wall spot.
U9 / Novice.why: The third forward on a forecheck is the safety valve. If you all go deep, you leave yourself exposed to a quick breakout and a 3-on-2 against you.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two forwards deep on the forecheck? You're the safety. Hold the blue line.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just won a puck battle in the corner in your own zone. The other team has four players still deep in your end. You have speed. What's the call?
opts: Reset and regroup behind your own net first.
Go — skate it out fast and attack while they're out of position.
Pass to your D and let them carry it up the ice and decide.
Shoot it down the ice and chase.
U9 / Novice.why: When you win a puck battle and the other team is caught deep, that's a transition opportunity. Going immediately puts them on their heels.
U9 / Novice.tip: Other team caught deep? Go now. Transition kills before they reset.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck behind your own net. A forechecker is coming at you hard. You have a defenseman open to your left. What do you do?
opts: Try to outskate the forechecker and carry it yourself up the wall fast.
Quickly pass to the open defenseman before the forechecker arrives.
Stop behind the net and wait it out.
Shoot the puck down the ice yourself.
U9 / Novice.why: Decision timing is everything. The forechecker closing on you is information — it tells you to move the puck before you get pressured.
U9 / Novice.tip: See pressure coming? Move the puck before it gets there.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck near the goal line, way out to the side. The goalie has the near post covered completely. A teammate is at the far post. What do you do?
opts: Shoot from where you are — any shot on net is a good shot from this angle for sure.
Pass across to your teammate at the far post — the goalie can't get there in time.
Skate to center ice and take the shot.
Dump the puck behind the net and go fight for it in the corner with your teammate.
U9 / Novice.why: Shooting from the goal line with the near post completely covered gives the goalie an easy save. A far-post pass forces the goalie to move across the entire crease.
U9 / Novice.tip: Goalie has your side? Pass across. Make them move all the way.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is cycling in the offensive zone below the dots. You're low in the corner with the puck. A defender is on you. Your winger is at the half-wall. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck alone in the corner and wait for help.
Pass to the half-wall winger and let the cycle continue.
Shoot from the corner and hope for a rebound near the net.
Pass all the way back to the point to reset the play again.
U9 / Novice.why: When you're pressured low in the corner, passing to the half-wall winger moves the puck to a player with more options — they can shoot, pass to the slot, or continue the cycle.
U9 / Novice.tip: Pressured in the corner? Pass to the half-wall. They have more options than you do.

U9 / Novice.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards toward your net. You're the defender. What's the goal of your positioning?
opts: Stop them cold by skating straight at them and poking the puck off their stick quickly.
Angle your body to steer them along the boards and away from the middle of the ice.
Back up all the way to your crease and let the goalie handle it.
Wait for them to shoot from a bad angle and then block the shot with your body fast.
U9 / Novice.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier where you want them — along the boards and away from the dangerous middle of the ice.
U9 / Novice.tip: Angling: push them to the boards. Keep them out of the middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D picks up the puck. The strong-side winger is covered. The weak-side winger is open on the far side. What's the breakout call?
opts: Force the pass to the covered strong-side winger and hope they win it.
Pass to the open weak-side winger — they're the clean option.
Skate it out yourself.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far-side corner area.
U9 / Novice.why: A covered winger is not an option. The clean outlet is the open player — even if they're on the far side. Read the ice, not just the default option.
U9 / Novice.tip: Strong side is covered? Go weak side. Find the open player.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is about to take a shot from the point. Nobody is near the net. What should you do right now?
opts: Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide of the net.
Skate hard to the front of the net and get ready for a rebound.
Back up to the blue line and be ready to defend a fast break.
Call for a pass instead of letting them take a long point shot.
U9 / Novice.why: Getting to the net before the shot arrives means you're in position for a rebound before the goalie can cover it. Net-front presence wins games.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate about to shoot? Crash the net. Get there before the puck does.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in your own zone. A defender is pressuring you. Your teammate is breaking into the neutral zone. They're 40 feet away but wide open. What's the read?
opts: Dump the puck out along the boards and chase it yourself.
Attempt the stretch pass to the breaking teammate.
Skate it out yourself through the pressure.
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for a better option.
U9 / Novice.why: A stretch pass to a breaking forward turns a defensive zone puck into an immediate odd-man rush. The risk is the pass — but if the lane is open, it's the highest-reward play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Breaking teammate wide open? Thread the stretch pass. Big reward if you make it.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending in your own zone. Your D partner goes behind the net to get a puck. A forward is crashing to the net and nobody is covering them. What do you do?
opts: Wait for your D partner to come back out from behind the net.
Call out the open player to your teammates and cover them yourself.
Leave them alone — the goalie has a good view and will make the save.
Stand in front of your own net and let the forward battle your goalie.
U9 / Novice.why: When your D partner leaves to get the puck, you need to cover the open attacker. Calling it out also helps your team organize.
U9 / Novice.tip: D partner left? You cover the open man. Call it out loud.

U9 / Novice.sit: An attacker is rushing the puck at you at full speed. You're the only defender. The attacker is fast. What do you do?
opts: Charge at them at full speed to meet them until someone tells you otherwise.
Back up at a controlled pace.
Turn around and race them to your net.
Stand completely.
U9 / Novice.why: Charging a fast attacker lets them blow past you. Backing up at a controlled pace while angling forces them to slow down or go where you want.
U9 / Novice.tip: Fast attacker coming? Back up controlled. Don't chase. Angle them.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D picks up the puck. Both wingers are tied up by forecheckers. But your center is open in the middle of the ice. What's the breakout call?
opts: Force the pass to one of the covered wingers along the wall.
Pass to the open center in the middle — they're clean.
Carry the puck out yourself through the pressure.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far corner.
U9 / Novice.why: The center in the middle is often the hidden option on a breakout. If both wingers are covered, the center up the middle is the smart outlet.
U9 / Novice.tip: Both wingers covered? The center in the middle might be the cleanest option.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're on the power play at the half-wall. The puck carrier passes it to you in stride. The goalie is leaning to their left. What's the right play?
opts: Stop the puck first and then look around carefully for a good pass.
One-time it to the right side where the goalie hasn't moved yet.
Stickhandle and wait.
Pass it back up to the point.
U9 / Novice.why: A one-timer on the power play catches the goalie before they can reset. Stopping the puck gives them time to adjust — a quick release doesn't.
U9 / Novice.tip: Pass comes in stride with the goalie leaning? One-time it. Quick release wins.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck turns over in the neutral zone and now the other team has a 2-on-2 going toward your net. You're one of the two defenders. What's your priority?
opts: Both defenders chase the puck carrier hard and try to strip the puck before they get a shot off.
Take your side — one defender takes the carrier, the other takes the second attacker — and protect the middle.
Both defenders collapse down into the crease and pack the net to block every shot attempt.
Skate straight back to the goal line and let the goalie handle the 2-on-2 rush on their own.
U9 / Novice.why: A 2-on-2 defense requires each defender to take a side. The most dangerous play is a middle pass — stay in the lanes and make them go wide.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-2 against you: each take a side. Protect the middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is entering the offensive zone. The defender is set at their blue line — they're not going to let you carry it in. What's the best entry option?
opts: Carry the puck in at full speed and try to blow right past the defender with pure speed.
Chip the puck into the zone behind the defender and skate hard to get it first.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your teammates.
Pass it back to the D and regroup.
U9 / Novice.why: A set defender at the blue line means you probably can't carry it in cleanly. Chipping the puck into space behind them and racing to get it first beats the defender's positioning.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defender set at the line? Chip it in and go get it. Don't force the carry.

U9 / Novice.sit: You don't have the puck in the offensive zone and your teammate is being pressured. The defender covering you is watching the puck. What do you do?
opts: Stand still in your spot and call loudly for the puck so your teammate knows you are open.
Skate to a new open spot — cut to the net or find a seam. Your defender will have to follow.
Skate over toward your covered teammate to give them a close support pass option quickly.
Wait right where you are until the pressure clears so you don't crowd your teammate out.
U9 / Novice.why: Moving without the puck forces your defender to follow you — and every step they take to follow you is a step away from the puck carrier. Create problems by moving.
U9 / Novice.tip: No puck and defender watching the play? Move. Make them follow you. Create space.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. The power play passes to the point. You're the near penalty killer forward. What do you do?
opts: Skate all the way into the corner to help your teammate battle for the loose puck down low.
Close on the point player to pressure the shot or take away the dangerous pass options.
Stay in the middle of the ice to block the cross-seam pass and protect the slot area well.
Back all the way up to your own crease to help the goalie see shots through heavy traffic.
U9 / Novice.why: Closing on the point player on the PK takes away a clean shooting lane or forces a rushed decision. You don't have to get the puck — just make them uncomfortable.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK: close on the point player. Make it hard for them to get a clean shot.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate just won a puck battle in the corner in the defensive zone. They have the puck but defenders are still around them. You're nearby without a defender on you. What do you do?
opts: Skate away to center ice.
Get close enough to be an easy pass — support behind or beside them.
Call for a long stretch pass up to the blue line and break out fast.
Stand still and watch the battle play out for yourself.
U9 / Novice.why: When a teammate wins a puck battle in a tough spot, they need immediate support. Getting close gives them a safe, quick outlet before the next pressure arrives.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate won the puck battle? Get close and give them a quick option.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D picks up the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are coming but they haven't arrived yet. You have 2 seconds. What's the key thing your D should do?
opts: Wait until both forecheckers have arrived before deciding which outlet pass to try to make.
Look up immediately and read which outlet is open before the pressure arrives — decide early.
Rim the puck around the boards no matter what the forecheckers are doing on that play.
Skate out of the zone with the puck no matter what pressure is coming from the forecheckers.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading the breakout before the pressure arrives — deciding early — is the difference between a clean exit and getting trapped. The D needs to see the ice before the forechecker takes the options away.
U9 / Novice.tip: D with the puck and pressure coming? Look up and decide early. Don't wait.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The lone defender commits fully to your teammate. The goalie slides across expecting your pass. What's the right call now?
opts: Pass to your teammate just as planned — stick with it.
Shoot — the goalie has moved and there's a lane on your side.
Pass back to the other side again.
Pull up and regroup back.
U9 / Novice.why: When the defender commits to your teammate AND the goalie slides to take away the pass, they've left your side open. Shoot — the goalie has moved.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1: if the goalie chases the pass, shoot. Read the goalie.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team turned the puck over and now three attackers are coming at your two defensemen. You're the left D. The puck carrier is on your side. What's your job?
opts: Rush hard at the puck carrier to try to strip the puck before they get into your zone.
Take your side and force the carrier wide — your partner protects the middle attacker.
Chase the trailing attacker down the far side and leave the puck carrier to your partner.
Back up all the way to your crease.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 3-on-2, each D takes a side and the D on the puck side forces the carrier wide. The partner covers the middle — the most dangerous attacker.
U9 / Novice.tip: 3-on-2 against you: take your side. Protect the middle. Don't both go one way.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're about to receive a pass in the offensive zone. Before the puck reaches you, what should you already be doing?
opts: Waiting with your stick flat on the ice, ready to catch and control the puck cleanly on contact.
Looking up and deciding where you're going with the puck before it arrives — pre-reading the play.
Calling loudly for the puck so your teammate knows you are ready.
Watching the puck travel the whole way to make sure that you don't miss it when it arrives.
U9 / Novice.why: Pre-reading the play — deciding where you're going before the puck arrives — is what separates slow players from fast ones. By the time the puck hits your stick, your decision should already be made.
U9 / Novice.tip: Pass coming? Decide what you're doing with it before it arrives. Pre-read.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Chase the puck carrier with everyone and try to strip the puck back fast.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
All five skaters retreat to your own zone.
Ignore it — you'll get the puck back soon.
U9 / Novice.why: A PP counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures, everyone else recovers. Panicking and chasing creates a turnover.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't all chase.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has a power play. You're standing in front of the goalie. Your teammate at the point winds up for a shot. What's your job?
opts: Skate out of the way so the goalie can see the puck clearly and make an easy save there.
Stay planted and block the goalie's sightline — screen them, and be ready to tip the shot.
Skate to the corner to get position early for the rebound off the boards.
Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can score from a better angle.
U9 / Novice.why: A screener in front of the net on the power play is one of the highest-value positions. You block the goalie's view and tip shots combination creates goals.
U9 / Novice.tip: In front of the net on the PP? Screen the goalie and tip the shot. Stay planted.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the right defenseman covering an attacker on your side. Another attacker cuts across in front of the net toward your side. Your partner calls 'switch!' What do you do?
opts: Stay with your original assignment and completely ignore the switch call from your partner.
Take the attacker cutting to your side, let your partner recover their original man.
Call the referee for a penalty.
Back up all the way to the crease and try to cover both of the attackers at once.
U9 / Novice.why: A switch call means you take the player coming to your side while your partner recovers their original man. It's communication and trust.
U9 / Novice.tip: Partner calls switch? Take the player coming to you. Trust the call.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the defenseman and the opposing team's winger wins a puck battle in the corner in your defensive zone. They're about to pass to someone in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Skate down into the corner and try to re-win the puck battle from the opposing winger.
Seal off the passing lane to the slot — get between the puck carrier and the dangerous area.
Skate to your blue line and hope your teammate wins the battle in the corner.
Watch the puck carrier closely and only react after the pass has already been made.
U9 / Novice.why: Once the puck battle is lost, the priority shifts to cutting off the next play — which is almost always a slot pass. Get between the puck carrier and the slot immediately.
U9 / Novice.tip: Lost the puck battle? Don't chase the puck. Seal the passing lane to the slot.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure. Your D picks up the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are pinching in from both sides. What breakout option gives you the best chance?
opts: Wheel around the net and try to carry the puck out all by yourself straight through the pressure up the middle of the ice.
Reverse the puck to the other side — one forechecker can't follow, buying time for your winger to get position.
Rim it hard around the boards.
Shoot it down the ice as far as you can for icing to get a whistle and a fresh faceoff in the defensive zone right now.
U9 / Novice.why: Under heavy forecheck pressure with two pinching forecheckers, the reverse gives you an extra second. One forechecker can't cover both sides simultaneously.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two forecheckers pinching? Reverse the puck. One of them can't follow.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone setup. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Keep carrying the puck in at full speed until the trap finally breaks down and lets you by.
Pull the puck back to your defensemen, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Dump the puck deep every single rush and grind them down with a heavy forecheck eventually.
Skate around the outside of the trap along the boards and curl back into the middle with speed.
U9 / Novice.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement.
U9 / Novice.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is taking a defensive zone faceoff. Before the puck drops, what should every player already know?
opts: Nothing at all — players should just react to where the puck goes after the official drops it.
Their assignment — who they're covering and where they're going based on whether you win or lose the draw.
Where the nearest door to the bench is in case their shift ends right after the puck drops.
To skate as fast as they possibly can toward the puck the moment it hits the ice surface.
U9 / Novice.why: A faceoff is a set play. Every player should know their assignment before the puck drops — what to do if you win, what to do if you lose. The setup before the drop is as important as the drop itself.
U9 / Novice.tip: Before the faceoff drops: know your assignment. Win or lose — be ready to execute.

U9 / Novice.sit: An opposing winger has broken the puck out of their own zone and is coming at you with speed. You're at the red line. The attacker is fast and has a step on you. What's your priority?
opts: Charge right at them and try to take the puck off their stick before they can get across the red line and into your zone.
Back up at a pace that doesn't let them get speed on you, angle them toward the boards, and wait.
Turn around and race them straight back to your net to try to beat them to the front before they can fire off a shot.
Stand completely still at the red line and wait for them to come to you before you commit to any kind of defensive move.
U9 / Novice.why: An attacker with speed is trying to blow past you. Backing up at the right pace while angling keeps you ahead of them and denies the clean entry.
U9 / Novice.tip: Fast attacker with a step on you? Back up fast and angle. Don't let them get speed going.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but there's barely any time. What do you do?
opts: Try to switch the puck from your backhand to your forehand for a stronger shot.
Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway.
Pass to a teammate outside the slot.
Skate around the net to get a better shooting angle.
U9 / Novice.why: In tight to the net with time running out, a quick backhand release is often better than taking the time to switch to your forehand. The goalie is already positioned for the forehand.
U9 / Novice.tip: In tight with time running out? Shoot what you've got. Don't switch hands.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is cycling in the offensive zone. The cycle isn't working — the defenders have perfectly matched your movement for 20 seconds and nothing is opening. What should you do?
opts: Keep cycling the same way — something has to open up for a shot eventually.
Stop the cycle — dump the puck deep, change lines, and come back with a new entry.
Shoot from the corner area.
Pass all the way back to the blue line point.
U9 / Novice.why: A cycle against a perfectly matched defense is just burning your legs. Recognize when the cycle isn't creating opportunities and make the change — dump, line change, come back fresh.
U9 / Novice.tip: Cycle going nowhere? Dump and change. Come back with a fresh entry.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just got called for a penalty. Before you leave the ice, what should the remaining four players know?
opts: Nothing really — just go out and defend hard.
Their specific penalty kill assignments — who covers the point, who covers the half-wall, and what the plan is if they get the puck.
To skate as fast as they can and chase every puck carrier around the zone without ever stopping until the penalty ends.
To surround the puck carrier at all times with all four skaters so there's no chance for a clean pass or shot on the goalie.
U9 / Novice.why: The penalty kill is a system. The four remaining players need to know their assignments before the power play sets up. Improvising on the PK gives up goals.
U9 / Novice.tip: Going on the PK? Know your assignment. The system works when everyone knows their job.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the last defender back and two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit hard to the puck carrier on the left and try to take the puck off their stick before they can make any kind of play.
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter all the way to the right post to make sure they can't tap in any pass that comes across to the back door.
Back up all the way to your own crease so you're right next to the goalie and can cover any rebound that comes out on a shot.
U9 / Novice.why: Last man back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie. Don't commit to either attacker until they force your hand.
U9 / Novice.tip: Last man back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass. Let the goalie have the shot.

U9 / Novice.concept: Read the goalie on a breakaway
U9 / Novice.sit: You're on a breakaway. The goalie is skating aggressively out to cut your angle. What's the most effective play?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
Pull the puck wide, make the goalie commit to one side, then shoot or go to the other side.
Try a quick straight backhand shot right down the middle before the goalie can get set.
Slow way down and wait until you are right on top of the goalie before making a move.
U9 / Novice.why: An aggressive goalie is looking for an early shot or for you to go straight. Make them commit — take the puck wide, let them follow, then shoot or go opposite.
U9 / Novice.tip: Aggressive goalie on a breakaway: make them commit first. Then go where they're not.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1 and has been set up for 30 seconds without generating a shot. Two PK forwards are pressuring the top. What should the top player do?
opts: Try to hold the puck and fight through both PK forwards to get a shot from the top.
Move the puck quickly across to the half-wall before both pressure players arrive.
Take a slap shot from distance through all the traffic and hope for a rebound chance.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone and start the whole power play over.
U9 / Novice.why: Two PK forwards pressuring one point means the rest of the formation has a gap — the half-wall is almost always where that gap is. Move the puck before both arrive.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two PKers coming? Move the puck before they get there. They can't cover two spots at once.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Take a big slap shot immediately on the faceoff draw to try to extend your lead early.
Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
Dump the puck deep into the far corner and chase it hard with all your forwards.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone to be safe and kill more clock.
U9 / Novice.why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots forces the other team to chase, burns clock, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.
U9 / Novice.tip: Leading late: the clock is your ally. Protect the puck. Make them chase.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the D and you pick up the puck in your own corner. The forechecker is coming from your left. Your left winger is up the wall on your left — open, but the forechecker is between you and that pass. Your right winger is also up the wall on the right, completely open. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass to the left winger — they're your first option immediately.
Pass to the right winger — the forechecker has already cut off the left.
Carry it out yourself.
Rim it around the boards.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading the forechecker's angle tells you which options they've taken away. If they're cutting off the left, the right side is the clean exit.
U9 / Novice.tip: Forechecker cutting off one side? The other side is your outlet. Read the angle.

U9 / Novice.sit: A skilled forward is coming at you 1-on-1 at full speed. They're a good stickhandler and have already made two players fall down tonight. What do you avoid doing?
opts: Backing up and staying patient until they make their move.
Poking check from maximum distance or charging them.
Angling them toward the boards and closing your gap.
Waiting for their first move before reacting.
U9 / Novice.why: A skilled stickhandler wants you to commit early — a premature poke check gives them exactly the step they need to blow past you. Stay patient and contained.
U9 / Novice.tip: Skilled forward 1-on-1? Don't commit early. Stay patient. Make them decide first.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck at the offensive blue line. A defender is closing hard from your right. Your center is open five feet behind you. Your only shooting lane is gone. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck and fight through the pressure until a lane opens.
Drop a back pass to your center — they have time and space and you don't.
Shoot it right into the defender.
Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase.
U9 / Novice.why: When your shooting lane is gone and a player is closing fast, a back pass to a player with time and space is the right play. Don't force the situation — solve it.
U9 / Novice.tip: Shooting lane gone with pressure coming? Drop it back. Someone behind you has time.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're about to shoot from the slot. Before you release, you have a split second to look at the goalie. They're leaning hard to your right. Where should you shoot?
opts: Top right — shoot high over their shoulder on the same side they are leaning hard toward.
Low left — the opposite side of where they're leaning. They can't recover in time.
Right at their chest — power wins.
Wait for the goalie to reset their position before you decide to release the shot.
U9 / Novice.why: Pre-shot reads of the goalie give you a target before you even release. A goalie leaning hard to one side is leaving the other side vulnerable — shoot there.
U9 / Novice.tip: Goalie leaning hard one way? Shoot the opposite side. They can't recover in time.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're on a line change. Your linemates are still on the ice finishing a rush. The play is still alive. When do you go on?
opts: Go immediately — you're fresh and your team needs the help.
Wait until the puck is dead, there's a stoppage, or your teammates are safely at the bench and can't be caught short.
Jump onto the ice as soon as your teammate steps off, even if they're still in the play and skating hard.
Focus on your own play and let others figure out theirs.
U9 / Novice.why: Taking a too-many-men penalty on a rush because you jumped on too early is a critical mistake. The line change is clean only when the puck is dead or your teammate is safely off.
U9 / Novice.tip: Line change during a rush: wait for a stoppage or until your teammate is safely off. Don't jump on early.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just won a puck battle in your own zone. Two of your forwards are already at center ice. The other team has one player back. A 3-on-1 is developing. What's the trigger?
opts: Wait until all three forwards are together in the neutral zone first.
Go immediately — the 3-on-1 window closes fast as the other team gets back.
Regroup back at your own blue line first.
Dump it in deep and head to the bench.
U9 / Novice.why: Transition opportunities close fast. If two forwards are already at center and you have a 3-on-1 developing, going immediately before the other team recovers is how you exploit it.
U9 / Novice.tip: Transition chance opening? Go now. Those windows close in seconds.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're a defenseman in your own zone. The opposing winger has the puck in the corner and keeps looking at the player in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Watch the puck carrier closely and wait for the pass to actually happen first.
Step in front of the anticipated pass lane — intercept it before it arrives.
Skate into the corner to pressure the puck carrier.
Back up and hug your crease.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading a puck carrier's eyes tells you where the pass is going. If they keep looking at the slot, get in that lane before the pass is made — not after.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck carrier keeps looking at one spot? Get in that lane. Intercept before the pass arrives.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. You're being pressured. The other team has a 2-on-1 developing in your end. You have the puck behind you. What do you do?
opts: Keep skating toward the offensive zone and try to create your own chance before help arrives.
Recognize the 2-on-1 developing and get the puck to your D immediately so they can deal with the threat.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone right away and change lines for fresh legs to come.
Stop where you are and wait for your teammates to catch up before making your next decision.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading the play behind you — a developing 2-on-1 — is an important defensive awareness skill. Getting the puck to your D before the 2-on-1 is established gives them a chance to deal with it.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 developing in your end and you have the puck? Get it back to your D. Don't skate forward.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending in your own zone. An attacker cuts from the left side to the right side in front of the net. Your partner was covering them but now they're on your side. What do you do?
opts: Ignore the attacker completely and trust that your partner will just follow them over.
Call switch and take the attacker coming to your side so your partner can recover.
Call for the referee to whistle a penalty on the crossing play.
Back up all the way to the crease and let the attacker set up wherever they want to go.
U9 / Novice.why: When an attacker cuts across in front of the net, the defending pair needs to communicate a switch. You take the player coming to your side — your partner recovers.
U9 / Novice.tip: Attacker cuts to your side? Call switch and take them. Let your partner recover.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. All three forwards are on the left side of the ice — bunched up. The entire right side is empty. You're the right winger. What do you do?
opts: Stay on the left side of the ice where the puck is and help your teammates battle for possession along the boards in the zone.
Move immediately to the right side — the space is there and the D will have to follow you or leave you open.
Go to the bench for a line change.
Stand in front of the opposing net.
U9 / Novice.why: When everyone goes to the same side, the other side opens up completely. Moving to the open side as the right winger creates an immediate passing option and forces a defensive reaction.
U9 / Novice.tip: Everyone bunching up on one side? Go the other way. The space is there.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill and in a box formation. The power play moves the puck quickly around the outside. One of your PK players starts chasing every pass. What goes wrong?
opts: Nothing at all — chasing pucks is actually a great PK strategy.
Chasing breaks the box — gaps open up and the power play gets into the dangerous areas you were protecting.
Chasing tires out the power play faster.
Chasing makes the power play players hesitate and second-guess every pass they make around the outside of the box setup.
U9 / Novice.why: The box on the penalty kill works because every player holds their position and protects the dangerous areas. Chasing every pass breaks the structure and creates gaps the power play exploits.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK box: hold your spot. Don't chase every pass. The box works when everyone stays in it.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're at center ice with the puck. You see a 3-on-2 developing in the offensive zone. One winger is on the left, one is on the right, and a third forward is trailing. Both defenders are inside. What's the read?
opts: Pass immediately to the left winger because that's the first open option you see and quick passes are always the right play.
Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open.
Shoot the puck from center ice right at the goalie to try to surprise them and maybe sneak one in through traffic.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone and chase it down with all three forwards to start a forecheck right now.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 3-on-2, reading both defenders first tells you which option is actually open before you commit. The best puck carrier reads the whole ice, not just one option.
U9 / Novice.tip: 3-on-2 developing? Read both defenders before picking your option. Don't just default to one side.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the center on a rush. The defenders are both backing up. You're at the top of the circles moving at full speed. You have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Pass to a winger immediately — passing first on a rush is always the right play.
Shoot — you're at full speed and the goalie has to set against a moving shooter.
Pull up and stickhandle.
Dump the puck in deep and reset.
U9 / Novice.why: A moving shooter at full speed at the top of the circles is harder for the goalie to set against than a stationary shooter. Use the speed — shoot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Full speed at the circles with a lane? Shoot. Moving shooters are harder to stop.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D picks up the puck. The forechecker is coming from the left. Your left winger is covered. Your right winger is also covered. Your center is late getting back. What's the option?
opts: Force a hard pass to one of the covered wingers and hope they can win the battle down the wall.
Reverse behind the net — the forechecker on the left has to follow, which buys time for the center to get back.
Carry the puck straight out yourself and try to skate through the forechecker coming from the left side.
Shoot the puck down the ice and take the icing whistle to get a fresh faceoff in your own zone.
U9 / Novice.why: When all outlets are covered and a forechecker is coming from one side, reversing behind the net forces the forechecker to follow — buying a few seconds for outlets to get open.
U9 / Novice.tip: All outlets covered and a forechecker coming? Reverse behind the net. Buy time.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. A power play player is standing right in front of your goalie screening them. The puck is at the point. What's your immediate priority?
opts: Go pressure the defenseman at the point who has the puck right now so they can't get off a clean shot through the screen.
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie can't see the shot and that's more dangerous than the shot itself.
Chase the puck instead of covering your assignment.
Back up all the way to the crease yourself to help the goalie block any shot that might be coming from the point.
U9 / Novice.why: A screener blocking your goalie's sightline on the PK is the most dangerous player on the ice. Moving them out is the priority — before the shot is taken.
U9 / Novice.tip: Screener blocking your goalie on the PK? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is about to shoot from the half-wall. The goalie is set. You're the weak-side winger. What should you be doing?
opts: Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide and comes out to your side cleanly.
Crashing the net hard on the weak side — be there for the rebound before the goalie can cover.
Back up all the way to your own blue line to protect against a breakaway the other way.
Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can get a better chance from your side.
U9 / Novice.why: The weak-side winger crashing the net on a shot is one of the highest-value plays without the puck. Rebounds bounce weak side — being there before the shot lands is how goals are scored.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate shooting? Crash the net. Weak-side rebound is your job.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team wins a faceoff in the offensive zone. The opposing center had to tie the draw. The puck goes back to your defenseman at the point. Before the opposing center gets their bearings, what should you do?
opts: Wait patiently for the play to develop normally before you decide what to do with your positioning in the offensive zone right now.
Drive hard to the net immediately — the opposing center just spent all their energy on the draw and the lane to the net may be briefly open.
Skate all the way over to the corner and see what kind of play develops down low before you commit to any real position.
Call for a pass from the defenseman at the point so you can get the puck back and try to set up a play in the high slot.
U9 / Novice.why: Winning a faceoff in the offensive zone creates a split-second window where the opposing center is off-balance. Driving hard to the net immediately exploits that window before they recover.
U9 / Novice.tip: Win the OZ faceoff? Drive the net immediately. The center just fought the draw — they're recovering.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. Two teammates are available — one is at the half-wall with a clear shot lane, and one is right in front of the net with no lane but good positioning. Which do you pass to?
opts: The teammate in front of the net — they're in the best scoring position right on the doorstep.
The one at the half-wall — they have the open lane right now and can shoot immediately.
Hold the puck yourself.
Pass back to the point to reset the play.
U9 / Novice.why: The immediate open lane wins over the better position without a lane. The half-wall player can shoot right now — the net-front player would need to receive, control, and find a lane. Open lane first.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two options — one with a lane, one without? Give it to the one who can shoot right now.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The puck goes from the corner to the half-wall. As the nearest forward, you move to pressure the half-wall player. Who covers your corner assignment?
opts: Nobody — you handle both.
Your D partner rotates to cover the corner behind you the rotation.
Your winger on the other side skates across and trust the play to develop.
You stay in both spots somehow right away.
U9 / Novice.why: When a forward pressures the half-wall, the D partner behind them rotates to cover the area the forward left. This is how defensive zone rotations work — everyone shifts together.
U9 / Novice.tip: You go pressure the half-wall? Your D rotates behind you to cover. Everyone shifts.

U9 / Novice.sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three attackers are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
opts: Backing up too fast and giving the attackers too much free space between the blue lines.
Backing up at the right pace, not overcommitting to the rush.
Staying in the middle of the ice and not moving to one side to take a specific attacker away.
Talking to their partner during the rush instead of just skating back in total silence.
U9 / Novice.why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right pace — not too fast, not too slow — is the correct response to a rush with speed.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush coming with speed? Back up controlled. Never pinch up against a fast rush.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. Your D has the puck and is looking to pass. You're a winger and you're currently covered. When should you break to the wall to get the pass?
opts: Stay right where you are and trust that your defenseman will figure out the play on their own.
Skate to the wall hard, using your speed to lose the forechecker for a split second — make the D's decision easy.
Don't break to the wall until the forechecker has already left your area and given up the chase.
Stand completely still in your spot and call loudly for the puck to get your D's attention.
U9 / Novice.why: Creating separation from your forechecker — even for a split second — is how you get open. Skate hard to the wall to create the separation that makes the pass possible.
U9 / Novice.tip: Covered on the breakout? Skate hard to create separation. Make yourself open.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you have the puck and a decent angle, so take the shot before anything changes.
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
Hold the puck and wait for a better chance.
Pass all the way back to the point and let the defenseman set up a new play from there.
U9 / Novice.why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.
U9 / Novice.tip: OK angle vs. slightly covered slot? The slot wins. Higher danger is worth the risk.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're breaking into the offensive zone with speed and a pass is coming to you in stride. The goalie is set in the middle of the net. You have a half-second. What's the best play?
opts: Wait until you have the perfect angle to shoot.
One-time it low blocker side — the goalie hasn't moved yet and a one-touch in stride catches them off-balance.
Stickhandle to slow yourself down and aim carefully at the open corner before you take the shot at the set goalie.
Pass across to a winger on the other side who might have a better angle at the net than you do at this particular moment.
U9 / Novice.why: A one-touch shot in stride catches the goalie before they can set against a stationary shooter. Stopping the puck gives the goalie time to adjust.
U9 / Novice.tip: Pass coming to you in stride with a lane? One-time it. Don't stop the puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: There's a loose puck in your defensive zone right in front of your net. You and an opposing player are racing to it at the same time. What's your priority?
opts: Get to the puck first and try to skate it all the way out of the zone yourself before the opponent can close on you.
Get your body between the opponent and the puck — inside position wins this battle even if you get there second.
Poke at the puck with your stick from a distance and try to knock it loose without getting into the physical battle at all.
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and get the puck while you block the opposing player from reaching the front of the net.
U9 / Novice.why: Inside position on a loose puck in front of your own net wins the battle even if you get there a step late. Body between opponent and puck the priority.
U9 / Novice.tip: Loose puck in your own zone? Get inside position. Body between opponent and puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on the power play trying to enter the offensive zone. The penalty killers are set at their blue line — two of them. How should your team attack?
opts: Carry the puck straight up the middle every time since you have the extra man advantage anyway.
Use the extra man advantage: hold the puck until one PK player commits, then move it to the open man.
Dump the puck deep into the corner and send all five skaters in hard to chase and recover it.
Skate back into your own zone and regroup over and over until the PK gets tired and breaks.
U9 / Novice.why: The extra man on the power play means someone is always open. Don't just carry into a set defense — hold until a PK player commits, then move the puck to who they left open.
U9 / Novice.tip: PP zone entry vs. set PK: hold until they commit. Then use the extra man.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck carrier has the puck in the offensive zone but is being smothered by defenders. You don't have the puck and a defender is near you. What's the best thing you can do?
opts: Stand still and call for the puck loudly so the puck carrier knows exactly where you are and can make the pass to you.
Skate into open space — cut to the net, move to a seam. Make yourself available AND drag your defender out of the puck carrier's area.
Go help the puck carrier in the battle for the puck by skating right into the pile to try to get the puck loose for the team.
Skate out to the blue line right away so you're ready to make a breakout pass if the other team gets the puck back and clears.
U9 / Novice.why: Off-puck movement does two things: it makes you available for a pass and it drags your defender away from the puck carrier — creating more space for them.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck carrier in trouble? Move without the puck. Create space for them by dragging your defender away.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your penalty kill clears the puck down the ice. Icing is called and the faceoff comes back to your defensive zone. Your two PK players are tired. What matters most before the next faceoff?
opts: Change lines immediately even if it causes confusion in the bench area because the tired players really do need a rest right away.
Get your two best faceoff players and PK coverage players out — fresh legs and the right assignments matter more than speed.
Stay out on the ice because the familiar lineup is better than rolling new guys out cold without any time to prepare or set.
Send out the power play line instead because they have the best sticks on the team and can probably win the faceoff cleanly.
U9 / Novice.why: A faceoff after an icing on the PK is a chance to reset with the right personnel. Getting the right PK players and faceoff specialist on the ice matters more than rushing.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK icing faceoff? Get the right players out. Fresh legs and right assignments.

U9 / Novice.concept: Read the rush before it happens
U9 / Novice.sit: You're in the offensive zone and the puck is about to turn over. Before it does, where should you already be moving?
opts: Stay exactly where you are in the offensive zone and wait to see if the turnover really happens.
Start transitioning early — turn toward your own zone before the turnover is complete. Get a head start.
Skate to the bench.
Go to the corner.
U9 / Novice.why: The best backcheckers start their transition before the puck has fully changed hands. Reading that a turnover is about to happen and moving early gives you a huge head start.
U9 / Novice.tip: Turnover about to happen? Start moving early. Get a head start on the backcheck.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. A teammate is in the slot but a defender is between you and them. You can see a sliver of the teammate. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass through the defender — your teammate is in the slot and you can see them.
Find a better option — a covered pass through a defender is high-risk. Look for the winger or the point.
Hold the puck and skate around.
Take the shot from where you.
U9 / Novice.why: A pass through a defender risks a turnover and an immediate counter-attack. If a defender is between you and your target, find a different option — the pass isn't open.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defender in your passing lane? Find a different option. A blocked pass is a turnover.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. Your winger just gained possession in the corner. Should you pinch to join the attack below the dots?
opts: Yes — always pinch hard and join every attack so you can keep the puck deep in the zone.
Only if the other winger or center is positioned to cover the blue line behind you — otherwise hold the line.
Yes — pinch every single time your winger touches the puck, no matter who is covering behind you.
No — never pinch from the blue line under any circumstances because it is always too risky.
U9 / Novice.why: Pinching is right only when someone is positioned behind you to cover the blue line. Pinching without coverage gives up a breakout against you if the puck turns over.
U9 / Novice.tip: Want to pinch? Make sure someone covers the line behind you first. Then go.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team is trying to enter your zone 4-on-4. Your team has set up in the neutral zone with all four players between the puck and your net. The puck carrier is coming at you. What's the key to making this work?
opts: Everyone chase the puck carrier at the same time to surround them and force a quick turnover.
Hold your positions and make the puck carrier make a decision — force them one way and take away the easiest options.
All four players skate backwards to your own blue line to protect the net area immediately.
One player steps up to challenge while the other three retreat all the way back to the crease.
U9 / Novice.why: A neutral zone defensive structure works when everyone holds their lane and forces the puck carrier into a decision. If everyone chases, the structure collapses.
U9 / Novice.tip: Neutral zone defense: hold your lane. Force the puck carrier to decide. Don't all chase.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team wins a faceoff in the neutral zone and the puck goes back to your defenseman. You're the center — you just took the faceoff. What do you do now?
opts: Stand completely still at the faceoff dot and watch what your defenseman decides to do with the puck before you even consider making any kind of next move up the ice at all.
Transition immediately to your assignment — if you won the draw, get to your position in the rush development. The faceoff result gives your team the advantage if everyone executes.
Skate straight back to the bench for a quick line change because the puck is already safe with your defenseman and your shift is basically over, so the next line can handle the rush.
Celebrate winning the draw with your linemates and tap sticks, because winning a neutral-zone faceoff is a big deal and your whole team should enjoy the moment before the next play.
U9 / Novice.why: Winning a faceoff is just the start — executing the assignment immediately after is what turns a faceoff win into a scoring chance. The center transitions to their rush role.
U9 / Novice.tip: Win the faceoff? Execute your next assignment immediately. Don't watch — go.

U9 / Novice.sit: A shot comes from the blue line. You're standing in front of the net. The puck is coming at the middle of the net right at the goalie. What's your job?
opts: Get out of the way fast so your goalie has a completely clear look at the incoming shot from the point.
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's.
Call loudly for the shot to go wide of the net so the puck rims around for a rebound chance.
Skate away from the blue paint quickly so you don't block your goalie's view of the incoming shot.
U9 / Novice.why: Tipping a shot on net is one of the most effective plays in hockey. Even a small deflection changes the trajectory enough to beat a goalie who is tracking the original shot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Shot coming from the point right at the goalie? Tip it. Any deflection beats their tracking.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in your own zone under pressure. You have two options: a tricky cross-ice pass to a teammate at the blue line, or a simple short pass to your D partner three feet away. Which do you take?
opts: The tricky cross-ice pass — it leads to a faster breakout.
The simple short pass to your D partner — take the safe option under pressure and let them move the puck from a better position.
Try to carry it yourself around the back.
Wait for a better option to appear because one of your teammates will probably get open soon if you just hold the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Under pressure in your own zone, the simple play is almost always right. A tricky cross-ice pass into pressure risks a turnover in the worst possible area.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two options under pressure? Take the simple one. Safe pass first, then build.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is defending in your own zone. The puck is in the far corner. The other team has three attackers in the zone and you have two defenders and one forward back. Where should the forward position?
opts: Skate down into the corner to help your partner battle for the puck and win it back quickly.
Hold the high slot — be between the corner player and the open attackers in the high-danger area.
Go all the way to your blue line.
Follow the nearest attacker wherever they go around the zone and stay glued to them tightly.
U9 / Novice.why: With three attackers in the zone and only three defenders, the forward holds the high slot to take away the most dangerous pass options. Going to the corner leaves the front of the net completely open.
U9 / Novice.tip: Three attackers, you're the lone forward back? Hold the high slot. Don't go to the corner.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
opts: Watch carefully to see if your goalie covers the rebound or plays the puck before you commit to going into the corner.
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck.
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and play the puck in the corner before the opposing player can get to it first.
Back up all the way to the crease.
U9 / Novice.why: Beating the opposing player to the rebound — or at minimum getting inside position — prevents the second shot. Letting them get a clean rebound in the corner means a second chance against you.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rebound in the corner? Race to it or get inside position. Prevent the second shot.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D picks up the puck in your own corner. They're looking up but they can't see behind them. Your winger is open up the wall but a forechecker is about to close on them from the blind side. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing and let the D figure it out themselves.
Call out 'man on!' or tell the winger to hold — give the information that only you can see.
Skate over yourself to get between the forechecker and the winger before the pass arrives.
Call for the pass yourself.
U9 / Novice.why: You can see something neither the D nor the winger can see. That information is valuable — communicating it immediately makes the play better.
U9 / Novice.tip: You see something others can't? Say it loud and fast. That's your job.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your penalty kill clears the puck all the way into the other team's zone. You're the first PK player over the other team's blue line. What do you do?
opts: Slow down because it's probably just going to come back out of their zone anyway so there's no point in really chasing it.
Attack hard — a PK icing or clear can become a shorthanded chance if you pursue it aggressively. Speed wins that race.
Go straight to the bench for a quick line change since the clear is long enough to get fresh legs out onto the ice safely.
Wait for your penalty kill teammate to come up the ice with you so you can pressure the puck together as a tandem up there.
U9 / Novice.why: A PK clear that gets into the other team's zone is a chance for a shorthanded goal if you pursue aggressively. The other team is coming back for it — getting there first wins the race.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK clear going deep? Attack it. Speed wins the race and shorthanded goals are game-changers.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has been hemmed in your own zone for 45 seconds. Your D picks up the puck. Before they do anything else, what's the single most important question they should answer?
opts: What is the current score of the game and how much time is left on the clock right now?
Which outlet is actually open — not which one I want to use, but which one is actually free?
Where is the nearest bench door so I can get a fresh change of players out on the ice now?
What did the coach say about the breakout during the pre-game meeting in the locker room?
U9 / Novice.why: Under pressure after a long zone time, the temptation is to default to a favorite outlet. But reading which option is actually open — not which one you want — is what leads to a clean exit.
U9 / Novice.tip: Long zone time, D finally has the puck? Read what's actually open. Don't default.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. The puck is far away from them. Do you watch the puck or watch your man?
opts: Watch the puck carefully because it's way more important to know where the puck is than to know where your assigned man is.
Watch your man — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Watch both equally at once.
Go get the puck yourself in the corner.
U9 / Novice.why: In man coverage, losing sight of your assignment is how they get open for a pass. Keep them in front of you and use your peripheral vision for the puck.
U9 / Novice.tip: Man coverage in your own zone: watch your man, not the puck. Use your peripheral vision for the puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're playing hard in the offensive zone on a long shift. The puck turns over and the other team is breaking out. Your legs are tired. What's the responsible play?
opts: Push through your tiredness because the play needs you and your linemates are counting on you to come back on the backcheck.
If you can't backcheck at full speed, get off and get a fresh player on. A tired player is worse than no player.
Slow jog back to the D-zone.
Call out to the other team to stop their rush and give your team a chance to get set up in the defensive zone properly.
U9 / Novice.why: A tired player who can't backcheck at full speed is a liability, not an asset. The responsible play is to get off the ice and get a fresh player in who can actually defend.
U9 / Novice.tip: Legs gone and the other team is breaking out? Get off. Fresh player is better than a tired one.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck at center ice. Three options: your winger is open on the left but a defender is closing on them. Your center is open in the slot with a clear lane. Your D is open at the blue line on the right. What do you do?
opts: Pass to the winger on the left — they were the first option you saw in your vision.
Pass to the center in the slot — open lane, dangerous position, immediate scoring threat.
Pass to the D on the right — they're the safest option and nobody is close to them.
Carry it in yourself.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading all three options before picking tells you the center in the slot with a clear lane is the best play. The winger has a closing defender and the D is the safest but least dangerous option. Best chance wins.
U9 / Novice.tip: Multiple options? Read all of them before you pick. The open slot beats everything else here.

U9 / Novice.sit: A forward is coming at you on a breakaway. You're standing on your goal line. What should you do with your position?
opts: Stay flat on your goal line.
Skate out toward the shooter to cut the angle — give them less net to shoot at.
Skate all the way out past your blue line to challenge the shooter far from the net.
Lie flat down on the ice right away and hope the shooter misses the net entirely.
U9 / Novice.why: Coming out toward the shooter cuts down the angle — the shooter sees less net. A goalie who stays on the goal line gives the shooter a huge target. Move out and take space away.
U9 / Novice.tip: Come out toward the shooter. Take away the angle. Give them less net.

U9 / Novice.sit: Two attackers are coming at your lone defenseman on a 2-on-1. The puck carrier has the puck. What's the goalie's job?
opts: Skate out of the crease to challenge the puck carrier and cut down their shooting angle before they can fire a shot at you.
Stay in your crease, be ready for a shot, and watch for the pass — the D takes away the pass, you take the shot.
Yell at your defenseman to do something.
Come out past the crease to the top.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender takes away the pass and the goalie takes the shot. The goalie stays set in the crease and is ready for either — but primarily prepared for the shot.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1: your D takes the pass, you take the shot. Stay set and read it.

U9 / Novice.sit: A shot is coming low along the ice. What's the most effective way to stop it?
opts: Try to kick the puck away with one foot while standing up on your skates the whole time.
Drop into butterfly position — both knees down, pads flared out to cover the bottom.
Fall sideways quickly.
Stand straight up and hope that the shot hits you somewhere on your leg pads or chest.
U9 / Novice.why: The butterfly position drops both pads to the ice and covers the bottom of the net effectively. Low shots along the ice are best stopped this way.
U9 / Novice.tip: Low shot coming? Butterfly. Pads down, cover the bottom of the net.
