U9 / Novice.sit: You make a save but you can direct where the rebound goes. Where should you try to send it?
opts: Straight back into the slot where a teammate might be.
Into the corner where your team can retrieve it.
Over the boards and out of play to stop the attack.
Anywhere far from the net and away from trouble.
U9 / Novice.why: Directing rebounds to the corner keeps the puck away from the most dangerous area — the slot. Your team can retrieve it in the corner and start a breakout.
U9 / Novice.tip: Control your rebounds. Aim for the corners, not the middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player from the other team is standing right in front of you, blocking your view of a point shot. The puck is about to be shot. What do you do?
opts: Give up on seeing the puck and just hope the shot hits some part of your equipment cleanly.
Move laterally to try to find a sightline, and be ready to react to tips and deflections.
Skate hard into the screener to knock them out of the way before the shot gets released.
Call for your teammates to clear the screener and wait for them to move the player away.
U9 / Novice.why: Screeners are a real problem but not impossible. Moving slightly helps you find the puck, and being ready for a deflection means you're not just frozen behind the screen.
U9 / Novice.tip: Screener in front? Move to find the puck. Expect a tip. React.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck comes behind your net. Your winger is coming back to get it but they're a second away. You can get there first. Should you play it?
opts: Never touch the puck because it's too risky and leaving the crease always gives the other team a chance to score on the empty net.
Yes — if you can get there first and your winger is a step away, stop it for them so they have a clean pickup.
Only if the other team definitely isn't coming for the puck too and you have a completely clear path behind your net to play it.
Kick it with your skate to the corner.
U9 / Novice.why: Goalies who can handle the puck are a huge asset. If you can get to it first and stop it for your winger, you've started the breakout and taken pressure off your defense.
U9 / Novice.tip: Comfortable behind the net? Play it. Stop it for your winger. Be an extra D.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player is on a breakaway coming straight at you. They're moving fast. When should you commit to going down?
opts: Right away as soon as they cross your blue line — drop into the butterfly immediately and wait.
Wait until they've made their move — commit when you see what they're going to do, not before.
Never go down at all — always stay up on your skates no matter what the shooter chooses to do.
As soon as they cross the blue line so you are ready for the shot before it gets released.
U9 / Novice.why: The goalie who commits too early loses. A patient goalie who waits for the shooter's move before committing forces the shooter to beat them rather than just beat the commit.
U9 / Novice.tip: Breakaway: be patient. Make them show you something first. Then commit.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is in the corner to your left. An attacker has it but hasn't shot. Where should you be in your net?
opts: In the middle of the net.
Touching the left post — take away the near side.
All the way across touching the far right post.
Out of the crease to cut the angle.
U9 / Novice.why: When the puck is in the corner, the goalie moves to the near post. This takes away the short side — the most dangerous option for that angle. The long side is much harder to score from.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck in the corner? Move to the near post. Take away the short side.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is bouncing in the crease area and both you and your defenseman are going for it. What should you say?
opts: Say nothing and grab it.
Yell 'Mine!' loud and clear so your defenseman knows to get out of the way.
Tap them on the shoulder pad.
Wait until after the play is over to talk about who should have gone for the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: 'Mine!' is one of the most important calls in hockey. It tells your defender to clear out and lets you take control of the puck without a collision or confusion.
U9 / Novice.tip: 'Mine!' — say it loud and say it early. Own your crease.

U9 / Novice.sit: Three players from both teams are battling in front of your net. Sticks and bodies everywhere. The puck is somewhere in the mess. What's your priority?
opts: Watch the bodies carefully.
Keep your eyes moving — track the puck through the traffic. Move with the puck, not with the players.
Wait patiently for the puck to come out of the pile into a clear area where you can see it and make a save.
Drop immediately into your butterfly to cover the bottom of the net in case the puck squeaks out right at the goal line.
U9 / Novice.why: Traffic in front is the hardest goalie situation. The key is to track the puck through the bodies, not get distracted by movement. The puck is what can score — bodies can't.
U9 / Novice.tip: Traffic in front? Track the puck, not the bodies. The puck tells you everything.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is on the half-wall. You're set on the post. A player cuts to the back door completely open on the far side. What do you do?
opts: Stay on your near post.
Quickly push across to take away the back-door pass — your near post is less dangerous.
Skate out hard toward the puck carrier to challenge them right at the top of the crease.
Stay exactly where you are.
U9 / Novice.why: A back-door pass to an open player is one of the highest-danger plays in hockey. When you see the cut, you have to push across quickly to take away that option — the shooter at the half-wall is less dangerous than an open player at the back post.
U9 / Novice.tip: Back-door cut? Push across. The post can wait — the open player is the emergency.

U9 / Novice.sit: You make a save and the puck is loose right in front of you. Two opposing players are about to get to it before your team. Should you cover it or try to play it?
opts: Always cover the puck for a faceoff.
Read the situation — if two opponents are about to get it, cover it immediately. If your team has it, leave it for them.
Always leave the puck for your team no matter what's happening in front of you because your team needs to start the breakout play.
Kick the puck away from the crease as hard as you can to anywhere so the other team can't get a second shot on you at all.
U9 / Novice.why: The decision to freeze or play is a real read. If opponents are about to get to a dangerous rebound, cover it. If your team has control, leaving it lets them start the breakout faster.
U9 / Novice.tip: Freeze or play? Read who's going to get it. Cover when opponents are closest.

U9 / Novice.sit: A shooter is coming at you. You notice their shoulder is turned slightly toward the left side of the net before they shoot. What does this tell you?
opts: Nothing — ignore the shoulder and just play the puck straight up the way you would with any other shooter coming at you.
They're likely shooting to your right — their body is opening to the left which usually means the shot goes.
They're going to deke around you to the left.
They'll pass.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading a shooter's body language — especially shoulder position — is an advanced skill that top goalies develop. A shoulder turning left often means the shot goes right because the body follows the shot direction.
U9 / Novice.tip: Read the shooter's shoulders and hips before they release. They tell you where it's going.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're down and out of position — the shooter has a completely open net. You can't get back to your feet in time. What do you do?
opts: Give up the goal and just see what happens next because there's really nothing else you can do from flat on the ice like this.
Use whatever you can reach — your stick, your glove, your skate — to get something in the way. Any part of you in the lane is better than nothing.
Stay completely still on the ice and hope the shooter somehow misses the wide-open net that you've given them with the mistake.
Call for help from your defensemen so they can slide in front of the shooter and take away the shooting lane from where you're down.
U9 / Novice.why: Desperation saves are real. Even if you're out of position, getting any part of your body or equipment into the shooting lane can change a goal into a save. Never stop until the whistle.
U9 / Novice.tip: Out of position? Reach for it anyway. Desperate saves happen. Never quit.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck moves quickly from the left corner to the right corner. You were on your left post. What's the fastest way to get to the right post?
opts: Skate slowly and carefully across the crease to make sure you don't lose your balance or get caught out of position by the play.
Push off the left post and use a strong T-push or shuffle across — keep your eyes on the puck the whole time.
Turn around and skate quickly across.
Stay on the left post a bit too long and hope the puck comes back to your side before you commit to moving across the crease.
U9 / Novice.why: A T-push off the post is the fastest way for a goalie to move across the crease. You push with your post foot and slide across, keeping your eyes on the puck.
U9 / Novice.tip: Post-to-post: T-push off the near post. Stay low, eyes on puck. It's a race.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team's goalie stops the puck. Put the basic breakout steps in order.
items: Goalie leaves the puck for the defenseman or makes a short pass
Wings skate to their lanes along the boards
D picks up the puck and looks up for an option
D passes to the open winger and the team moves up ice
U9 / Novice.why: A breakout starts with the goalie, then wings get open, D retrieves and reads, then makes the pass. Everybody has a job and the order matters.
U9 / Novice.tip: Breakout: goalie starts it, wings get open, D reads, pass and go.

U9 / Novice.sit: You receive a pass in the offensive zone. Put the steps for taking a good shot in order.
items: Receive the pass and control the puck
Get your head up and pick your target
Get the puck into shooting position on your stick
Release the shot at the target
U9 / Novice.why: A good shot starts with a clean reception. Then you look where to shoot, get the puck ready, and release. Rushing past the target-picking step means you're just throwing it at the net.
U9 / Novice.tip: Catch it clean, pick your spot, set it up, let it go. Good shots follow a sequence.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1 rush. Put your defensive steps in order.
items: Skate backward and get between the two attackers
Take away the pass by positioning your stick in the passing lane
Force the puck carrier to the outside
Trust your goalie to handle the shot
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender's job is to take away the pass and force a shot. Get position, stick in the lane, steer the shooter wide, and let the goalie do their job.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 defense: get between, take the pass away, force wide, trust your goalie.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just intercepted a pass in your own zone. Put the transition to offense in order.
items: Recognize you have the puck — transition starts
Puck carrier looks up for options
Forwards sprint up ice to create passing lanes
Pass ahead to a forward with speed through the neutral zone
U9 / Novice.why: Fast transitions catch the other team off guard. The moment you get the puck, everyone's job changes — recognize it, read options, create lanes, and move the puck quickly.
U9 / Novice.tip: Got the puck? Transition now. Forwards go. Move it fast while they're caught flat.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team enters the offensive zone with the puck. Put the positioning steps in order.
items: Puck carrier drives wide or deep
One forward goes to the net for screens and rebounds
Third forward supports high — near the top of the circles
Defensemen hold the blue line and keep the puck in
U9 / Novice.why: Good offensive zone structure means everyone has a role. Puck goes deep, someone covers the net, someone supports high, and D hold the line. Structure creates chances.
U9 / Novice.tip: In the O-zone: puck deep, player at the net, support high, D on the line. Structure.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're carrying the puck through the neutral zone toward the offensive blue line. Put your decision-making steps in order.
items: Read the defender's position at the blue line
Check if teammates are available for a pass
Decide: carry it in, pass it in, or dump and chase
Execute your choice with speed and commitment
U9 / Novice.why: Zone entry is a read, not a guess. Read the D, check your options, pick the best one, and commit. Players who don't read first make bad entries.
U9 / Novice.tip: At the blue line: read, check options, decide, commit. Don't guess — read.

U9 / Novice.sit: You should always dump the puck into the offensive zone instead of carrying it in.
U9 / Novice.why: Dumping is one option, but if you have speed and space, carrying it in keeps possession and creates better chances. Read the situation — dump when you have to, carry when you can.
U9 / Novice.tip: Dump or carry? Read the defense. Carry when you can, dump when you must.

U9 / Novice.sit: Forwards should skate back hard to help on defense when the other team has the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Backchecking is every forward's responsibility. A forward who doesn't backcheck leaves the team short in the defensive zone and creates odd-man rushes against.
U9 / Novice.tip: Forwards backcheck. Period. It's not optional. Your team needs you back there.

U9 / Novice.sit: You should always aim for the top corner of the net when you shoot.
U9 / Novice.why: Top corner is a great target, but low shots create rebounds, five-hole shots catch goalies off guard, and sometimes a quick release matters more than perfect placement. Read the situation.
U9 / Novice.tip: Top corner isn't the only option. Low shots, far side, five-hole — read the goalie and pick the best target.

U9 / Novice.sit: In the offensive zone, your team should spread out and use the whole ice instead of bunching up near the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out creates passing lanes, opens up space, and makes it harder for the defense to cover everyone. Bunching up makes the defense's job easy — they can cover everyone at once.
U9 / Novice.tip: Use the whole ice on offense. Spread out. Bunching up helps the other team, not yours.

U9 / Novice.sit: When breaking out of your own zone, passing up the middle of the ice is always the safest play.
U9 / Novice.why: The middle of the ice in your own zone is the most dangerous area for a turnover. A picked-off pass in the middle goes straight to the slot. The boards are the safe breakout route.
U9 / Novice.tip: Middle ice in your own zone is danger. Use the boards to break out safely.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your team loses the puck, you should immediately start skating back toward your own net.
U9 / Novice.why: Transition defense is instant. Every second you wait is a step the other team gains. The fastest backchecking teams give up the fewest goals on the rush.
U9 / Novice.tip: Lose the puck? Instant backcheck. No thinking. No watching. Go.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck in the corner. You don't.
U9 / Novice.why: When your teammate has the puck, you want to be somewhere they can pass to you. The slot is the best spot — it's in front of the net and nobody's there yet.
U9 / Novice.tip: No puck? Find open ice near the net.

question.prompt: Click the best spot for you to skate to.

feedback.correct: Great. Skating to the slot means your teammate can pass to you right in front of the net.
feedback.partial: High slot is close — but try to get a little closer to the net so you can shoot quickly.
feedback.wrong: Too far from the net. When your teammate has the puck, go where they can pass to you for a shot.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in front of the net. Your teammate is also in front of the net wide open. There's a defender between you and the goalie.
U9 / Novice.why: Passing to a wide-open teammate gives them a better shot than you forcing one through a defender's legs. Passing is how teams score.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open teammate? Give them the puck.

question.prompt: What do you do?

options.text: Pass to your open teammate
options.feedback: Perfect. Your teammate has an open shot. That's the best play.

options.text: Try to shoot through the defender's legs
options.feedback: The defender blocks most shots like that. Passing gives you a better chance to score.

options.text: Hold the puck and wait
options.feedback: Waiting gives the defender time to get in the way and the goalie time to set up.

options.text: Skate backwards to get more space
options.feedback: You were in a great spot. Going backwards gives up the scoring chance.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate just shot the puck at the net.
U9 / Novice.why: When anyone shoots, the puck might bounce off the goalie. That bounce is called a rebound — and it usually stays right in front of the net. Get there!
U9 / Novice.tip: Shot goes up? You go to the net.

question.prompt: Click where you should go for the rebound.

feedback.correct: Yes! Rebounds come right off the goalie. Stay at the net and hit it in.
feedback.partial: Slot is close but you want to be as close to the goalie as you can — that's where rebounds are.
feedback.wrong: Rebounds don't go that far. Get right in front of the goalie.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck. You don't have it.
U9 / Novice.why: To help your team score, you have to make it easy for your teammate to pass you the puck. That means skating to open ice — not standing still and not bunching up next to them.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate has it? Skate to open ice.

question.prompt: What do you do?

options.text: Skate to open ice so they can pass to you
options.feedback: Exactly right. Finding open space is how you help your team.

options.text: Stand right next to them
options.feedback: Standing next to the puck carrier doesn't help — one defender can cover both of you.

options.text: Chase the puck and try to take it from them
options.feedback: That's your teammate! Let them have the puck. Your job is to get open.

options.text: Stand still and wait
options.feedback: Standing still makes you easy to cover. Keep moving to open space.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in the other team's zone. Your teammate is carrying the puck. You don't have the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: The slot is the best scoring area in hockey. If you go there, your teammate can pass you the puck and you can shoot — or you can be ready for rebounds.
U9 / Novice.tip: In the O-zone with no puck? Go to the slot.

question.prompt: Click the best spot to go for a scoring chance.

feedback.correct: That's the spot. The slot is where most goals are scored.
feedback.partial: You're in the scoring area, good. The slot is even better for a hard shot.
feedback.wrong: Too far from the net. Scoring chances happen close to the net, not out there.

U9 / Novice.sit: You got the puck in front of the net. Nobody's right on top of you.
U9 / Novice.why: When you have the puck in front of the net and you have a shot, take it! Shooting is how you score. Don't waste the chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open in front? Shoot!

question.prompt: What's your first thought?

options.text: Shoot at the net
options.feedback: Yes! You're in the slot with space. Shoot and try to score.

options.text: Pass back to someone at the blue line
options.feedback: That's a safe play, but you just gave up a great shot. When you're open in the slot, shoot.

options.text: Skate behind the net
options.feedback: You had the best spot on the ice and you just left it. Behind the net is not a scoring spot.

options.text: Stop and look around
options.feedback: Stopping gives the defender time to get to you. If you have a shot, take it.

U9 / Novice.sit: Both teams are in your defensive zone (your own end). The puck is on the left side. You're on the right side.
U9 / Novice.why: When the puck is on one side of the ice, you should stay on the other side so the other team can't pass it across to an open player. That's called covering your side.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck on one side? You cover the other.

question.prompt: The puck is on the left. Click where you should be.

feedback.correct: Nice. You're on your side, covering the opponent so they can't get an easy pass.
feedback.partial: Middle of the ice is okay, but being a little to the right-side covers your man better.
feedback.wrong: Don't chase the puck to the other side. You have someone to cover on your side.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck and two opponents are closing in on them fast.
U9 / Novice.why: When your teammate is in trouble, they need help. The best way to help is to get open — skate to a spot where they can pass you the puck to get out of trouble.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate in trouble? Be the escape pass.

question.prompt: Your teammate is getting swarmed. What do you do?

options.text: Skate to open ice for a pass
options.feedback: That's the right play. Your teammate can pass you the puck to escape the pressure.

options.text: Stay far away so you don't get in the way
options.feedback: Staying far away means your teammate has no one to pass to. They need your help.

options.text: Skate behind the net
options.feedback: Behind the net isn't a good pass spot when they're on the far side. Be visible.

options.text: Wait until they lose the puck
options.feedback: Never wait for your teammate to lose the puck. Help them keep it.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is loose in the corner. You're the closest player.
U9 / Novice.why: If you're the closest to a loose puck, GO GET IT. That's how your team keeps possession. Hesitating lets the other team get there first.
U9 / Novice.tip: Closest to the puck? Go hard.

question.prompt: Click where you should skate.

feedback.correct: Yes! Go get the puck. That's your job when you're closest.
feedback.partial: You're close to the puck but the corner is where the puck actually is. Go all the way.
feedback.wrong: The puck is in the right corner. Go to the puck — don't skate somewhere else.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is skating toward the net with the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: If you skate to the other side of the net, the goalie has to worry about you AND your teammate. That makes it harder for them to stop a shot or a pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate has it? Attack the other side of the net.

question.prompt: What's your job right now?

options.text: Skate to the other side of the net
options.feedback: Perfect. Now the goalie has to watch both of you — and if your teammate passes, you've got a tap-in.

options.text: Follow right behind your teammate
options.feedback: Two of you in the same spot means one defender covers both. Spread out.

options.text: Skate back to your own end
options.feedback: Your teammate is about to score — they need a target, not a retreat.

options.text: Stand still in the middle
options.feedback: Staying in the slot is okay, but going to the weak-side of the net is even better.

U9 / Novice.sit: The slot is the middle spot in front of the net. Where is it?
U9 / Novice.why: The slot is the best place to shoot from. Every player needs to know where it is.
U9 / Novice.tip: Slot = straight out from the net.

question.prompt: Click the slot.

feedback.correct: That's the slot! Best shooting spot on the ice.
feedback.partial: Close — that's the home plate area, which includes the slot. The slot itself is right in the middle.
feedback.wrong: Not quite. The slot is straight out from the front of the net.

U9 / Novice.sit: The corners are where you go to get loose pucks.
U9 / Novice.why: Knowing the corners helps you know where to go when the puck is there.
U9 / Novice.tip: Corner = all the way to the side, down low.

question.prompt: Click the left corner.

feedback.correct: Yes! That's the left corner.
feedback.partial: That's the left boards, a bit higher up. The corner is down low by the net.
feedback.wrong: The left corner is all the way to the left, right by the net.

U9 / Novice.sit: The two points are where the defensemen usually stand.
U9 / Novice.why: The point is high and wide — a safe spot for defensemen to get the puck and start a play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Point = up high by the blue line.

question.prompt: Click a point (there are two!).

feedback.correct: Perfect! The points are up at the top on each side.
feedback.partial: Close — that's the high slot. The points are even higher, near the blue line on each side.
feedback.wrong: A point is up high by the blue line, to the left or right.

U9 / Novice.sit: The orange shape in front of the net is called the home plate. Most goals happen here.
U9 / Novice.why: The home plate is where the best scoring chances come from. Know where it is — and try to get the puck there.
U9 / Novice.tip: Home plate = goal-scoring country.

question.prompt: Click the home plate.

feedback.correct: That's it! Goals live inside the home plate.
feedback.partial: Close — that's inside the home plate, but the whole orange shape is the home plate.
feedback.wrong: The home plate is the big orange shape in front of the net.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck in the corner. You and another teammate are both open. You're in the slot. The other one is on the far boards with a defender nearby.
U9 / Novice.why: Pass to the friend who has space. The slot friend is open and right in front of the net — that's a scoring chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open friend in the slot? Pass there.

question.prompt: You're in the slot. Who should your teammate pass to?

options.text: Me — I'm open in the slot
options.feedback: Yes! You're open and in front of the net. Best chance to score.

options.text: The other friend on the far boards
options.feedback: They're covered by a defender. Pass to the open teammate.

options.text: Skate the puck themselves
options.feedback: Could work, but you were wide open. A pass is faster.

options.text: Wait and see what happens
options.feedback: Waiting lets the other team catch up. Make the play now.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck behind the other team's net. You're on the far boards. Your team needs someone in front of the net.
U9 / Novice.why: When your teammate has the puck behind the net, get in FRONT of the net. That's where the pass is going and where the goal happens.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate behind net? You go in FRONT.

question.prompt: Where do you go to help?

options.text: Skate to the front of the net
options.feedback: Perfect. Your teammate can pass you the puck for a tap-in.

options.text: Stay on the boards
options.feedback: Nobody scores from the boards. Get to the net.

options.text: Skate to the point
options.feedback: The point is okay, but the net-front is where the goals are.

options.text: Skate to your own net
options.feedback: The play is here! Help your team score.

U9 / Novice.sit: Three friends are all in the offensive zone. One is in the slot with NO defender on them. One is in the corner. One is at the point.
U9 / Novice.why: The player with the open shot should get the puck. The slot with no defender = wide-open shot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open shot in the slot? That's who gets the puck.

question.prompt: Who should take the shot?

options.text: The friend in the slot — wide open
options.feedback: Yes! Open friend in the slot = best shot.

options.text: The friend in the corner
options.feedback: The corner is too far from the net to score from.

options.text: The friend at the point
options.feedback: The point can work for older players, but the slot is way better here.

options.text: Nobody — just wait
options.feedback: Don't wait! There's an open shot right now.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in the slot. A defender is coming at you. You have a split second to decide.
U9 / Novice.why: In the slot with a defender coming, the best play is to shoot. If you wait, the defender takes the puck or the goalie gets ready.
U9 / Novice.tip: Slot + defender coming = SHOOT.

question.prompt: Defender is coming. What do you do?

options.text: Shoot right now
options.feedback: Yes! Best shot you'll get — take it!

options.text: Pass back to the point
options.feedback: Safer, but you just gave up an open shot in the best spot on the ice.

options.text: Skate behind the net
options.feedback: You were in the best spot. Now you're in the worst.

options.text: Stop and wait
options.feedback: Waiting means the defender takes the puck. Shoot!

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is attacking. One friend is near the net, one is in the corner, one is at the point. The friend near the net is the closest.
U9 / Novice.why: Someone has to be in front of the net so the goalie has to worry about them. The closest teammate is the one who should go.
U9 / Novice.tip: Closest to the net? You're the net-front.

question.prompt: Who should go to the net?

options.text: Me — I'm already closest
options.feedback: Yes! If you're closest to the net, that's your spot.

options.text: The teammate at the point
options.feedback: The point is the defenseman's spot, not the net-front.

options.text: The teammate in the corner
options.feedback: They're going for the puck. The net needs someone else.

options.text: Nobody has to be there
options.feedback: Someone ALWAYS has to be in front of the net on offence.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammates have the puck on the right side of the ice. You're the only one on the left side. Where should you be?
U9 / Novice.why: Stay on your side! If you crowd the puck, the other team covers everyone. Being on the weak side means you're open for a pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck on their side? You stay on yours.

question.prompt: Where should you be?

options.text: Stay on the left side, ready for a pass across
options.feedback: Perfect — weak-side support. If the pass comes across, you've got a wide-open shot.

options.text: Skate over to help on the right side
options.feedback: Too many friends on one side makes it crowded. Stay on yours.

options.text: Skate behind the net
options.feedback: Behind the net isn't a scoring spot. Stay on the weak side.

options.text: Go to the point
options.feedback: Not wrong, but closer to the slot on the weak side is the scoring play.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D has the puck behind your net. You're a winger. Where do you support?
U9 / Novice.why: Wingers have to give the D a short, safe pass option on breakouts.
U9 / Novice.tip: Support along the BOARDS on your side, not in the middle.

question.prompt: Click where a left winger supports on the breakout.

feedback.correct: Yes! Wall support — a clean pass option for your D.
feedback.partial: That works — you're on your side. A bit lower by the boards is even better.
feedback.wrong: The middle is the worst spot — too much traffic. Support along the wall.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck behind the opposing net. You're open. Where should you go?
U9 / Novice.why: Behind-the-net passes work best to the slot — the highest-percentage shooting ice.
U9 / Novice.tip: Drive to the slot to create a scoring chance.

question.prompt: Click where you skate to get a scoring chance.

feedback.correct: Yes! Slot for the shot — goalies hate passes to the slot.
feedback.partial: Home plate is good — slot is the bullseye.
feedback.wrong: That's too far from the net to shoot. Get to the slot.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team has the puck in your zone. Their winger is on your side. What's your job?
U9 / Novice.why: In your own end, wingers usually pick up the other team's D at the point.
U9 / Novice.tip: Winger's check = the other team's D at the point on your side.

question.prompt: Who do you cover?

options.text: The D at the left point.
options.feedback: Yes — wingers cover the same-side point in the D-zone.

options.text: The forward in the slot.
options.feedback: That's usually the centre or a D's job. Wingers cover the point.

options.text: Skate to the puck.
options.feedback: Everyone chasing means the point is wide open for a one-timer.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the middle forward on a 3-on-2 rush. Where do you drive?
U9 / Novice.why: The middle driver pulls a defender in — that opens up the wingers.
U9 / Novice.tip: Straight to the net. Make the D choose.

question.prompt: Click the net to drive at.

feedback.correct: Yes — drive straight. Make the defender pick between you and the pass.
feedback.partial: That's where you start. Keep going — drive harder.
feedback.wrong: Drifting wide kills the 3-on-2. Go middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck along the boards. A defender is skating at you. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Putting your body between the defender and the puck is legal, smart, and keeps possession.
U9 / Novice.tip: Body between defender and puck = protection.

question.prompt: Defender's closing. You…

options.text: Turn my back to them and protect the puck with my body.
options.feedback: Yes — puck on the far side, body as a wall.

options.text: Try to stickhandle around them in open space.
options.feedback: Risky — you might lose the puck. Protect first.

options.text: Pass right away even if nobody's open.
options.feedback: Sometimes. But if you can protect and wait a beat, a better pass comes.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just got the puck. What's the first thing you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Before you can pass, you have to LOOK. Head down = wrong pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Head UP first, pass second.

question.prompt: First thing you do when the puck arrives?

options.text: Pick my head up and look around.
options.feedback: Yes. No information = no good pass.

options.text: Pass to whoever called for it.
options.feedback: If you didn't look, you don't know if they're actually open.

options.text: Stickhandle in a circle.
options.feedback: That burns time but doesn't give you information. Look up first.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D just grabbed a loose puck behind your net. Where should she pass?
U9 / Novice.why: The safest first pass is along the wall to a supporting winger. Up the middle is risky.
U9 / Novice.tip: D-to-winger on the wall = clean breakout.

question.prompt: Where should the D pass?

options.text: To the winger along the wall on either side.
options.feedback: Yes — safe, simple, starts the breakout.

options.text: Cross-ice through the slot.
options.feedback: The riskiest pass in hockey. Gets picked off → goal.

options.text: Skate it out herself.
options.feedback: Works sometimes. Better option if a winger is open.

U9 / Novice.concept: Stand where the goalie can't see you
U9 / Novice.sit: Your D is shooting from the point. You're a forward in front of the net. What's your job?
U9 / Novice.why: Screening and tipping from the net-front is how most U9 goals get scored.
U9 / Novice.tip: Stand in front of the goalie — don't run into the D's shot.

question.prompt: What's your job at the net-front?

options.text: Stand in front of the goalie — screen them, tip the shot.
options.feedback: Exactly — goalie can't save what they can't see.

options.text: Skate away so the D can shoot clearly.
options.feedback: You WANT the screen. Stay put.

options.text: Go to the blue line to help the D.
options.feedback: You're a forward. Net-front is where you matter.

U9 / Novice.sit: On offense, where do your defencemen stand when your team has the puck deep?
U9 / Novice.why: Ds stand at the points to keep the puck in the zone and take shots.
U9 / Novice.tip: Points = top of the circles, near the blue line.

question.prompt: Click a point (left or right).

feedback.correct: Yes — that's the point. Ds hold there.
feedback.partial: Close — points are a bit wider, near the boards.
feedback.wrong: That's not the point. Points are the top corners of the zone.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're tired. Your team just got possession in the other team's zone. Should you change?
U9 / Novice.why: Changes happen when the puck is away from your net — never the other way.
U9 / Novice.tip: Safe to change = puck deep in the offensive zone.

question.prompt: Tired. Puck's in the O-zone. Change?

options.text: Yes — safe to change. Get off the ice.
options.feedback: Correct — tired = off. The puck is deep and safe.

options.text: No, stay until the puck comes out of the zone.
options.feedback: You'll be too tired to back-check. Change now.

options.text: Skate harder instead.
options.feedback: Sometimes. But if you're gassed, change is the right call.

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team has the puck in your defensive zone. What's the most important thing every player on your team should be doing?
opts: Watching the puck.
Finding a man to cover.
Skating to the blue.
Waiting for a turnover.
U11 / Atom.why: In your defensive zone, every player needs to find a man. Watching the puck without picking up a check is how goals happen.
U11 / Atom.tip: Own zone: find your man first. Then watch the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has the puck in the offensive zone. It's sliding toward the blue line. You're the defenseman. What do you do?
opts: Let the puck slide out of the zone.
Skate to the puck and keep it in the zone.
Back up toward the blue line to give yourself more room to react.
Skate to the front of the net.
U11 / Atom.why: Keeping the puck in the offensive zone is the defenseman's first job.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck going to the line? Keep it in. That's your job as D.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is forechecking. The opposing D just picked up the puck behind their net. You're the first forward in. What's your goal?
opts: Crash the net hard right away and look for a rebound chance in tight.
Put pressure on the D so they can't make an easy breakout pass.
Wait at the blue line for your linemates to catch up before forechecking.
Skate back to the red line.
U11 / Atom.why: The first forward on the forecheck pressures the puck carrier to take away easy options and force a mistake.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forechecking: pressure the D. Take away their easy out.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just lost the puck in the offensive zone. The other team is breaking out. What should every forward on your team do immediately?
opts: Stay high in the offensive zone and wait for a long stretch pass.
Turn around and skate back toward your own zone.
Stop and call for a pass.
Stand still and watch.
U11 / Atom.why: The instant your team loses possession, every forward transitions back. Not eventually — immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lost the puck? Turn around. Right now.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck along the boards and a defender is coming at you from behind. What's the right thing to do with your body?
opts: Turn around to face the defender.
Put your body between the defender and the puck.
Spin off the boards and try to skate through the defender.
Pass the puck away right before the defender arrives.
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection is about body position. Getting your body between the puck and the defender gives you time to make a play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defender behind you? Get your body between them and the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck in your own zone with a forechecker right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to skate out of the zone yourself.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
Shoot the puck the length of the ice.
Wait for a better option to open.
U11 / Atom.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. The two defenders take your two wingers. You're the trailing center. Where do you position?
opts: Race hard to the net for a tip-in chance.
Stay high and be ready for a drop pass or rebound shot.
Skate to the corner to cover the weak-side winger.
Stop at the red line to backcheck early.
U11 / Atom.why: The trailer on a 3-on-2 stays high — ready for a drop pass if the play breaks down or to get a rebound shot from the top.
U11 / Atom.tip: Trailer on a rush: stay high. You're the safety and the extra option.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Pass across to the right winger for a quick one-timer chance.
Pass across to the left winger for a clean shot at the net.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Pull up at the blue line and circle back for a full regroup.
U11 / Atom.why: When both defenders go to the wings, they've given you the middle. Driving the lane and shooting catches the goalie off-guard.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. The puck goes to the corner. Your nearest winger is there but about to get pressured. No coverage behind you. Should you pinch?
opts: Yes — go help your winger immediately — crash down fast and support the battle because two players on the puck wins more than one every time.
No — hold the blue line. If your winger loses the puck you'll be caught deep.
Go to the net front — crash the crease for a rebound while your winger battles along the boards and let your partner cover the point area.
Skate behind the net to support — work down low and be ready to receive a D-to-D pass along the goal line from your winning teammate.
U11 / Atom.why: Pinching when there's no coverage behind you and your winger is under pressure is high-risk. If the puck turns over, you're caught deep with a 2-on-1 against you.
U11 / Atom.tip: No coverage behind you? Don't pinch. Hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the point on the power play. The slot is full of traffic. You have a shooting lane to the net. What do you do?
opts: Hold the puck and wait for the traffic in the slot to clear away.
Shoot immediately — traffic in front means screens and rebounds.
Pass cross-ice to the other point while reading the situation.
Skate into the slot with it yourself.
U11 / Atom.why: Traffic in front of the net is an asset, not a problem. Shoot through it — the screen blocks the goalie's view and the traffic creates rebounds.
U11 / Atom.tip: Traffic in front? Shoot. That's exactly what you want.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in a 2-1-2 forecheck. The puck is in the corner. As the high forward, where do you position?
opts: Go into the corner and chase the puck hard — win the battle with numbers by joining your teammate so you can pin the puck along the boards.
Stay high between the two defensemen — cut off passes and take away the middle.
Go straight to the net front — be a screen and look for rebounds while the low forwards battle for the puck along the boards in the corner.
Back up to the blue line and wait — let your low forwards work the corner and be ready to support if the puck comes to you up top there.
U11 / Atom.why: In a 2-1-2 forecheck, the high forward cuts off outlet passes through the middle.
U11 / Atom.tip: High forward in a 2-1-2: sit between the D. Cut off the middle outlet.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're behind your own net with the puck. A forechecker is coming around the left side. Your left D is covered. Your right D is open. What do you do?
opts: Try to carry the puck out to the left side fast.
Spin to the right and find your open defenseman.
Stop and wait for the forechecker to commit.
Shoot it down the ice.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading which side the pressure is coming from and reversing to the open side is basic puck protection under pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forechecker coming from the left? Spin right. Go where they're not.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards. You're the defenseman. You want to angle them into the corner. Which direction should you push them?
opts: Toward the middle of the ice.
Into the corner toward.
Straight backward toward your net.
It doesn't matter immediately.
U11 / Atom.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier away from the dangerous middle and into the corner.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angling: push them to the boards, away from the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. A shot comes from the point. You're standing in front of the net. What's your job on that shot?
opts: Get out of the goalie's line of sight so they can track the puck.
Screen the goalie and be ready to tip or deflect the shot.
Skate to the corner to grab the rebound.
Drop to the half-wall to be an outlet for a pass out of traffic.
U11 / Atom.why: The net-front player on the power play screens the goalie and tips shots. Getting out of the way defeats the purpose of being there.
U11 / Atom.tip: Net front on the PP: screen the goalie and get ready to tip.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck at the half-wall. The goalie is slightly out of position and you have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Pass to the winger breaking to the net.
Shoot at the exposed net immediately.
Hold the puck and look for an option.
Pass back to the defense at the point.
U11 / Atom.why: An out-of-position goalie with a shooting lane is an opportunity. Shoot before they recover.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie out of position with a lane? Shoot. Right now.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're low in the corner with the puck. Your winger is at the half-wall. A defender is pressuring you hard from behind. What do you do?
opts: Try to power through and hold the puck against the boards.
Bank a quick pass off the boards to your half-wall winger.
Spin and skate into the middle ice.
Shoot the puck on net from down low in the corner.
U11 / Atom.why: A quick board pass to the half-wall winger is the reliable escape when you're being pressured in the corner. The boards are your extra teammate.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressured in the corner? Bank it off the boards to the half-wall. Use the boards.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team turns the puck over. The other team has a 2-on-1 and you're the only forward close enough to get back. You can't catch the puck carrier. What do you do?
opts: Skate full speed straight at the puck carrier and try to poke it away from behind.
Skate back hard on the weak side — get between the second attacker and the net.
Go straight to the net to support your goalie.
Peel off to the bench for a quick change.
U11 / Atom.why: You can't catch the puck carrier, but you can take away the pass option on the weak side.
U11 / Atom.tip: Can't catch the puck carrier? Get to the weak side. Take away the pass.

U11 / Atom.sit: Three attackers enter your zone. They overload the left side — two attackers on the left and one on the right. Both your D are back. How should they adjust?
opts: Both D go to the left to cover the two attackers — when the other team overloads one side, your defensemen should both shift there to match numbers.
One D takes the two on the left, the other holds the middle and takes the weak-side attacker — both D communicate.
Both D shift to the right weak side — defend the back door because that's where the pass is coming to create a tap-in goal for the third attacker.
One D goes behind the net to cover — pre-rotate to cover a pass behind the goal line to take away wraparound plays before they develop at all.
U11 / Atom.why: An overload means the D have to communicate and adjust. One D takes the strong side with two attackers, while the partner takes the weak-side player and the slot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Overload coverage: communicate. One takes the strong side, the other holds the middle and weak side.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the offensive zone D. The puck went to the corner. Your winger got there first and has possession, but a defender is closing. No coverage behind you. Your other winger is at the half-wall. Should you pinch?
opts: Yes — pinch down to the corner and help your winger win the battle for the puck.
No — your winger has possession and your half-wall winger can support. Hold the blue line.
Yes — any time a teammate is battling for the puck you should pinch down.
Skate to the net-front instead.
U11 / Atom.why: Your winger has possession and the half-wall winger can support. You don't need to go. Pinching here with no coverage behind you risks a breakout against you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Winger has it and half-wall can help? Hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
opts: Chase the puck into the corner.
Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
Skate back hard to your defensive zone right away.
Go to the bench.
U11 / Atom.why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase.
U11 / Atom.tip: They won the corner battle? High forward reads the breakout. Don't chase — intercept.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in the corner. Two defenders are collapsing on you from different angles. Your only open teammate is at the blue line — a long pass blocked by two defenders. What do you do?
opts: Attempt the long stretch pass to the blue line and hope it sneaks through the two defenders.
Protect the puck tight to the boards with your body, wait for the angle to open, then make a short chip pass.
Try to spin through both defenders with quick footwork and create your own lane out.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and trust a teammate is there on the other side.
U11 / Atom.why: Under dual pressure with a long pass blocked, protecting tight and waiting for an opening is the disciplined play. Two defenders can't both have perfect angles — one will leave a gap.
U11 / Atom.tip: Two defenders collapsing? Protect tight and wait. The gap will open.

U11 / Atom.sit: A skilled attacker gets a step on you at the blue line and is now skating toward your net with speed. You can't catch them. What's the right approach?
opts: Give up the chase and skate calmly back so you're set for the next rush after the scoring chance.
Get your stick into their lane — angle them toward the boards and get your body in front of the shooting lane.
Try to grab them.
Dive forward and slide on your belly to block the shot — your body can take away the whole lane.
U11 / Atom.why: When you can't catch an attacker, get in their lane. Angling them toward the boards takes away the shooting lane.
U11 / Atom.tip: Can't catch them? Get your body in the lane. Take away the shooting angle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your penalty kill gets the puck out of your zone. It's a 2-on-3 as you exit. Do you try to score shorthanded or just clear?
opts: Go for the shorthanded goal — attack the net.
Clear the zone and get back — a clean clear is a win on the PK.
Pass back to your goalie.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
U11 / Atom.why: A clean clear on the PK is a win — it kills clock and resets the power play.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK clear: clean exit = success. Don't gamble on a shorthanded goal.

U11 / Atom.sit: You skate into the offensive zone with speed. You planned to shoot from the right side, but as you enter you see the goalie has that side covered and a teammate is breaking to the net on the left. What do you do?
opts: Shoot to the right side as planned — the coach drew it up this way so stick to the plan.
Read and react — pass to your breaking teammate. The plan changes when the situation changes.
Slow down, circle back to the blue line, and think about what the best option might be.
Dump it into the corner and send your linemates in to regroup and start the cycle.
U11 / Atom.why: Plans are starting points, not locks. If the situation changes, read it and react.
U11 / Atom.tip: Read what's actually there. Your plan is a starting point, not a commitment.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're at the half-wall with the puck. The point defender is cheating toward the net. The slot defender is watching the corner. Nobody is tight on you. What's the play?
opts: Pass the puck down to the corner — find a teammate low behind the goal line to start a cycle with the defender cheating up toward the net instead.
Shoot from the half-wall — you have time and the slot defender is distracted.
Pass up to the point defender — move the puck to the blue line for a one-timer since you have time and the slot defender isn't watching you.
Drop the puck behind you immediately — leave it for a trailing teammate to pick up since nobody is pressuring you on the half-wall right now.
U11 / Atom.why: When the slot defender is watching the corner and nobody's tight on you at the half-wall, you have a shooting opportunity. Take it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Nobody on you at the half-wall and slot D is distracted? Shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two forecheckers are deep in your zone. Your winger is up the wall but covered. Your center in the middle is also covered. Your D has the puck behind the net. What's the best option?
opts: Force a pass to a covered teammate — a quick hard pass can break through the coverage if you put it on the tape before the forechecker reacts.
Wheel and carry it out — both forecheckers are committed to your forwards. The D carrying it out is the best option.
Shoot the puck around the boards — rim it hard along the wall past the forecheckers and let your winger chase it up to the neutral zone.
Wait behind the net with the puck — hold it until the forecheckers commit to one side and then make your decision based on what they do.
U11 / Atom.why: When both forecheckers have committed to your forwards and left the D unguarded, the D carrying it out is the right play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both forecheckers on your forwards? D carry it out. The lane is open.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You win the puck along the boards in your own zone. What's the priority?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Get the puck out of your zone as fast as possible — any clean clear is a win.
Hold the puck in your corner to kill time off the penalty.
Try to score a shorthanded goal.
U11 / Atom.why: Clearing the zone on the PK is priority one. Every second you hold the puck in your zone is a second the power play can set up.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK with the puck? Get it out. First clean option. Don't hold.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Chase both attackers hard and try to attack and score on the rush the other way.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
All five players retreat to the front of your own net.
Ignore the rush and stay in the offensive zone ready for the next chance.
U11 / Atom.why: A power play counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures the puck, everyone else recovers position. Panicking creates a turnover.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't panic.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is breaking out. The strong-side winger is completely covered. The D picks up the puck on the right side. The center is in the middle and the left winger is open on the short side. What's the right read?
opts: Force the pass to the covered strong-side winger — the puck will sort itself out.
Pass to the open left winger on the short side — shortest distance, cleanest.
Carry it out yourself.
Rim it around the boards.
U11 / Atom.why: The short-side winger being open is the clean, low-risk breakout pass. The shortest distance pass under pressure is usually the right one.
U11 / Atom.tip: Short-side winger open? Take the short pass. Shortest distance = cleanest option.

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team is on the power play. Their point man has the puck. One of their forwards is screening your goalie in front of the crease. What's your job as the nearest PK forward?
opts: Chase after the point man to pressure the shot from outside.
Push the screener out of the crease area — your goalie needs to see.
Stay in the passing lane.
Back up toward your own goal line.
U11 / Atom.why: The screener in front of the crease is the most dangerous player on the power play. Your job is to move them out — not chase the puck.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screener in the crease? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Hold at the blue line and let the formation develop deeper.
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready if the puck turns over.
Ignore them completely and focus on keeping the puck in deep.
Call for the puck yourself so you can take the shot from the point.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter-attack lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.
U11 / Atom.tip: Center positioning for counter? Cheat back slightly. Ready for the turnover.

U11 / Atom.sit: Late in a shift, you beat a tired defenseman at center ice and you're a step ahead but they're right behind you. What's the priority?
opts: Try a complex deke to beat the goalie since the defenseman is still behind you.
Shoot as quickly as possible once you're in range — don't let the D recover.
Pull up and wait for a teammate.
Skate slowly to catch your breath.
U11 / Atom.why: With a tired D right behind you, the clock is against you. Get in range and shoot before they recover. Complex moves give them time to catch up.
U11 / Atom.tip: D right behind you on a partial break? Get in range and shoot fast. Don't deke.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your goalie stops a shot and has the puck. One winger is coming back and available. The forecheckers are still deep. What's the goalie's best move?
opts: Freeze the puck and take the faceoff in your own zone.
Pass to the winger coming back — beat the forecheck.
Rim it hard around the boards past the forecheckers.
Shoot it the length of the ice toward the far end.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie handling the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Goalie starts the breakout. Beat the forecheck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
opts: Goalie holds the puck and stops play for a faceoff — three forecheckers means breakout is too dangerous, so a faceoff is the safer play for your team.
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
Goalie shoots the puck hard down the ice — a long clear past their blue line gets the puck out of danger and forces them to reset fully.
Goalie rims the puck around the boards behind the net — the winger on the far side can pick it up as it comes around the far corner quick.
U11 / Atom.why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie made the save under pressure? Find the breaking player. Read the breakout.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They both have speed. The puck carrier is on your left. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on your left immediately — step up hard and take away the shot because the puck carrier is the biggest threat on the rush.
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter.
Race forward toward the net — get back to your crease to help your goalie on the shot coming from the puck carrier on the left side of the ice.
Skate to the weak side and cover the off-wing attacker — take away the back door pass and let your goalie handle the shot from the puck carrier on left.
U11 / Atom.why: Speed on a 2-on-1 is the attacker's advantage. Backing up fast enough while staying in the middle to take away the pass is the correct positioning.
U11 / Atom.tip: Fast 2-on-1? Back up fast. Stay in the middle. Force the shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Always wait for the perfect shot after a catch — take an extra second to settle the puck and pick your spot before releasing the shot on the goalie.
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Passed to an open teammate in the slot — don't shoot unless you have a perfect look, and moving the puck one more time opens a better chance.
Skated in closer to the net first — get to the top of the crease where the goalie can't cover as much net before you release the shot up high.
U11 / Atom.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone for a long shift. The defenders are getting tired. What should your forwards be doing to take advantage?
opts: Slow the pace down so your own forwards can save their legs.
Keep cycling and creating traffic — tired defensemen make mistakes under sustained.
Shoot from every spot on the ice no matter the angle or lane.
Change lines right away before the tired defenders can recover their wind.
U11 / Atom.why: Sustained offensive zone pressure tires defensemen. Tired defenders make bad reads, miss sticks, and give up inside position.
U11 / Atom.tip: D getting tired in your zone? Keep the pressure going. Fatigue creates mistakes.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is cycling and the slot is clogged. All four opponents are below the dots. What's the play?
opts: Keep cycling low and wait for the slot to finally clear out for a scoring chance.
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested.
Shoot from the corner no matter the angle and hope for a bounce off the goalie.
Dump it back in and change lines.
U11 / Atom.why: When all four defenders collapse below the dots, the point is wide open. A point shot from there is uncontested and generates traffic and rebounds.
U11 / Atom.tip: Four D collapsed below the dots? The point is open. Give it up. Free shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the last man back as two attackers come in on you. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on the left — step up hard and take away the shot because stopping the puck carrier removes the biggest threat on the rush.
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter to the right — take away the pass and the tip since the cutter is the biggest threat and your goalie can stop the shot from the puck carrier on the left.
Back up all the way to your crease — give your goalie the best sight line and let them handle the two-on-one from the top of the crease area.
U11 / Atom.why: Last man back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie.
U11 / Atom.tip: Last man back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass. Let the goalie have the shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just had a power play chance and the goalie covered the puck. Faceoff in the offensive zone. You win the draw. Two of the other team's forwards are already breaking out. What's the immediate play?
opts: Set up the power play again and let your teammates handle everything — your PP is already working so you reset in the offensive zone and run it again.
Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP.
Hold the puck along the boards and regroup — let your power play catch up and set up the attack after you've re-entered the offensive zone clean.
Dump the puck in deep and chase — get it behind the defense and forecheck hard since you've got fresh legs on your power play unit already.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a counter-opportunity after a dead puck is advanced hockey sense. If two forwards are breaking and you win the draw, the counter is a better chance than resetting the power play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Win the draw and teammates are breaking? Go. Counter-attack beats a reset every time.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck right in front of the opponent's net. A defender has grabbed your stick and a second is pushing you from behind. The goalie is scrambling. What do you do?
opts: Stop and call loudly for a penalty — the refs will see the stick hold and the push from behind, so let them make the call on the obvious infraction.
Keep your feet moving and get your stick free — any shot or swipe on net is worth taking.
Fall down hard to draw the penalty — going to the ice with two defenders on you is the easiest way to get the refs to blow the whistle right away.
Pass the puck out of the slot area — find a teammate at the point or half-wall to reset the play since you're tied up by two defenders.
U11 / Atom.why: In front of the net under pressure, stopping your feet is the worst thing you can do. Keep moving, get your stick free, and put anything on net.
U11 / Atom.tip: Front of net with defenders on you? Keep your feet moving. Get the stick free. Shoot anything.

U11 / Atom.concept: Read the ice not the noise
U11 / Atom.sit: It's late in the third period. Your team is down by one. You get the puck in the offensive zone with one minute left. Your coach is yelling to shoot. You see a teammate in a clearly better position. What do you do?
opts: Shoot right away because your coach is yelling at you to shoot.
Pass to the better position — the right play is still the right play.
Hold the puck and take a second to think about options.
Skate toward the bench for a line change.
U11 / Atom.why: Coaches yell shoot to create urgency — but the right play is still the right play. A teammate in a clearly better position is worth the pass even late in the game.
U11 / Atom.tip: Coach says shoot but a teammate has a better look? Pass. Read the ice, not just the noise.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has a power play with only 10 seconds left. You're in the offensive zone at the half-wall. No clear shot lane. What's the play?
opts: Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase it with the clock winding down.
Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than nothing with 10 seconds left.
Skate the puck back out, regroup, and try to set up a clean zone entry again.
Hold the puck and protect it.
U11 / Atom.why: With 10 seconds left on a PP, the time to set up is gone. Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than the power play expiring with no attempt.
U11 / Atom.tip: 10 seconds left on the PP? Shoot from anywhere. Time's up.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team forechecks 2-1-2. Both D and one forward are along the boards below the dots. You're the second high forward. The D has rimmed the puck to the far winger position. Who gets it?
opts: You go — you're the highest forward so the rimmed puck is your responsibility.
The near winger goes — they're in better position. You stay high and be the next pass option.
Both forwards chase it hard together so one of you wins the race to the boards.
The D jumps up from below the dots to retrieve the rimmed puck.
U11 / Atom.why: The near winger retrieves the rim their assignment. You stay high as the next option. If both forwards go deep, you lose your high forward safety.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rim going to the far winger? Let them get it. You stay high. Don't both go deep.

U11 / Atom.sit: The point man is winding up for a shot and there's a screener standing right in your sightline. You can't see the puck. What do you do?
opts: Give up on the shot — you can't see it — if there's no way to track the puck just cover the low corners and hope it hits you for a lucky save.
Move laterally to find a gap around the screener, call to your D to move them, and be ready for a tip.
Drop into butterfly early to cover the bottom half — most screened shots go low because the shooter can't see either, so seal the ice completely.
Stay completely still and trust positioning — any movement takes you out of the angle, so hold your spot and let the puck hit you on the save.
U11 / Atom.why: Screeners require active problem-solving. Moving to find the puck, calling for your D to move the screener, and being ready for a tip is the full response. Passive goalies get scored on by screens.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screened shot: find the puck, call for help, expect a tip. Don't be passive.

U11 / Atom.sit: A forechecker is coming hard around the left side. The puck is sitting behind your net. You get there first. What do you do with it?
opts: Try to carry the puck out yourself.
Stop it for your left defenseman who is coming to get it — get the puck to their forehand.
Shoot it the length of the ice and let your forwards chase it.
Freeze it.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie who handles the puck well under pressure is a huge asset. Stopping it cleanly for your D's forehand — not their backhand — sets them up for a clean breakout pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: Play the puck to your D's forehand. Clean stop, clean pickup, clean exit.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your two defensemen are both going to the corner to battle for a puck. An attacker is cutting to the front of the net with nobody covering them. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing — it's a D problem.
Call out loudly to your nearest forward — 'Front! Front!' — so they know to pick up the open player.
Skate out of your crease to cover the open attacker in front of the net yourself.
Drop into your butterfly early and wait for the pass to come out of the corner.
U11 / Atom.why: When your D are both going to the corner, the front of the net is exposed. The goalie sees this happening and must call for a forward to cover it. That verbal communication can prevent a goal.
U11 / Atom.tip: D both going to the corner? Call 'Front!' immediately. Get someone on that player.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're down and out of position. The puck is sliding toward an open corner of the net and you can't get up in time. What do you try?
opts: Give up the goal and reset — once you're down you can't recover, so accept it and get back in position for the next shift after the puck drop.
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction.
Call for a penalty on the shooter — wave your glove at the ref because you were knocked down and ask for a delayed call on the play.
Stay still and trust the post — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing in place is the best chance to get lucky.
U11 / Atom.why: Out-of-position desperation is a real skill. Throwing your blocker hand, extending your toe, or reaching with your stick can deflect a puck that would have been a sure goal. Never give up on a play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Out of position? Reach with anything. A blocker or a toe can make a save.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You make a save and the puck is loose at your feet. Your team has it covered nearby. Should you freeze it?
opts: Freeze the puck immediately to kill the clock — any stoppage you can get late in a one-goal game is good because it burns time off the clock.
Leave it for your team — freezing it gives the other team a faceoff in your zone with 90 seconds left, which is dangerous.
Kick the puck away from the front of your net — send it anywhere away from the danger area and let your team sort out possession after.
Throw the puck to the corner with your glove — get it out of the slot area and let your defensemen go retrieve it behind the goal line.
U11 / Atom.why: Late game freeze decisions matter. Freezing the puck in your own zone gives the other team a faceoff set play — potentially with their best players — in your zone. If your team has control, leaving it lets them clear and kill more clock.
U11 / Atom.tip: Leading late? Don't automatically freeze. If your team has it, let them clear. Faceoffs in your zone are dangerous.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker carries the puck behind your net at speed, going from your left to your right. They're about to try a wraparound. What do you do?
opts: Stay in the middle of your crease and wait for the puck to appear on the other side.
Move with them — push to the right post as they come around and seal it. Don't let them get the post.
Come out past the crease to stop them.
Drop into the butterfly right away and slide across behind you.
U11 / Atom.why: On a wraparound, the goalie moves with the puck carrier and seals the near post as they come around. If you get to the post before they do, there's no open net to stuff the puck into.
U11 / Atom.tip: Wraparound coming? Move with them. Seal the post before they get there.

U11 / Atom.sit: Put the steps of a basic breakout in the correct order.
items: D retrieves the puck behind the net
Wingers get up the boards and create outlet options
D makes a pass to the open winger
Center supports through the middle of the ice
U11 / Atom.why: A clean breakout starts with the D retrieving the puck, wingers getting open along the boards, then the D makes a pass, and the center fills through the middle for support.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breakout: D gets it, wingers get open, pass, center supports. In that order.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. Put these steps in the correct order.
items: Puck carrier enters the zone with speed and draws the first defender
Wide players drive the net and get open
Puck carrier reads the D and passes to the open player
Receiver shoots or makes the next play
U11 / Atom.why: A 3-on-2 works when the puck carrier draws attention first, wide lanes get filled, the read is made, and then the shot or next play happens.
U11 / Atom.tip: 3-on-2: draw, fill lanes, read, finish. Speed and sequence.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just got a power play. Put these steps in the right order to get set up in the offensive zone.
items: Win the faceoff or gain possession
Enter the zone with control — carry or pass it in
Get to your assigned PP positions
Move the puck to find the open lane or shot
U11 / Atom.why: A power play starts with possession, then a clean zone entry, getting into formation, and then executing the puck movement to create a chance.
U11 / Atom.tip: PP: win it, enter with control, get set, move the puck. Don't rush the setup.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is rushing up ice toward you. Put the defensive steps in order.
items: Skate backward and establish your gap
Read the attacker's speed and angle
Close the gap as they approach the blue line
Steer them to the outside with your body position
U11 / Atom.why: Good gap control is a sequence: establish position, read the attacker, close as they approach, then steer them wide. Skipping steps gets you beat.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defend the rush in steps: gap, read, close, steer. Don't jump ahead.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is about to take a defensive zone faceoff. Put these steps in order.
items: Know your assignment before the puck drops
Get to your position beside or behind the center
Win the draw back or tie up their center
Get to the puck first and make a quick play out of the zone
U11 / Atom.why: Faceoffs are structured. Know your job, get into position, compete on the draw, then execute. Players who don't know their assignment before the drop are already behind.
U11 / Atom.tip: Faceoff: know your job, get set, compete, execute. Preparation wins draws.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. Put the recovery steps in order.
items: Recognize the turnover immediately
Nearest player pressures the puck carrier
Other forwards sprint back toward the defensive zone
Defensemen hold the blue line or retreat to proper gap
U11 / Atom.why: Transition defense is a chain reaction: recognize, pressure, backcheck, hold structure. The team that recovers fastest gives up the fewest odd-man rushes.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turnover: recognize, pressure, backcheck, structure. Speed of reaction matters most.

U11 / Atom.sit: You need to make a line change during play. Put these steps in the right order.
items: Make sure your team has possession or the puck is in a safe area
Skate toward the bench — don't coast
Get close to the bench before your replacement jumps on
Fresh player jumps on and gets into the play immediately
U11 / Atom.why: A good line change requires timing. Changing at the wrong time or too slowly creates odd-man situations. Possession first, skate hard to the bench, short change, fresh player engages.
U11 / Atom.tip: Line change: safe puck, skate hard to bench, short swap, fresh legs engage.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is starting a cycle in the offensive zone corner. Put the cycle steps in order.
items: Win the puck battle along the boards
Protect the puck with your body and look for support
Pass to a teammate who's in motion along the wall or down low
Supporting players rotate to create the next passing option
U11 / Atom.why: The cycle is a continuous loop: win the battle, protect, pass, rotate. If any step breaks down — especially the rotation — the cycle dies.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cycle: win the battle, protect, pass, rotate. Keep the loop going.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The other team has the puck. One of your teammates skates to the puck instead of covering their player. What's the mistake?
opts: They should have stayed on the bench longer — too many players out there at once causes confusion, so the fix is a quicker line change on the shift.
They left their man open — in the D-zone, everyone needs to have a player covered before chasing the puck.
They should have gone straight to the net front — the slot is the most dangerous area in your zone so that's where everyone needs to be.
They skated too slow on that play — if they had chased harder they would have won the puck battle and taken possession back for the team.
U11 / Atom.why: Chasing the puck in the defensive zone without covering your man first is one of the most common mistakes at U11. It leaves someone wide open for a pass and easy goal.
U11 / Atom.tip: D-zone: cover your man first, then worry about the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate carries the puck into the offensive zone. You're skating hard to join the rush but you cross the blue line before the puck does. What went wrong?
opts: You were skating too slow to keep up with the play — if you had more speed through the neutral zone you would have caught up to the puck.
You entered the zone before the puck offside. You need to time your entry so the puck crosses the line first.
You should have stopped at center ice and waited — a trailing forward should stay back at the red line until the puck is clearly in the zone.
The puck carrier was too slow getting over the line — if they had pushed the pace harder you wouldn't have gotten ahead of the puck at all.
U11 / Atom.why: Offside kills momentum. The player without the puck must make sure the puck enters the zone before they do. Timing your entry is a basic but critical skill.
U11 / Atom.tip: Blue line: puck goes first, then you. Time your entry.

U11 / Atom.sit: On the forecheck, all three forwards go deep into the corner chasing the puck. The opposing D makes a quick pass up the middle and the other team breaks out with speed. What was the mistake?
opts: Back off the forecheck and let the D-zone defense handle it.
All three forwards went deep — at least one forward needs to stay high to prevent the breakout pass through the middle.
