The defensemen were too far back at the blue line — they should have stepped up to pinch and kept the puck in the offensive zone right away.
The goalie didn't stop the breakout pass across the ice — goalies should play the puck when their team is forechecking hard in the offensive zone.
U11 / Atom.why: Three forwards deep in the corner means nobody is covering the middle of the ice. The second or third forward needs to stay high to cut off the easy breakout pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheck: never all three deep. Someone stays high. Always.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the D at the offensive blue line. The puck is loose along the boards. You pinch down to get it, but the opposing forward gets there first and goes around you for a breakaway. What was the mistake?
opts: You should have pinched harder and finished the check — if you commit to a pinch you have to follow through, so a half pinch is what got beat on the play.
You pinched when the puck wasn't clearly yours to win — if you can't get there first, hold the line instead of gambling.
Your partner should have covered for you — when one D pinches, the other D rotates to the middle, and your partner didn't read the play in time there.
The forward got lucky on the bounce — the puck took a weird bounce off the boards that nobody could have predicted during that battle.
U11 / Atom.why: Pinching is a gamble. If you can't win the race to the puck, you're giving up your position and creating a breakaway. Only pinch when you're confident you'll get there first.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pinch only when you'll win the race. If it's 50/50, hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team loses the puck in the offensive zone. Two forwards stand and watch for a moment before skating back. The other team gets a 3-on-2 rush. What's the mistake?
opts: The defensemen were out of position when the turnover happened — they were too deep in the offensive zone and couldn't get back in time at all.
The forwards didn't transition immediately — when you lose the puck, you turn and skate back right away, no watching.
The goalie came out too far from the net — he was too aggressive playing the puck and couldn't recover in time to stop the three-on-two rush.
The coach called the wrong play on that shift — the system your team was running left too many forwards deep in the offensive zone area.
U11 / Atom.why: Standing and watching after a turnover is the number one transition mistake. The other team converts turnovers into rushes in seconds. Every moment of standing still is a step they gain on you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lose the puck? Turn and go. Immediately. No watching.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. One of your players keeps chasing the puck carrier around the zone instead of staying in the box formation. What's the mistake?
opts: They should chase even harder — when you're down a man you need to pressure the puck all over the zone to force a bad pass from their power play.
They're breaking the PK structure — on the penalty kill, stay in your box and force them to make plays around you instead of chasing.
They should go to the bench to change — a tired PK player chasing around is worse than no player, so they need to get off the ice right away.
They need to ice the puck more often to kill time — any chance to dump it down the ice is a good one when you're killing a penalty off.
U11 / Atom.why: The penalty kill box works because it covers passing lanes and takes away the middle of the ice. Chasing the puck opens up holes the power play will exploit.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK: stay in the box. Let them move the puck around you. Don't chase.

U11 / Atom.sit: During a line change, the fresh player jumps on the ice while the tired player is still at center ice skating slowly to the bench. The ref blows the whistle. What happened?
opts: The ref made a bad call on the change — the refs often miss line changes at this level and this one clearly shouldn't have been blown down at all.
Too many men on the ice — the fresh player jumped on before the tired player was close enough to the bench. The outgoing player needs to be within arm's reach of the bench.
Offside at center — the fresh player jumped onto the ice past the red line while the puck was still in the defensive zone, so the play was ruled offside.
Icing on the tired player — the tired player skated past center without playing the puck so the ref blew it down for icing against the team that was changing.
U11 / Atom.why: Too many men is an avoidable penalty. The outgoing player must be close to the bench before the new player jumps on. Communication and awareness prevent this.
U11 / Atom.tip: Line change: don't jump on until the other guy is at the bench. Communicate.

U11 / Atom.sit: You pick up the puck along the boards. A defender is coming from your right side. You turn to your right — directly into the defender — and lose the puck. What should you have done?
opts: Turned faster on the turn — if you had whipped your body around quicker the defender wouldn't have caught up and you'd have kept possession.
Turned away from the pressure — to your left — protecting the puck with your body between you and the defender.
Shot the puck immediately off the boards — a quick wrist shot toward the net would have been better than trying to turn into a defender.
Called for help louder from your teammates — a quick yell brings a winger low to support you along the boards before you get hit.
U11 / Atom.why: Turning into pressure is a guaranteed turnover. Always turn away from the check, putting your body between the puck and the defender. Read where the pressure is coming from before you turn.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressure coming? Turn away from it, not into it. Protect with your body.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a 2-on-1, you should always shoot no matter what the defender does.
U11 / Atom.why: On a 2-on-1, the right play depends on the defender. If they take you, pass. If they take the pass, shoot. Reading the defender is the whole point of a 2-on-1.
U11 / Atom.tip: 2-on-1 is about reading, not always shooting. Defender takes you? Pass. Takes the pass? Shoot.

U11 / Atom.concept: Everyone covers a man in D-zone
U11 / Atom.sit: In the defensive zone, every skater on your team should have an opposing player to cover.
U11 / Atom.why: Defensive zone coverage means every player picks up a man. If someone is left uncovered, the other team has an easy passing option for a scoring chance.
U11 / Atom.tip: D-zone: five skaters, five assignments. Nobody floats.

U11 / Atom.sit: When your team loses the puck, it's okay to wait a second to see what happens before skating back.
U11 / Atom.why: There is no waiting after a turnover. Every second of standing still is a step the other team gains on their rush. Transition back is immediate — always.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turnover means instant backcheck. No waiting. No watching. Go.

U11 / Atom.sit: On the penalty kill, your team should form a box shape to protect the middle of the ice and the slot.
U11 / Atom.why: The penalty kill box takes away the most dangerous area — the slot and middle of the ice. It forces the power play to shoot from the outside where chances are lower.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK box protects the slot. That's the whole idea. Stay in shape.

U11 / Atom.concept: Give lots of room on D
U11 / Atom.sit: As a defenseman, you should give the attacker as much room as possible so they can't get around you.
U11 / Atom.why: Too much gap gives the attacker time and space to make plays. Good gap control means closing the distance so they have less time to decide, while still being able to match their speed.
U11 / Atom.tip: Big gap = free time for the attacker. Close it down. Take away their time.

U11 / Atom.sit: Talking to your teammates on the ice — calling for the puck, calling out open players, warning about pressure — makes your team better.
U11 / Atom.why: Communication is one of the biggest advantages a team can have. Calling 'man on,' 'time,' 'wheel,' or 'open' gives your teammates information they can't see. Quiet teams get surprised.
U11 / Atom.tip: Talk on the ice. Every call helps. Quiet teams lose pucks they didn't need to lose.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a forecheck, all three forwards should go deep into the zone to chase the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: If all three forwards go deep, there's nobody high to cut off the breakout pass or recover if the other team escapes the pressure. At least one forward stays high.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheck: one or two deep, one stays high. Never all three in the corner.

U11 / Atom.sit: When making a line change, it's fine to glide slowly to the bench since your replacement is already coming on.
U11 / Atom.why: Gliding to the bench means you're on the ice longer without contributing and your team is effectively short a player in the play. Skate hard to the bench so the change is fast and clean.
U11 / Atom.tip: Skate hard to the bench. Every second of coasting is a second your team plays short.

U11 / Atom.sit: When protecting the puck, you should keep your body between the puck and the opposing player at all times.
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection is about body position. Your body is a shield — keep it between the defender and the puck, and they can't get it without going through you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck protection = body position. Shield the puck with your body. Simple and effective.

U11 / Atom.sit: As a defenseman, you should never join the rush into the offensive zone.
U11 / Atom.why: Defensemen can and should join the rush when the situation is right — when there's a clear odd-man advantage or your partner is back to cover. Good D join the play; they just do it smartly.
U11 / Atom.tip: D can join the rush — just make sure your partner has you covered. Smart D jump in at the right time.

U11 / Atom.sit: You've got the puck on the left faceoff dot. The goalie is set, and there's one opponent collapsing toward the high slot.
U11 / Atom.why: The slot is the highest-danger area on the ice. Getting a shot from there beats goalies far more often than shots from the perimeter.
U11 / Atom.tip: Drive to the middle. Inside shots score more.

question.prompt: Click the best spot to attack from.

feedback.correct: Slot is right. Highest-danger area, best shooting angle, goalie can't see through screens.
feedback.partial: Net-front is okay — rebounds and tips — but the slot gives you a direct shot with more scoring chance.
feedback.wrong: That spot is too low-danger for this look. You want the middle of the ice.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're on the left half-wall with the puck. Your center is open in the slot, your right wing is weak-side, and one defender is closing from the high slot.
U11 / Atom.why: Middle ice beats goalies. A seam pass to the slot turns a perimeter look into a high-danger chance — that's the #1 play from the half-wall.
U11 / Atom.tip: Half-wall with the puck? First look is always to the middle.

question.prompt: You've got the puck on the half-wall. What's the best pass?

options.text: Seam pass to the center in the slot
options.feedback: Money play. Middle ice creates the highest-danger look.

options.text: D-to-D pass behind your own net to reset
options.feedback: You don't reset from the offensive zone — that's a pass back and a missed chance.

options.text: Blind cross-ice pass to the weak-side winger
options.feedback: Blind cross-ice passes get picked off and turn into breakaways the other way.

options.text: Rim it around to the other corner
options.feedback: Safe, not dangerous. Keeps possession but you just skipped the best play available.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate is winding up for a shot from the half-wall. You don't have the puck and need to get to a spot where rebounds come out.
U11 / Atom.why: Rebounds come to the slot and net-front. That's where you need to be — a stick length from the goalie, ready to bang one home.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rebounds don't come to the corners. Get inside.

question.prompt: Click the best spot to be for a rebound chance.

feedback.correct: Net-front and slot are where rebounds live. Stick on the ice, ready to whack it.
feedback.partial: High slot is okay for a second shot if the rebound pops out that way — but most rebounds come closer to the net.
feedback.wrong: That spot's too far from the net. Rebounds don't travel that far.

U11 / Atom.sit: You just crossed the blue line with the puck on the left side. A defender is closing hard from the middle. Your winger is trailing behind you.
U11 / Atom.why: Wide carries protect the puck and buy time for your support to catch up. Cutting back into the defender is how you turn the puck over in the worst spot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Wide and protect. Don't feed the D.

question.prompt: D is closing from the middle. What's the play?

options.text: Carry wide into the corner and protect the puck
options.feedback: Wide carry keeps the puck safe and buys time for your linemates to catch up.

options.text: Cut back toward the middle to beat the defender
options.feedback: Cutting into the defender is how you lose the puck — now they're going the other way on a rush.

options.text: Chip it deep behind them and forecheck
options.feedback: Fine if you have no other read, but you had the time and space to carry. Chip is a last resort, not the first.

options.text: Blind pass back to the trailer
options.feedback: Blind pass under pressure + no vision = giveaway.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're on the left boards with the puck. F1 is forechecking you. Your D is open at the point.
U11 / Atom.why: When pressure takes away your low options, the point is a safe reset that keeps possession and can turn into a shot-tip.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressured low? Rim or pass it high.

question.prompt: F1 is on you. What's your best play?

options.text: Pass back to your D at the point
options.feedback: That's the low-to-high cycle. Resets the play, D can shoot or move it for a better look.

options.text: Try a cross-ice pass to the weak-side winger
options.feedback: Cross-ice through the middle is a turnover waiting to happen.

options.text: Throw it off the boards to exit the zone
options.feedback: You don't leave the O-zone when you have a safe pass. Keep possession.

options.text: Try to beat the F1 one-on-one
options.feedback: Sometimes you can — but passing to your open D is the higher-percentage play.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're F1 on the forecheck. Their D is about to pick up the puck behind the net and make a play.
U11 / Atom.why: F1's job is to take away time and space. You angle them to one side so your teammate can predict where the pass is going and jump the outlet.
U11 / Atom.tip: F1 doesn't go for the puck — F1 takes away the easy option.

question.prompt: Click where you angle the puck carrier.

feedback.correct: Nice. You force them to the weak-hand side and your teammate closes the boards.
feedback.partial: Behind the net isn't where you angle — you want to force them to commit to one side.
feedback.wrong: That direction leaves the puck carrier with too many options. Angle them to one specific side of the ice.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck behind your net. Your D partner is on the other side, open. F1 is closing fast.
U11 / Atom.why: D-to-D swings the puck away from the forechecker and buys time. It's the simplest, most reliable breakout option when you have a clean partner pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: D pressure? D-to-D first, always.

question.prompt: F1 is coming. What do you do?

options.text: D-to-D pass to your partner
options.feedback: Clean. Your partner gets it in space and starts the breakout.

options.text: Try to skate out yourself
options.feedback: Skating into the F1 with a teammate open is forcing it. D-to-D was there.

options.text: Rim it up the strong-side boards
options.feedback: Rim works if the partner isn't open — but they were. Take the easy pass.

options.text: Throw it off the glass
options.feedback: Giving up possession on purpose when you have a partner pass is a bad habit.

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team has the puck and is coming up the ice. You're the back-checking forward. There's a strong-side winger and a weak-side winger on the rush.
U11 / Atom.why: Your job on the backcheck is to take away the middle lane so the puck can't find the trailing forward. You don't chase the puck — you cover the slot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Backcheck the middle, not the puck.

question.prompt: Click the lane you cover as the back-checker.

feedback.correct: Middle lane. No trailing forward gets the puck if you're there.
feedback.partial: You're in the right general area, but the middle lane is the most dangerous. Get into the slot.
feedback.wrong: That's a boards lane — your D-men have the boards. You have the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the RW on an O-zone faceoff. Your center wins it cleanly back to the RD at the point.
U11 / Atom.why: Off a clean O-zone draw, the point shot is coming almost immediately. Your job is to get to the net or the slot — not stay at the boards.
U11 / Atom.tip: Win the draw clean? Drive the net fast.

question.prompt: Your D has the puck. What's your move?

options.text: Drive the net for tip-screen-rebound
options.feedback: That's the point of the O-zone draw win. Arrive at the net as the shot arrives.

options.text: Hold your spot on the boards
options.feedback: Hanging on the boards means you're not a scoring threat. Get inside.

options.text: Pull back to the slot and wait
options.feedback: Slot is better than the boards, but the net-front is where the goals come from off point shots.

options.text: Peel away to the corner
options.feedback: Corners don't score off point shots. Net-front does.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D is about to shoot from the point. You're the forward who needs to set up as the screen.
U11 / Atom.why: A good screen stands in the goalie's eyes without getting in the shot lane. Too close to the goalie and you're in the crease; too far out and you're just traffic.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screen the eyes, not the shot lane.

question.prompt: Click the best screening spot.

feedback.correct: Slot is the screen sweet spot. You block the goalie's view without sitting in the crease.
feedback.partial: Net-front works for tips, but you're a bit too close — a lateral move and the goalie sees the shot.
feedback.wrong: You're not in the shot lane from there. A screen only works between the shooter and the goalie.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're skating into the slot with the puck. No defender is between you and the goalie. The goalie is a little deep in the net.
U11 / Atom.why: When the goalie is deep and you have the slot with speed, shooting is the right read. Driving past gives the goalie time to reset and takes away your shooting angle.
U11 / Atom.tip: Slot + speed + goalie deep? Shoot now.

question.prompt: You're in the slot with speed. What do you do?

options.text: Shoot — low glove-side
options.feedback: Yes. You have space, you have speed, and the goalie is deep. Shoot.

options.text: Cut to the net for a backhand
options.feedback: A net drive works but you gave up the better shooting angle by getting too close.

options.text: Stop and look for a pass
options.feedback: Stopping kills your advantage. You had the chance — take it.

options.text: Drop it back for the trailer
options.feedback: Drop passes work when you're denied. You had a clean look — shoot it.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is cycling the puck low on the right side. You're the weak-side winger. Where do you support?
U11 / Atom.why: The weak-side winger on a cycle fills the high slot — so they can take a pass for a quick release or be the receiver on a cross-seam play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cycle on one side? Weak-side winger fills the high slot.

question.prompt: Click where you support as the weak-side winger.

feedback.correct: High slot. You're the seam option and a quick-release threat.
feedback.partial: Close to the right idea — but the high slot is the most dangerous spot for a cross-ice feed.
feedback.wrong: That spot gives up the seam. Weak-side stays in the high slot when the puck is low.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're skating up the wing with the puck. The D is stepping up at the blue line with no gap. Your linemate is trailing.
U11 / Atom.why: When the D steps up with no gap and you have no support with speed, chipping past them behind the D lets you get into the O-zone with a forecheck — better than a turnover at the line.
U11 / Atom.tip: D stepping up + no support? Chip and chase.

question.prompt: D is stepping up. What's the play?

options.text: Chip it past them and chase it down
options.feedback: Right call. Your speed takes you past the D, and the chip beats the step-up.

options.text: Try to deke them
options.feedback: No gap + speed on the D = almost impossible to deke. Turnover risk too high.

options.text: Pull up and swing back for support
options.feedback: Works if you have the time — but chipping in is a better use of your speed here.

options.text: Fire the puck across to your trailer
options.feedback: Cross-ice in the neutral zone is a rush the other way waiting to happen.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the D on the offensive blue line. The puck rims around the boards toward your side. A winger is skating back to support.
U11 / Atom.why: A good pinch kills the breakout before it starts. But you only pinch if you can reach the puck before their winger — and if there's support behind you. Misread it and it's a 2-on-1 the other way.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pinch only if you beat the winger to it AND have support.

question.prompt: Puck's rimming toward you. Pinch or hold?

options.text: Pinch — kill the puck on the wall
options.feedback: With C covering above you and the puck arriving before their winger, the pinch keeps the O-zone alive.

options.text: Back off — let them exit and regroup
options.feedback: Safe but passive. You had the pinch and gave it up. Only back off if you aren't sure you win the race.

options.text: Stand still and see what happens
options.feedback: Hesitation is the worst read. Commit to pinch or retreat — don't freeze.

options.text: Chase the puck into the corner
options.feedback: D don't chase into the corner — you lose your position. Pinch on the wall, not below the hash.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the LW. Your LD has the puck at the left point. The puck is about to move.
U11 / Atom.why: LW support on a point release lives on the strong-side boards or in the slot — not at the net-front (that's the C's job) and not across the ice.
U11 / Atom.tip: Your D has it at the point? You're on the boards or in the slot.

question.prompt: Where should you be as the LW?

feedback.correct: That's the read. You're either available for the shot-pass or threatening the slot off the release.
feedback.partial: Close — but the net-front is the C's spot. Stay on the boards or in the slot to give a shot option.
feedback.wrong: That's not your lane. LW supports strong-side on a point release.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is cycling the puck on the right-side boards. You're the weak-side LW. Where do you sit?
U11 / Atom.why: Weak-side winger's job is to hold the slot or high slot — ready for a cross-seam pass or a shot rebound. Go too low and you clog the net-front; go too wide and you're out of the play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side wing = hold the slot seam.

question.prompt: Where does the weak-side LW sit?

feedback.correct: Right read. You're a seam-pass threat and you can hunt rebounds from the middle.
feedback.partial: Close — sit a little more central. Weak-side wing lives in the slot seam, not the dot.
feedback.wrong: That's out of the play. The weak-side wing has to hold the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the LD with the puck at the point. Your C is open in the slot. Your RD is at the right point. Your RW is on the half-wall.
U11 / Atom.why: From the point, the seam pass to the slot is the highest-danger play. D-to-D is a reset, not a chance. Forcing a shot with no screen feeds the goalie.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open seam to the slot? Hit it.

question.prompt: Best play from the point?

options.text: Seam pass to the C in the slot
options.feedback: Money play. Slot pass beats the box and gives you a high-danger shot.

options.text: Shot from the point — no screen
options.feedback: No screen means the goalie sees it the whole way. That's a save or a rebound the other way.

options.text: D-to-D across to your partner
options.feedback: Safe reset, but you had the seam. Take the chance when it's there.

options.text: Dump it to the corner
options.feedback: You had the puck and options. Dumping gives up possession for nothing.

U11 / Atom.sit: Look at your team's forwards. Two are at the point. One is in the slot. One is in the corner. One of the forwards at the point drifted up from where they should be.
U11 / Atom.why: Forwards at the point = defence coverage missing. The F who drifted up is the one who should have stayed lower to support the cycle or hold the slot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Two forwards at the point? One of them is in the wrong spot.

question.prompt: Who is out of position?

options.text: The RW at the right point
options.feedback: Right read. Wingers don't live at the point — that's the D's spot. The RW should be on the boards or in the slot.

options.text: The LD at the left point
options.feedback: LD belongs at the point — that's exactly where they should be.

options.text: The LW in the corner
options.feedback: The LW is supporting the puck in the corner. Good spot.

options.text: The C in the slot
options.feedback: The C is in the scoring area where they should be.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck on the right half-wall. Your D is open at the point. Your C is driving the net. Your LW is weak-side.
U11 / Atom.why: Low-to-high (boards up to point) is the safest cycle release and creates a shot-pass off a clean D shot. Seam passes are tempting but get picked off when the slot is covered.
U11 / Atom.tip: On the wall with pressure? Low-to-high.

question.prompt: Best cycle option?

options.text: Pass low-to-high to the RD
options.feedback: Clean cycle release. D has the shot, bodies in front for tips.

options.text: Seam pass to the LW weak-side
options.feedback: Nice thought, but it goes through two opponents. Too risky under pressure.

options.text: Cross-ice to the C at the net
options.feedback: Defender in the slot picks that off — it's a rush the other way.

options.text: Skate behind the net
options.feedback: You had a clean cycle play. Behind the net kills the momentum.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're F1 forechecking. The other team's D just picked up the puck behind their net. They have options both ways.
U11 / Atom.why: F1's job is to angle the puck carrier to ONE side of the ice — don't chase the puck. Forcing them to one side lets F2 and F3 predict the play and jump the outlet.
U11 / Atom.tip: F1 doesn't chase — F1 cuts off one side.

question.prompt: What's F1's job?

options.text: Angle the D to one side — force them to commit
options.feedback: Exactly. Make them predictable so F2 can jump the outlet.

options.text: Skate straight at the puck
options.feedback: Straight-on gives the D both sides. They'll pick the open one.

options.text: Back off to the blue line and set up a 1-2-2
options.feedback: You're F1 — that's a passive read that gives up the forecheck entirely.

options.text: Stand still and wait
options.feedback: The D gets time and space. F1 has to pressure.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the C in your own zone. Your D just picked up the puck on the left boards. The forecheck is coming.
U11 / Atom.why: The C on a breakout is the D's primary outlet — swing low behind the D and read their pressure. Sitting too high or too low kills the two-option read the D needs.
U11 / Atom.tip: C on breakout = low and behind your D.

question.prompt: Where's your support?

options.text: Swing low behind your D — give them a two-option read
options.feedback: That's C support. You're the safety-valve outlet, moving with speed.

options.text: Stay high in the neutral zone
options.feedback: Too far — your D has no short outlet. They get pinned on the wall.

options.text: Go to the far boards
options.feedback: Cross-ice outlet in your own zone is a turnover and a breakaway.

options.text: Skate to the net-front
options.feedback: Wrong end of the ice. Help the breakout, not offensive work.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're C in front of the net. The opponent D is on your back, screening you. The puck is coming from your point.
U11 / Atom.why: Net-front in a battle: stick on the ice, stay strong with your feet, be ready for tips and rebounds. Getting pushed out or letting the opponent clear you kills the shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Net-front = stick on the ice and hold your ground.

question.prompt: Shot incoming. What do you do?

options.text: Stick on the ice, hold ground, hunt the tip
options.feedback: That's net-front work. Tips and rebounds win games.

options.text: Cycle out to the corner
options.feedback: You're abandoning the scoring area right when the shot is coming.

options.text: Pull the defender away from the net
options.feedback: Okay read if the screen is already beaten, but first job is to hold the front.

options.text: Skate to the slot for a one-timer
options.feedback: You leave the net-front empty. D shot with no screen is a save.

U11 / Atom.sit: The opponent has the puck at their point. Your strong-side D is pressuring the point. You're the weak-side D.
U11 / Atom.why: Weak-side D holds the slot — the most dangerous pass target. Stepping up to challenge the point leaves the slot wide open for a cross-seam goal.
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side D = slot, not the point.

question.prompt: What's your coverage?

options.text: Hold the slot — cover the cross-seam target
options.feedback: That's the read. Weak-side D's job is to take away the home plate.

options.text: Step up to challenge the opposite point
options.feedback: Leaves the slot wide open. Cross-seam pass and it's a goal.

options.text: Collapse to the net-front
options.feedback: Net-front is the F's job. Weak-side D covers the slot, not the crease.

options.text: Skate to the boards
options.feedback: Not your lane at all. Slot coverage is non-negotiable for weak-side D.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate has the puck on the wall under heavy pressure. You're the closest support. Your D is way at the point.
U11 / Atom.why: Under pressure on the wall, your teammate needs a SHORT clean outlet, not a long diagonal. A nearby support option lets them escape the pressure and keep possession.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate pinned? Get close enough to bail them out.

question.prompt: How do you help?

options.text: Offer a short clean puck-support option
options.feedback: Right read. Short pass beats the forecheck. Possession stays yours.

options.text: Skate to the far boards for a long pass option
options.feedback: Too far. Long passes under pressure get picked off.

options.text: Stay at the net for a shot option
options.feedback: Your teammate can't hit the net from pinned on the wall. They need an outlet.

options.text: Skate away to spread the defenders
options.feedback: Sometimes, but your teammate needs a present option right now, not spacing.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're a winger on the wall. The D looks to pass. There's pressure on the wall.
U11 / Atom.why: Under pressure on the wall, the middle D (or C) is usually the better release — but not always.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressure on wall → middle. No pressure → wall.

question.prompt: Pressure's on you. You want the D to…

options.text: Pass to the centre in the middle of the ice.
options.feedback: Yes — escape the pressure to the middle, start the breakout clean.

options.text: Pass to you on the wall anyway.
options.feedback: You'll get hit and the puck dies. Bad read.

options.text: Reverse to the other D.
options.feedback: Fine sometimes. But if the middle is open, use it.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. You're the weak-side winger. Where do you go?
U11 / Atom.why: The late-coming winger is the shooter on the rush — drive the far post for a tap-in.
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side winger drives the far post.

question.prompt: Click where the weak-side winger drives.

feedback.correct: Yes — far post for a back-door tap-in.
feedback.partial: Slot's OK, but the far post is gold on the 3-on-2.
feedback.wrong: Too high — you've taken yourself out of the play.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the low forward (F3) in the D-zone. Where's your spot?
U11 / Atom.why: F3 hangs in the high slot as an outlet and also covers the strong-side D at the point.
U11 / Atom.tip: F3 = high slot, eyes on strong-side point.

question.prompt: Click F3's starting spot.

feedback.correct: Yes — high slot. Support the D, cover the strong-side point.
feedback.partial: Close, but high-slot is the textbook spot.
feedback.wrong: Too deep or too wide. High slot is the anchor.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're F1 on the forecheck. Their D has the puck in the corner. How do you angle them?
U11 / Atom.why: F1 angles the D toward the boards — not the middle — so they can't escape to a clean play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angle them INTO the wall, not out of it.

question.prompt: How do you take F1's angle?

options.text: Angle from the middle out — force them into the wall.
options.feedback: Yes — pin the play. No escape.

options.text: Come at them from the wall toward the middle.
options.feedback: You just opened the ice. They'll skate past you.

options.text: Hit them as soon as you get there.
options.feedback: Sometimes. But angle first, hit second.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate has the puck below the goal line on your wing. You're the high forward. What's your read?
U11 / Atom.why: On a cycle, the high forward rotates low so the puck-carrier has a short support.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cycle = high F drops low, creates F1-F2 rotation.

question.prompt: Click where you rotate.

feedback.correct: Yes — drop low, give a short support. That's a cycle.
feedback.partial: Slot works as a screen, but the cycle needs a low support first.
feedback.wrong: Staying high breaks the cycle. Get low.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the lone D on a 2-on-1. What's the right gap?
U11 / Atom.why: Tight gap gives the shooter no time. Sag off and you take away the pass but give up the shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Take the pass. Make the goalie beat the shooter.

question.prompt: 2-on-1. Your job?

options.text: Stay in the middle. Take away the pass. Make the shooter beat the goalie.
options.feedback: Yes — goalie's job is the shot; yours is the pass.

options.text: Charge the puck-carrier.
options.feedback: You just made it a 1-on-0 for the open guy.

options.text: Drop to the ice to block.
options.feedback: Last-ditch. If you're there, the puck's already going in.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck crossing centre. The D is giving you the wide lane. What do you do?
U11 / Atom.why: If the D gives you wide, take it. Speed beats them outside and opens up a wrap-around or pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: Drive the lane they give you.

question.prompt: Click where you take the puck.

feedback.correct: Yes — drive wide, beat them with speed.
feedback.partial: Eventually — but first take the open lane with speed.
feedback.wrong: You're skating into the D. Take the space they're giving you.

U11 / Atom.sit: D-zone face-off on the left side. You're the LW. What's your first job?
U11 / Atom.why: On a D-zone draw, the LW takes the left point man — stop them from getting a one-timer.
U11 / Atom.tip: LW → left point. RW → right point.

question.prompt: Puck drops. Your first move is…

options.text: Jump to the left point to take their D.
options.feedback: Yes — winger's job is the point.

options.text: Battle for the puck in the circle.
options.feedback: That's the centre's job. You get the point.

options.text: Skate to the net to screen.
options.feedback: Only if the puck's out and shot. Your D-zone job is the point.

U11 / Atom.sit: You carried the puck to the red line. No one's open. What's the right read?
U11 / Atom.why: Regrouping — turning back, finding a trailing teammate — is better than dumping into bad pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: No entry? Regroup. Don't force it.

question.prompt: No entry open. You…

options.text: Turn back and pass to the D. Regroup.
options.feedback: Yes — live to play the next rush.

options.text: Dump-and-change even though nobody's chasing.
options.feedback: That's giving up possession for nothing.

options.text: Fire it at the D's skates.
options.feedback: If you HAVE to get it in, sure. But better to regroup.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're a D. The puck-carrier is driving wide. What do you lead with?
U11 / Atom.why: Stick-on-puck is how you take away plays without getting beat. Body second.
U11 / Atom.tip: Stick first. Body second.

question.prompt: Attacker driving wide. You lead with…

options.text: Stick on the puck, body close.
options.feedback: Yes — take the puck first; the body closes the door.

options.text: Shoulder charge them into the boards.
options.feedback: Miss and it's a breakaway. Stick first.

options.text: Skate straight at them.
options.feedback: You'll get beat. Angle + stick.

U11 / Atom.sit: D is winding up for a point shot. Where do you want to be as the net-front forward?
U11 / Atom.why: Net-front is for tips and screens — not for getting hit by the shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Stand to the side of the goalie — not in the shooting lane.

question.prompt: Click where you stand for tips.

feedback.correct: Yes — net-front, offset from the shooter's path. Tip and screen.
feedback.partial: Slot's fine for rebounds — net-front is better for screens.
feedback.wrong: That's not a tip spot.

U11 / Atom.sit: The play turned. You're the backchecking F. Which lane do you take?
U11 / Atom.why: First F back takes the middle lane — the most dangerous ice.
U11 / Atom.tip: First back = middle lane. Second back = wide lane.

question.prompt: You're first back. Which lane?

options.text: The middle lane — the most dangerous.
options.feedback: Yes. Kill the middle, force them wide.

options.text: Follow the puck-carrier.
options.feedback: That collapses the middle and opens up passes.

options.text: Head straight to net-front.
options.feedback: Only if a shot's already coming. Middle lane first.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. The puck comes to the half-wall. What's the first look?
U11 / Atom.why: Half-wall to net-front or to the weak-side point — those are PP goals.
U11 / Atom.tip: Half-wall reads = net-front or weak-side D.

question.prompt: First PP read from the half-wall?

options.text: Net-front for the tip, or weak-side D for a one-timer.
options.feedback: Yes — PP goals come from those two spots.

options.text: Pass back to the strong-side D immediately.
options.feedback: That resets the PP but gives up your look. Scan first.

options.text: Hold and cycle to the corner.
options.feedback: Fine if nothing's there. But scan the dangerous options first.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D just won a puck battle. A teammate is streaking through centre. What should the D do?
U11 / Atom.why: Quick-up passes catch the defense before they're set — highest-value transition play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck up fast beats any forecheck.

question.prompt: D won the puck. Best play?

options.text: Quick-up to the centre through the middle.
options.feedback: Yes — fast pass, catches them before they set.

options.text: Hold the puck and wait for support.
options.feedback: You give the forecheck time to get set. Bad read.

options.text: Dump it out around the boards.
options.feedback: Safe but slow. Quick-up is better when the lane's open.

U11 / Atom.sit: A defender just finished a check on your teammate in the corner. You're the nearest F. What do you do?
U11 / Atom.why: Finishing the hit is fine; running across the ice to retaliate takes you out of position and costs goals.
U11 / Atom.tip: Don't chase. Play the puck.

question.prompt: Teammate got hit. You…

options.text: Get to the puck or cover my check.
options.feedback: Yes — play hockey, not revenge.

options.text: Skate across to retaliate.
options.feedback: That's two minutes and a goal against.

options.text: Yell at the ref.
options.feedback: Refs hate that and the play keeps going.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're carrying the puck down the wing. The D is standing at the blue line. What's the read?
U11 / Atom.why: If the D stands still, drive through them. If they skate back with you, cut in.
U11 / Atom.tip: Static D → drive. Skating D → cut inside.

question.prompt: D is flat-footed at the blue line. You…

options.text: Drive through them with speed.
options.feedback: Yes — a flat-footed D is easy to beat wide.

options.text: Stop and wait for support.
options.feedback: You've given them time to recover.

options.text: Chip the puck in behind.
options.feedback: Works if a teammate is racing in. If you're alone, drive.

U11 / Atom.sit: Offensive zone draw. You're the weak-side winger. What's your job if your centre wins it back?
U11 / Atom.why: On offensive zone wins, the weak-side winger crashes the net for the screen/rebound.
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side W crashes net-front.

question.prompt: Your centre wins it. You skate to…

feedback.correct: Yes — net-front for screen and rebound.
feedback.partial: Slot's a good rebound spot but net-front is the primary job.
feedback.wrong: You've taken yourself out of the scoring chance.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're a D. Their forward is parked net-front. What's your job?
U11 / Atom.why: Net-front D: box them out with body, stick across their stick. Keep them from a clean shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Body between them and the puck side.

question.prompt: Net-front coverage is…

options.text: Body between them and the puck, stick on theirs.
options.feedback: Yes — take away the tip and the rebound.

options.text: Stand in front of the goalie.
options.feedback: That's a screen for your OWN goalie. Bad.

options.text: Just stand beside them.
options.feedback: Partial. Body contact and stick-on-stick is the full answer.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck is down low on the left boards. You're the right defenceman. Where should you be?
U11 / Atom.why: Weak-side D reads the play — usually slide to the middle to cover the slot and support the pinch.
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side D slides to middle-point.

question.prompt: Where does the weak-side D go?

feedback.correct: Yes — slide to the middle, cover the slot.
feedback.partial: You're still at the point. Slide a bit middle.
feedback.wrong: Too far from the play. Support the slot.

U11 / Atom.sit: A pass is coming to you on the wing. What should you do BEFORE it arrives?
U11 / Atom.why: Pre-scanning — looking over your shoulder before the puck arrives — is what separates U11 from rep hockey.
U11 / Atom.tip: Look BEFORE the puck, not after.

question.prompt: Pass coming. You…

options.text: Look over my shoulder to see who's open.
options.feedback: Yes — knowing what you'll do BEFORE the puck arrives is a pro habit.

options.text: Focus on the puck.
options.feedback: You'll catch it, but you won't know what to do next.

options.text: Tap the stick for a target.
options.feedback: Nice, but pre-scanning is more valuable.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier entering the offensive zone. One defender is set at the blue line right in front of you. What's the right decision?
opts: Force the carry through the defender instead of using your linemate or chip option.
Read the defender — if they're set, use your linemate or chip it behind them.
Dump it in rather than trying to force a risky play here before they score.
Dump it in since the other team has committed to the other side right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A set defender at the blue line is telling you something. Read them — if they're set and you try to carry, you risk a turnover in the worst spot. Use your linemate or chip the puck in.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender set at the line? Read them. Chip, use your linemate, or carry — but read first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers enter your zone. You're the center on defense. Your two wingers have taken the two opposing wingers. Who do you cover?
opts: Hold the puck.
The opposing center.
Carry it wide.
Skate hard and recover.
U13 / Peewee.why: In man-to-man defensive zone coverage, the center covers the opposing center. Covering your assignment — not the puck — is the foundation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Three attackers in your zone? As the center, you've got their center. Find them and cover them.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the center on a 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to slow the rush down and give your support time to get back.
Shoot low since that creates the best look and look for support.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Shoot low before the play develops further and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: When both defenders go wide to cover the wings, they've given you the middle. Drive it and shoot — the goalie is unscreened and the lane is open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. The other team has it and is breaking out. What should every forward do immediately?
opts: Shoot low a better chance.
Turn around and backcheck hard — immediately.
Shoot low since that creates the best look.
Shoot low while your teammates recover.
U13 / Peewee.why: The instant the puck changes hands, every forward transitions back. The sooner you go, the less damage the other team can do.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover? Turn around. Right now. Every forward.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate is about to shoot from the point. You're the nearest forward. Where should you be?
opts: Carry it wide since that creates the best look and look for support.
Crash the net — be there for the rebound before the shot arrives.
Hold the puck from your current spot while staying in position.
Hold the puck before the defense recovers and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Crashing the net before the shot arrives puts you in position for a rebound before the goalie can cover. Net-front presence on shots wins games.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Teammate shooting from the point? Crash the net. Get there before the puck does.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. One forechecker is closing from the left. Your right winger is up the wall on the right — open. Your left winger is covered. What's the breakout call?
opts: Drive to the net front rather than trying to force a risky play here instead.
Pass to the open right winger — the forechecker has committed to the left side.
Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
Challenge at the line a better chance while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: The forechecker committing to one side creates an opening on the other side. Read the forechecker and use the open outlet.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forechecker committed to one side? The outlet on that same side may be open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They have speed. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter.
Skate to the corner and dig it out since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Skate to the corner and dig it out before the play develops further while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Speed on a 2-on-1 is the attacker's advantage. Backing up fast enough while staying in the middle takes away the pass and forces the shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Fast 2-on-1? Back up fast. Stay in the middle. Force the shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier in the neutral zone at full speed. Two defenders are set at the blue line. Your linemate is trailing. Best entry?
opts: Shoot from wherever you are now since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Chip the puck past the inside defender and go get it, or use a give-and-go with your trailer.
Shoot from wherever you are now before the play develops further while staying in position.
Force the carry through the defender instead of using your linemate or chip option.
U13 / Peewee.why: Carrying against two set defenders at the blue line is low-percentage. The chip-and-chase or give-and-go with the trailer are the smart options.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two set defenders? Chip + go or use your trailer. Don't carry into a wall.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the right D at the point on the power play. A PK forward is closing hard on you. Your left D partner is wide open. What do you do?
opts: Charge through the forechecker and try to break the pressure yourself.
Make a quick D-to-D pass to your partner — they're open and you're not.
Chip it past them rather than trying to force a risky play.
Skate the puck back behind your own net and start over.
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure at the point, the D-to-D pass resets the play. Your partner has time and a better look.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pressure coming at the point? D-to-D. Let your partner reset.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is cycling below the dots in your zone. As the weak-side defenseman, when do you challenge the puck carrier?
opts: Chip it past them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
When a player with the puck is in a dangerous spot — near the slot or about to pass to the slot.
Change lines because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Back up and defend because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: Defending the cycle means picking your moments. You don't challenge every pass — protect your position and challenge when the puck is in a truly dangerous spot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defending the cycle: stay in position. Challenge when dangerous, not just moving.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The lone defender is backing up in the middle, not committing to either side. What do you do?
opts: Back up and defend while staying in position and look for support.
Drive wide to force the defender to fully commit first, then decide.
Drive to the net front since that creates the best look from this spot.
Back up and defend and then look for support and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: If the defender isn't committing, you haven't created the advantage yet. Drive wide to force their decision.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender not committing? Make them. Drive wide first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're on a breakaway. The goalie is skating aggressively out to cut your angle. What's the most effective play?
opts: Pass it to your D at the far point before the play develops further and look for support.
Pull the puck wide, make the goalie commit to one side, then shoot or go to the other side.
Challenge at the line because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
U13 / Peewee.why: An aggressive goalie is looking for an early shot or for you to go straight. Make them commit — take the puck wide, let them follow, then shoot or go opposite.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Aggressive goalie on a breakaway: make them commit first. Then go where they're not.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. Both wingers are covered. Your center breaks free through the neutral zone — 40 feet away but completely open. What's the call?
opts: Hit the near winger with a short pass — safer than threading the needle.
Thread the stretch pass to the center — they're breaking and open.
Chip it off the boards to center ice and race for it yourself.
Hold the puck and wait for a trailer to come back for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stretch pass to a breaking center turns a defensive zone puck into an immediate rush chance. The reward — an odd-man rush — is worth the risk when the lane is clean.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Breaking center open on the stretch? Thread it. Risky but the reward is a rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1. The puck is at the top. A penalty killer closes hard on the top player. What should the top player do?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Move the puck quickly before the pressure arrives — pass to the half-wall or the other side.
Pass it to your D at the far point and then get back into your defensive position.
Swing wide along the boards since the other team has committed to the other side at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: On the power play, moving the puck before pressure arrives is the key. One extra skater means someone is always open — find them before the PK player gets there.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP with pressure coming? Move the puck early. Someone is open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Force a play and risk losing the puck to maintain possession.
Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
Dump it in since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
Thread a pass to the slot rather than trying to force a risky play here and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots burns clock, forces the other team to chase, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late: the clock is your ally. Protect the puck. Make them chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Swing wide along the boards rather than trying to force a risky play.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
Shoot from wherever you are now and wait for the play to reset and look for support.
Swing wide along the boards and then get back into your defensive position.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PP counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures, everyone else recovers. Panicking and all chasing creates a turnover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't panic.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is on the power play. A forward is screening your goalie right in front of the crease. The puck is at the point. What's your job as the nearest PK player?
opts: Challenge at the line before the defense recovers right away.
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie needs to see.
Challenge at the line a better chance.
Pressure the carrier hard while staying in position at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: A screener blocking your goalie's sightline is the most dangerous player on the ice. Moving them out is the priority — before the shot is taken.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Screener in the crease? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shot comes from the blue line right at the middle of the net. You're standing in front of the goalie. What's your job?
opts: Get out of the way so the goalie can see the shot clearly.
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's tracking.
Screen the goalie with your back but don't touch the puck.
Move off to the side and look for a rebound instead.
U13 / Peewee.why: Tipping a shot on net is one of the most effective plays in hockey. Even a small deflection changes the trajectory enough to beat a goalie who is tracking the original shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot coming from the point right at the goalie? Tip it. Any deflection beats their tracking.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the point on the power play. You have the puck and a shooting lane — but two PK forwards are closing on you hard. You have one second. What's the best play?
opts: Pressure the carrier hard because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice from this spot.
Fake the shot to freeze them, then pass to the half-wall player who opened up when the PKers committed to you.
Drop back and play defense because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Get your stick in the lane because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: When two PK players commit to you, they've left someone open. A shot fake freezes them for a split second — the half-wall opens up the instant they both lean at you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two PKers closing? Fake the shot. Someone opened up when they committed.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the D at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Chip it past them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready to pursue if the puck turns over.
Stay high at the blue line — don't venture into the offensive zone.
Take the outside lane since forcing it would be risky here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Center positioning for a counter? Cheat back slightly. Ready for the turnover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone trap. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Pull the puck back to your D, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
Try to stickhandle through them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice before they score.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement — go backward to go forward.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Two attackers entered your zone and both D picked them up. A third attacker trails in 5 seconds later — nobody picked them up. You're the center on defense. What do you do?
opts: Pass back because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Pick up the late attacker — your winger on that side needs to find their assignment.
Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Skate hard since the other team has committed to the other side before they score.
U13 / Peewee.why: A late attacker entering the zone creates a coverage breakdown. The center needs to pick them up or communicate a switch immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Late attacker uncovered? Call it and pick them up. Coverage gaps get exploited.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The defender commits fully to your teammate. The goalie slides across expecting your pass. What's the right call now?
opts: Drop back and play defense while staying in position first.
Shoot — the goalie has moved and there's a lane on your side.
Pull up at the hash marks along the boards and look for support.
Drop back and play defense while staying in position again.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the defender commits to your teammate AND the goalie slides to take away the pass, they've left your side open. Shoot — the goalie has moved.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1: if the goalie chases the pass, shoot. Read the goalie.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but barely any time. What do you do?
opts: Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway.
Skate to the corner and dig it out a better chance on the play.
Skate to the corner and dig it out while your teammates recover on the play.
U13 / Peewee.why: In tight to the net with time running out, a quick backhand release is often better than taking the time to switch to your forehand. The goalie is already set for the forehand.
U13 / Peewee.tip: In tight with time running out? Shoot what you've got. Don't switch hands.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team breaks out. As you cross the red line you see both opposing defensemen are pinching up — they're at the red line, not their blue line. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
Skate hard behind them — you have a breakaway if you beat them to their blue line.
Pull up at the hash marks since the other team has committed to the other side first.
Change lines rather than trying to force a risky play here and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Defensemen pinching up means they've given up the space behind them. If you can beat them to the puck, you have a breakaway or a clean zone entry.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D pinching up? Burn them. Get behind them before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling and the slot is clogged. All four opponents are below the dots. What's the play?
opts: Take the outside lane and try to drive the net yourself.
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested.
Force a centering pass through all four defenders to a teammate in the slot.
Skate into the slot yourself to draw coverage away from your teammates.
U13 / Peewee.why: When all four defenders collapse below the dots, the point is wide open. A point shot from there is uncontested and generates traffic and rebounds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Four D collapsed below the dots? The point is open. Give it up. Free shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play with only 10 seconds left. You're in the offensive zone at the half-wall. No clear shot lane. What's the play?
opts: Challenge at the line since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than nothing with 10 seconds left.
Challenge at the line before the play develops further while staying in position.
Challenge at the line and then get back into your defensive position on the play.
U13 / Peewee.why: With 10 seconds left on a PP, the time to set up is gone. Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than the power play expiring with no attempt.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 10 seconds left on the PP? Shoot from anywhere. Time's up.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You're the center taking a defensive zone faceoff. The power play wants to win the draw back to the point for a shot setup. What's your goal on this draw?
opts: Drive to the net front and then get back into your defensive position.
Win it forward — kick it toward the corner or boards to prevent the PP shot setup.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Drive to the net front since the other team has committed to the other side.
U13 / Peewee.why: On a PK defensive zone faceoff, winning it forward — toward the boards or corner — takes away the PP's plan. Winning backward gives the PP exactly what they want.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK faceoff in your own zone: win it forward. Take away the point shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
opts: Hold the puck and then get back into your defensive position at that point.
Backing up at the right controlled pace, not overcommitting.
Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
Hold the puck since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right controlled pace is the correct response.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush coming with speed? Back up controlled. Never pinch up against a fast rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You win a puck battle in the neutral zone. What's the right thing to do with the puck?
opts: Cut to the middle of the ice because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Control the puck along the boards, protect it, and force the other team to come get it — use every second.
Set up behind the net and wait and then get back into your defensive position while staying in position.
Shoot from wherever you are now because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: Game management late means using the clock. Protecting the puck in the neutral zone makes the other team come to you and burns precious seconds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late and won the puck? Protect it. Use the clock. Make them come to you.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
opts: Skate to the corner and dig it out from the point and look for support.
Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
Skate to the corner and dig it out before they react before they score.
Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase. Holding position and reading the breakout gives you a chance to pick off the first pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: They won the corner battle? High forward reads the breakout. Don't chase — intercept.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1. The half-wall player has the puck. The near PK forward is cheating toward the half-wall. The seam pass to the middle player is now open. What's the right play?
opts: Change lines because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Thread the seam pass to the middle player — the PK forward cheating toward you opened that lane.
Back up and defend because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Pull up at the hash marks because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice first.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the PK forward cheats toward you, they've vacated the seam. The middle player is open. The seam pass is the highest-danger play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK forward cheated toward you? The seam is open. Thread it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're protecting the puck behind the net. A defender has you pinned. Two teammates are in the zone but both are covered. What do you do?
opts: Throw the puck at the net and hope for a lucky bounce off the goalie.
Wheel to the other side of the net — moving forces the defender to move and may open a teammate.
Hold the puck against the boards until a teammate gets open.
Pass back to the D at the point for a shot attempt.
U13 / Peewee.why: Wheeling behind the net when both outlets are covered forces the defender to move with you, which can open a teammate, and resets the pressure.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both outlets covered behind the net? Wheel. Force the defense to move.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team just changed lines mid-play in the neutral zone. Their new players are still getting up to speed. You have the puck. What does this create?
opts: Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
A transition window — attack immediately while their fresh players aren't at full speed or in position.
Force a play through traffic rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
Force a play through traffic because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: A line change mid-play creates a brief window where the fresh players aren't at full speed or position. Attacking immediately exploits that window before it closes.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Other team just changed mid-play? Attack now. Their fresh players aren't set yet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're skating at full speed toward the net. A defender is chasing from behind. Should you slow down to take a more accurate shot?
opts: Yes — slow down and take a more accurate wrist shot.
No — shoot while moving at full speed, making it harder for the goalie.
Yes — stop completely and shoot from a still position.
Yes — slow down, cut across the crease, then shoot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Shooting while moving at full speed is harder for a goalie to track. Slowing down also lets the defender catch up. Shoot while moving.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Full speed toward the net with a defender behind you? Shoot moving. Don't slow down.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. Do you watch the puck or watch your man?
opts: Skate hard because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit first.
Watch your man — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Crash the net for a rebound since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
U13 / Peewee.why: In man coverage, losing sight of your assignment is how they get open for a pass. Keep them in front of you and use your peripheral vision for the puck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Man coverage in your own zone: watch your man, not the puck. Use your peripheral vision for the puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're forechecking. The D picks up the puck behind the net and starts to walk out to the left. You're closing from the right. Your linemate is on the left. How do you split?
opts: Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
You force the D's direction from the right, your linemate cuts off the outlet to the left — trap them between you.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover again.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
U13 / Peewee.why: A two-player forecheck trap works by forcing the puck carrier in a direction while the other player cuts off the outlet in that direction.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two-man forecheck: one forces, one cuts the outlet. Trap them.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Get your stick in the lane since forcing it would be risky here and look for support.
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
Force a play and risk losing the puck to maintain possession.
Get your stick in the lane because your teammates need time to set up from this spot.
U13 / Peewee.why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.
U13 / Peewee.tip: OK angle vs. slightly covered slot? The slot wins. Higher danger is worth the risk.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your penalty kill is in a box. The power play's center has dropped below the dots to create a 1-3-1. Your box is now mismatched against their formation. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Adjust — one PK forward drops to pressure the center who dropped in. The box has to mirror the threat.
Get your stick in the lane since the other team has committed to the other side and look for support.
Pull up at the hash marks because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-3-1 power play vs. a box requires a PK adjustment. One forward drops to pressure the center who's now below the dots — leaving them open in that area gives up the most dangerous shots in hockey.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP dropped a player below the dots? One PK forward drops to match. Adjust to the threat.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
opts: Hold your position at the crease because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice before they score.
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
Skate hard since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover from this spot.
U13 / Peewee.why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Goalie made the save under pressure? Find the breaking player. Read the breakout.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
opts: Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck.
Hold your position at the crease since the other team has committed to the other side and look for support.
Skate to the corner and dig it out rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Beating the opposing player to the rebound — or at minimum getting inside position — prevents the second shot. Letting them get a clean rebound in the corner means a second chance against you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rebound in the corner? Race to it or get inside position. Prevent the second shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck for what you think will be a better shot. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Pass back because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice early.
Skate hard rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1. You've taken away the pass. The shooter pulls up and shoots. The goalie makes the save but the rebound comes right to the second attacker. What do you do?
opts: Set up behind the net and wait because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Get your body in front of the second attacker — between them and the net — before they can get the shot off.
Set up behind the net and wait because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
U13 / Peewee.why: When a rebound goes to the second attacker, your job shifts from pass prevention to body blocking. Getting your body between them and the net before they shoot is the only play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rebound goes to the second attacker? Get your body between them and the net. Block the shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: It's a tied game in the third period. You have the puck in the neutral zone. Your team's fastest forward is breaking on your left, completely open. A defended pass is available on your right. What do you do?
opts: Pressure the carrier hard rather than trying to force something through the middle of the ice when the lane is not clean and the defenders are set.
Thread the pass to the breaking forward on the left — a tied game in the third is the time to take the calculated risk for a high-danger chance.
Swing wide and dump the puck in — a tied third period is no time to take risks.
Hold the puck and kill the clock until your line can change.
U13 / Peewee.why: A tied game in the third period is when high-danger chances matter most. An open breaking forward is a calculated risk worth taking — the reward is a scoring chance, the risk is a missed pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Tied late with a breaking forward open? Thread it. High-danger chances matter most now.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your goalie makes a save and controls the puck. Put the breakout steps in order.
items: D sets up behind the net and calls for the puck
Wingers sprint to their breakout lanes along the boards
Center supports low then curls through the middle
D reads pressure and hits the first open option
U13 / Peewee.why: A structured breakout has defined roles. D retrieves, wings get to their lanes, center fills the middle, and the D reads and passes. The system only works when everyone hits their spots.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Structured breakout: D retrieves, wings to lanes, center supports, D reads. Hit your spots.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play and needs to set up in the offensive zone. Put the PP entry steps in order.
items: Win the draw or gain clean possession in the neutral zone
Puck carrier enters with speed and reads the PK's setup
Supporting players stretch the PK by driving wide and high
Puck moves to the strong side and the PP gets into formation
U13 / Peewee.why: A clean PP entry is the first step to a successful power play. Possession first, enter with speed and a read, stretch the PK, then set up your formation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP entry: possess, enter with speed, stretch the PK, get into formation. Clean entries = more PP time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a defensive zone faceoff with the other team's top line on the ice. Put the defensive steps in order.
items: Everyone knows their assignment before the puck drops
Get to your assigned position around the faceoff circle
Center competes on the draw — win it back or tie up
If you lose the draw, immediately get to your coverage assignment
U13 / Peewee.why: D-zone faceoffs are structured moments. Knowing your job, getting positioned, competing on the draw, and executing coverage immediately are the steps. Teams that wing it on faceoffs give up goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: D-zone faceoff: know your job, get set, compete, execute coverage. No freelancing.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team dumps the puck into the corner to start a cycle. Put the cycle steps in order.
items: First forward wins the puck battle on the boards
Protect the puck and scan for support options
Second forward comes in low for the short pass or picks up a loose puck
Third forward stays high and the cycle rotation begins
U13 / Peewee.why: The cycle is a system: win the board battle, protect, get support, and rotate. If the first battle is lost, the cycle never starts. If the third forward goes low, you lose your high option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle: win the battle, protect, support, rotate. Lose the first battle and nothing starts.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A skilled forward is carrying the puck through the neutral zone at full speed, 1-on-1 against you. Put your defensive steps in order.
items: Pivot and match their speed while establishing your gap
Read their body language — hips, head, stick position
Close the gap at the blue line and angle them to the outside
Use an active stick to take away their preferred move and force them wide
U13 / Peewee.why: 1-on-1 defending is a sequence of reads and actions. Match speed, read their intent, close at the right time, and take away their best option. Skip the read step and you'll commit too early.
