U13 / Peewee.tip: 1-on-1: match speed, read them, close at the line, steer wide. It's a sequence, not a gamble.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has the puck but can't enter the offensive zone — the D are too tight. Your center calls for a regroup. Put the steps in order.
items: Puck carrier turns back and passes to the trailing defenseman
Forwards loop through the neutral zone and rebuild speed
D-to-D pass stretches the PK or defensive setup horizontally
Forwards attack with speed on the second entry attempt
U13 / Peewee.why: A regroup is a tactical reset, not a failure. Bring it back, rebuild speed, stretch the defense, and attack again with better timing. Forcing a bad entry is worse than resetting.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Can't get in clean? Regroup. Bring it back, rebuild speed, attack again. Smart, not weak.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're set up in front of the net on the power play. A shot is coming from the point. Put your steps in order.
items: Establish position in front of the goalie — between the dots
Screen the goalie by getting big without interfering
Read the shot — is it a tip opportunity or a rebound play?
React — tip on net, collect the rebound, or jam at a loose puck
U13 / Peewee.why: Net-front presence is a skill with steps. Position, screen, read, react. Players who just stand there without reading the shot miss tip and rebound chances.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Net front: position, screen, read, react. Standing there isn't enough — you need to read the play.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 3 minutes left. The other team is pressing. Put the puck management steps in order.
items: Make safe, simple passes — no high-risk plays in your own zone
Use the boards and the glass to clear when under pressure
Change lines quickly to keep fresh legs
Force them to earn zone entries — don't give up easy possession
U13 / Peewee.why: Protecting a late lead is about discipline. Safe passes, smart clears, quick changes, and denying easy zone time. One high-risk turnover can cost you the game.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Protecting a lead: simple plays, clear when pressured, fresh legs, deny entries. Discipline wins.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. The puck is loose along the boards near the blue line. You pinch down to get it, but your D partner has also jumped into the play low. The opponent chips it past both of you for a 2-on-0. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have pinched harder.
Both D went — one defenseman must always stay back to cover. If your partner is low, you hold the line. If you're pinching, they hold. Never both.
The forwards should have helped.
The puck bounced wrong.
U13 / Peewee.why: Two defensemen caught deep is a catastrophic defensive breakdown. One D always stays high as the safety valve. Communication between the D pair prevents this.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D can't go. Ever. One pinches, one stays. Communicate with your partner.

U13 / Peewee.sit: In the defensive zone, the puck is behind your net. Both your defensemen go behind the net to battle for it. The opposing center sneaks to the front of the net completely uncovered. They score on a pass out front. What's the mistake?
opts: The goalie should have stopped it.
Both D went behind the net — one D battles for the puck, the other covers the front of the net. Leaving the front of the net empty is one of the most dangerous mistakes in hockey.
The forwards should have been there.
The play happened.
U13 / Peewee.why: The front of the net must always be covered. When both D go behind the net, the most dangerous area on the ice is left wide open. One battles, one protects.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D behind the net? Someone better be in front. The front of the net is never optional.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. The puck carrier slows down to make a play at the blue line. Two forwards are flying into the zone at full speed and cross the blue line before the puck. Offside. Rush killed. What's the mistake?
opts: The puck carrier was too slow.
The two forwards didn't read the puck carrier and entered the zone early — on a rush, everyone times their entry off the puck. If the carrier slows down, you slow down.
The ref made a bad call.
The D played it well.
U13 / Peewee.why: Offside on a rush with numbers is one of the most frustrating mistakes in hockey. The forwards must read the puck carrier's speed and time their entry. The puck always crosses first.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush entry: read the carrier's speed. Puck crosses first. Always. Kill your speed if you have to.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the penalty kill, your forward leaves the box to chase the puck behind the net. The power play moves it quickly to the open slot area where your forward was supposed to be. Goal. What's the mistake?
opts: The forward should have chased faster.
The forward left their position in the PK box — on the penalty kill, you stay in structure and cover your zone. Chasing the puck out of position opens the most dangerous area on the ice.
The D should have shifted.
The goalie wasn't ready.
U13 / Peewee.why: PK discipline means staying in the box. When a player chases out of position, the slot opens up. The power play is designed to exploit exactly that mistake.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK: stay in the box. Chase out of position and the slot opens. That's where PP goals come from.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your linemate makes a beautiful cross-ice pass to you in the slot. You catch it cleanly and have a half-second to shoot. Instead, you look down to admire the pass and adjust the puck. The goalie sets and you shoot into their chest. What's the mistake?
opts: The pass was too hard.
You hesitated — in the slot with a half-second window, you shoot on the catch. Looking down and adjusting gave the goalie time to get set. Quick release beats perfect setup.
The goalie was lucky.
You should have passed it back.
U13 / Peewee.why: Slot chances are about speed of release, not perfection of setup. A half-second of puck adjustment gives the goalie time to square up. Shoot on the catch and beat them before they're ready.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Slot chance? Shoot on the catch. Don't look down. Don't adjust. Release beats perfection.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck behind the net. The forechecker is closing hard from the left side. Instead of going right or using the boards, your D skates directly into the forechecker. Turnover. What's the mistake?
opts: The D wasn't strong enough.
The D skated into pressure instead of away from it — when a forechecker closes from one side, go the other way or use the boards. Skating into pressure is a guaranteed turnover.
The forechecker was.
The forwards didn't help.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading the forecheck and going away from pressure is breakout 101. Skating into the forechecker gives them exactly what they want — a turnover in a dangerous area.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forechecker from the left? Go right. Away from pressure. Always away. Never into it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone. Your defenseman keeps backing up all the way to the top of the crease, giving the attacker the entire offensive zone to work with. What's the mistake?
opts: The D should have been more aggressive.
The D gave up too much ice — backing up to the crease gives the attacker time, space, and options. Close the gap at the blue line and take away their time to make plays.
The forwards should have backchecked.
The attacker was too skilled.
U13 / Peewee.why: A defender who backs up to the crease surrenders the entire offensive zone. Gap control means closing the distance at the blue line — taking away the attacker's time and space before they can set up.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Don't back up to your crease. Close the gap at the blue line. Take away their time and space.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team turns the puck over in the offensive zone. Two forwards are exhausted and glide slowly to the bench for a change. The other team rushes 4-on-3 and scores before the fresh players get into position. What's the mistake?
opts: The coach should have called for a change earlier.
The tired forwards changed too slowly at the wrong time — after a turnover, you sprint back first, then change when the puck is safe. Changing during transition is how you give up odd-man goals.
The defensemen should have held.
The other team got lucky.
U13 / Peewee.why: Line changes during defensive transition are deadly. Sprint back first, stabilize the defense, then change when the puck is in a safe area. Gliding to the bench during a rush against is a gift to the other team.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover? Sprint back first. Change when it's safe. Never change during a rush against.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling on the strong side. The defense has matched your cycle perfectly — nothing is opening up. Your D at the point makes a D-to-D pass to the weak side. What happens next?
opts: The cycle continues on the same side.
The weak-side D has an open shooting lane or passing option — the defense was overloaded to the strong side and the D-to-D pass shifts the play to open ice.
Nothing changes.
Pass back out immediately instead of maintaining cycle.
U13 / Peewee.why: A D-to-D pass against an overloaded defense opens the weak side completely. The defense was committed to the cycle side and can't recover across the ice in time.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle stuck? D-to-D to the weak side. The defense overloaded to the strong side can't get there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. Both defenders focus on the three attackers. Your fourth player — the trailing D — jumps into the play late from the blue line, completely unnoticed. The puck carrier sees them. What happens next?
opts: The trailing D stays back.
The puck carrier drops it back to the trailing D who has a clean shot from the top of the circles — the defense never accounted for the trailer.
The puck carrier shoots alone.
Everyone regroups.
U13 / Peewee.why: The trailing player on a rush is one of the most dangerous weapons in hockey. The defense accounts for the three rushers but forgets the trailer. A drop pass to an uncontested shooter at the top of the circles is a prime chance.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Trailer on the rush? Drop it back. The D has a clean look that nobody's covering.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your center strips the puck from the opposing center in your defensive zone slot area. The other team's forwards are all deep in your zone. Your wingers are at the hash marks. What happens next?
opts: Your center dumps it out.
Your center headmans it to a winger breaking up ice — the other team is caught deep, and a quick pass to a winger with speed creates an odd-man rush the other way.
Everyone regroups in the D-zone.
Your center holds the puck and waits.
U13 / Peewee.why: A turnover in the defensive zone with the other team caught deep is a counter-attack opportunity. The quick headman pass to a winger with speed can create a 2-on-1 or breakaway before the other team recovers.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Strip the puck in your zone with them caught deep? Headman it fast. Counter-attack before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the penalty kill, your team clears the puck to center ice. The power play's defenseman retrieves it, but your two PK forwards are already forechecking hard at the blue line. What happens next?
opts: The PP sets up again easily.
Your PK forwards pressure the D and force a turnover or a bad re-entry — aggressive PK forechecking at the blue line disrupts the PP's rhythm.
Your PK falls back to the box.
The PK changes lines.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PK clear followed by immediate pressure on the retrieving D is elite penalty killing. You've broken their setup and now you're making them earn the zone entry all over again — under pressure this time.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK clears it? Don't fall back. Pressure the retrieval. Make them earn the entry again.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team loses the defensive zone faceoff clean — the puck goes back to their point man who one-times it. Your goalie makes the save but can't control the rebound. It's loose in front. What happens next?
opts: Everyone watches the goalie.
Your forwards and D collapse to the net, box out opposing players, and either clear the puck or help the goalie freeze it — loose pucks in front after a faceoff loss require an all-hands response.
The D pass it around the boards.
Your goalie comes out to play it.
U13 / Peewee.why: A loose puck in the crease after a faceoff loss is maximum danger. Everyone collapses to the net — box out, clear, freeze. Half a second of hesitation is a goal against.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Loose puck in front after a lost draw? Collapse. Box out. Clear or freeze. Everyone. Now.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You drive to the net and the goalie goes down in butterfly early. You still have the puck on your stick and you're in tight. The entire top of the net is open. What happens next?
opts: You shoot low into the pads.
You lift the puck high over the butterfly — goalie is down, the top of the net is wide open, and you have the puck and time to elevate it.
You pass it across.
You deke to the backhand.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie in butterfly early leaves the top of the net open. If you have time and the puck, lifting it over the pads into the open net is the highest-percentage play. Read the goalie, not just the net.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Goalie down in butterfly early? Go high. Lift it. The top of the net is yours.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending a 1-on-1. The forward drives wide, then suddenly stops up at the top of the circle and cuts back to the middle. You were committed to the outside angle. What happens next?
opts: You keep going to the outside.
You pivot hard and recover to the inside — the forward just beat your angle, and if you don't recover inside, they'll have the slot. Pivot feet, close the inside lane.
You stop and watch.
You go for a poke check from behind.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stop-and-cut-back to the middle is designed to beat a defender committed outside. The recovery is a hard pivot to close the inside lane before they reach the slot. Feet must move — reaching from behind creates penalties.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forward cuts back inside? Pivot and recover inside. Don't reach from behind. Move your feet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On a breakout, the safest option is always to go up the boards.
U13 / Peewee.why: The boards are often safe, but the best play depends on where the pressure is. Sometimes the middle is open. Sometimes a reverse behind the net opens the other side. Always use the boards and you become predictable. Read the forecheck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Boards are often safe, but not always best. Read the forecheck and use what's open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the power play, moving the puck quickly to find the open lane is more effective than holding onto it and trying to beat players one-on-one.
U13 / Peewee.why: Power play efficiency comes from puck movement that forces the PK to shift. One player holding and deking lets the PK stay compact. Quick passes open seams the PK can't cover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP: move the puck. Quick passes open seams. Holding it lets the PK stay compact.

U13 / Peewee.sit: When you're under pressure in the defensive zone, you should always freeze the puck to stop play.
U13 / Peewee.why: Freezing gives the other team a faceoff in your zone — a set play with their best players. If you can make a quick play to a teammate or clear it, that's better than giving them a structured offensive opportunity.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Freezing in your zone = giving them a set play. Clear or pass if you can. Freeze only when you must.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Taking a lot of shots from bad angles and low-danger areas is just as effective as fewer shots from high-danger areas.
U13 / Peewee.why: Shot quality matters more than shot quantity. A shot from the slot is worth far more than five shots from the corner. Getting to high-danger areas and shooting from there produces more goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot quality over shot quantity. One slot shot beats five corner shots. Get to dangerous areas.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Using an active stick to poke the puck away is often more effective than going for a big body check.
U13 / Peewee.why: An active stick disrupts plays without taking you out of position. A missed body check puts you behind the play. Good defenders use their stick first and their body when the timing is right.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick first, body second. Active stick keeps you in position. Missed hits put you behind the play.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Turnovers kill more than missed shots
U13 / Peewee.sit: Turnovers in the neutral or defensive zone are more costly to your team than missed shots in the offensive zone.
U13 / Peewee.why: A missed shot is a lost possession in the offensive zone — the other team still has to exit and come 200 feet. A neutral or defensive zone turnover gives them the puck in a dangerous area with less ice to cover. Location of turnovers matters.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Missed shot in their zone? Acceptable. Turnover in yours? Deadly. Location of turnovers matters.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Best time to attack is after a turnover
U13 / Peewee.sit: The best time to create a scoring chance is immediately after your team forces a turnover, when the other team is out of position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Transition offense works because the other team is still in their offensive mindset and out of defensive position. The fastest teams in transition create the most odd-man rushes and high-danger chances.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover = attack window. They're out of position. Go now, before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is rimming around the boards and will end up behind your net. Two opposing forwards are forechecking hard. What do you do?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Get to the puck first and either rim it to your D's forehand on the weak side, or freeze it if your D can't get there.
Protect the puck there since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
Stay in the neutral zone since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie who plays the puck well under forecheck pressure is a huge weapon. Beat the forecheckers to the puck and either set up your D on the open side or freeze it to relieve pressure. Passive goalies let forechecks succeed.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck coming behind the net with pressure? Beat them to it. Set up your D or freeze.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A point shot is coming and there's an opposing forward in front of you with their stick on the ice, ready for a tip. What should you prepare for?
opts: Try to stickhandle through them because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit right away.
The deflection — assume the puck will change direction. Stay in your stance, keep your stick on the ice, and read the deflection angle in real time.
Pressure the carrier hard rather than trying to force something through the middle of the ice when the lane is not clean and the defenders are set.
Drop back and play defense because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stick on the ice in front of you is a deflection setup. You have to assume the puck gets tipped. Stay low, keep your stick on the ice, and track the puck through the deflection — it usually changes angle and elevation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick on the ice in front? Expect a tip. Stay low, track through the deflection.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're making a save from a shot on the right side. You can control where the rebound goes with your pads or blocker. What's the best target for the rebound?
opts: Pass back since the lane is open now while staying in position.
Into the left corner — away from the shooter and out of the slot.
Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Pass back and then get back into your defensive position first.
U13 / Peewee.why: Rebound direction is an active skill. Angling your pad to redirect the rebound to the far corner takes it out of the most dangerous area. A rebound in the slot is a goal waiting to happen.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Control your rebounds. Far corner. Away from the slot. Always.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shooter is coming down the right wing. You know from previous shifts they always shoot short side, top corner on the rush. What's your approach?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Cheat slightly to take away their preferred shot — respect their tendency by giving a bit more of the opposite corner.
Force a play through traffic since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
Crash the net for a rebound because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Elite goalies track shooter tendencies within a game. If a player has shown you their preferred spot multiple times, slightly cheating to take it away forces them to beat you somewhere else — often a spot they're less comfortable with.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Know a shooter's tendency? Respect it. Cheat slightly to take away their spot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You see an opposing player sneaking to the backdoor completely uncovered by your D. Your D are both focused on the corner battle. The pass hasn't come yet. What do you do?
opts: Take the outside lane because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Yell loudly — 'Back door!' or 'Net front!' — to warn your D to get a stick on the open player.
Hold the puck because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice right away.
Carry it wide because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: The goalie has the best view of developing threats. A loud, specific call — 'Back door!' — can get a defender's stick into a passing lane before the pass even happens. Silent goalies watch goals happen.
U13 / Peewee.tip: See a coverage problem? Call it. Loud. Specific. 'Back door!' stops goals.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is shot around the boards behind your net. A forechecker is closing from your left. Your D is coming to the left side. Your other D is wide open on the right side. What's the best play?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Stop it, then make a short pass to your right D who is wide open on the weak side — a clean D-to-D breakout under pressure.
Take the outside lane since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover on the play.
Crash the net for a rebound since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie who can play a D-to-D pass under pressure is a major asset. Identifying the uncovered defender and getting them the puck clean starts the breakout before the forecheck sets up. This is an advanced skill worth developing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Weak-side D wide open? Play it to them. D-to-D breaks forechecks.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate has the puck in the left corner. You're the weak-side forward. Where do you drive?
U13 / Peewee.why: The backdoor of the slot is the most-open piece of ice when the puck is low in the far corner. Goalie has to track the puck — you arrive into nothing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck low-far-side? Drive the backdoor.

question.prompt: Where do you drive as the weak-side winger?

feedback.correct: Backdoor. Puck in the far corner means the slot and net-front are wide open — that's where the seam pass goes.
feedback.partial: Near the right dot is in the area but you want to arrive at the slot as a scoring threat, not just the faceoff dot.
feedback.wrong: That's not the scoring lane here. When the puck's low-far-side, the backdoor is the read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D winds up for a point shot. An opponent is tight on you at the net-front. What do you do?
U13 / Peewee.why: The net-front job on a point shot is non-negotiable: screen the goalie, stay for the rebound, tip anything in reach. Drifting off the net erases all three.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Point shot? You stay at the net. Period.

question.prompt: Your D is about to shoot. What do you do at the net-front?

options.text: Hold your screen and box out for the rebound
options.feedback: That's the whole job. Block the goalie's eyes, stay strong on your stick, hunt the rebound.

options.text: Tip it if it comes in waist-high, otherwise screen
options.feedback: Same idea — tips and screens are both net-front work. Perfect.

options.text: Swing out to give the shooter a clearer lane
options.feedback: Clearer lane is worthless if there's no screen and no rebound threat. Stay home.

options.text: Peel to the corner for a missed shot
options.feedback: You just abandoned the best scoring area on the ice. Missed shots go wide — rebounds stay near the net.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck on the left-side boards. A forechecker is on you hard. Your D is at the point, center is in the slot but covered.
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure on the wall, the reverse is the highest-success play: it uses your body as a shield, beats the F1 who has all his momentum going forward, and resets possession for your D.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pressure on the wall? Reverse it and spin.

question.prompt: F1 is all over you on the wall. What do you do?

options.text: Reverse the puck low off the boards and spin back
options.feedback: Reverse beats the F1's momentum and gets the puck back to your D. Cleanest wall escape in hockey.

options.text: Blind pass up to the point
options.feedback: Blind pass into traffic is a blocked shot or a turnover at the blue line. Don't feed the rush.

options.text: Throw it off the glass and chase
options.feedback: Glass is a safe escape but you're giving up possession. Reverse keeps it.

options.text: Try to power through the forechecker
options.feedback: Power moves against an F1 with momentum = you get stripped. Use his momentum against him.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck behind the net. Your weak-side winger is open in the slot with a defender just stepping toward them.
U13 / Peewee.why: A seam pass from behind the net catches the goalie moving and the D recovering. It's the most dangerous pass in hockey — but only if the receiver is open right now.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Behind the net + open seam? Don't wait.

question.prompt: RW is open in the slot. What's the play?

options.text: Seam pass to the slot immediately
options.feedback: Money. The pass beats the goalie's track across the crease. High-danger chance created.

options.text: Hold it and wait for a better option
options.feedback: Waiting closes the seam. This pass had to go when you had it.

options.text: Wrap it around to the other corner
options.feedback: Keeps possession but passes up the best play on the board.

options.text: Stuff it wraparound style
options.feedback: Low-percentage solo play when a teammate was wide open for a cleaner chance.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has been cycling low. The D just got the puck at the point. You're the low forward — where do you go when the shot is coming?
U13 / Peewee.why: When a cycle releases to the point, low forwards should collapse to the net and slot for tips and rebounds. Standing still in the cycle spot means no scoring threat.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle releases high? Low F's collapse to the net.

question.prompt: D has the puck up top. Where do you go?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Low F collapses to the net on a point release. Tip, screen, or hunt the rebound.
feedback.partial: High slot is a possible rebound spot but the real work is at the net.
feedback.wrong: You're a non-threat from there. When the shot's coming, get inside.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck at the point. Your net-front man has a defender draped on them. Your strong-side winger is open for a pass along the wall.
U13 / Peewee.why: If the shot lane is clogged, don't force it — move the puck to your open winger, reset, and try again. A blocked point shot leads straight into a rush the other way.
U13 / Peewee.tip: No shot lane? Don't force. Move it.

question.prompt: Shot lane is clogged. What do you do?

options.text: Move it to your open LW on the wall
options.feedback: Smart. Reset the look, create a new shot angle, don't feed the penalty kill.

options.text: Shoot it anyway — net has good rebounds
options.feedback: Blocked point shot = short-handed rush. Not worth the risk when you have an open pass.

options.text: D-to-D across the blue line
options.feedback: Safe reset but a wall pass to the LW gives you a better next-shot angle.

options.text: Pinch down for a wraparound
options.feedback: You're the D. Pinching down the boards leaves the point wide open for a rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your line is entering the zone. F1 takes the puck wide. F2 drives the net. You're F3 — the trailer. Where do you arrive?
U13 / Peewee.why: F3 arrives at the high slot to be an outlet for a drop pass or a delay play. Going low takes you out of the play and eliminates the trailer option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: F3 trails? High slot, always.

question.prompt: Where do you arrive as the trailer?

feedback.correct: High slot. Behind the D, one stride from shot or pass, and a clean drop-pass target.
feedback.partial: Slot is a scoring spot but not the trailer spot. F3 needs the high slot to support the rush.
feedback.wrong: You're out of the play. Trailer = high slot. Period.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're F1 pressuring the D on a retrieval. The D sees you coming and leaves the puck for their partner who is hidden behind the net.
U13 / Peewee.why: When a D leaves the puck and you commit too hard, you're out of the play. Peeling off to take away the partner or the up-boards outlet is the better read than chasing nothing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: D leaves the puck? Don't chase air — find the next read.

question.prompt: D left the puck for their partner. What do you do?

options.text: Peel off and take the up-boards outlet
options.feedback: Great read. You take away the breakout pass while your teammates collapse behind.

options.text: Keep chasing the puck
options.feedback: Chasing means you're out of the play. The partner now has time and space.

options.text: Stop and wait for them to move
options.feedback: Standing still takes you out of both the forecheck and the read. Commit to a lane.

options.text: Go to the net-front to screen
options.feedback: Wrong zone for a forechecker. The net-front is for offensive setups — not breakout reads.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck in the corner. A defender is angling you into the wall.
U13 / Peewee.why: Turning your body between the puck and the defender (a cut-back or protect spin) buys time and keeps the puck on your strong side. Trying to beat a defender on their hand is how you lose the puck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender on your wrong hand? Spin and protect.

question.prompt: Defender is pinning you. What do you do?

options.text: Cut back and protect the puck with your body
options.feedback: Good hockey. You keep the puck on your strong side and the defender can't reach.

options.text: Try to beat them to the middle
options.feedback: Going middle into their stick is exactly where they want you. Turnover city.

options.text: Throw a blind backhand to the slot
options.feedback: Blind backhands in the corner go to the other team most of the time.

options.text: Rim it around to the far corner
options.feedback: Safe play — you keep possession. But cutting back creates offense; the rim just resets.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A puck is coming up the strong-side boards to an opposing winger. You're the strong-side D. Your F3 is high in the neutral zone.
U13 / Peewee.why: With F3 high, a missed pinch becomes a 3-on-2 or worse. Pinch only if you win the race — otherwise hold the line and backcheck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pinch = full commit. Not sure? Don't.

question.prompt: Pinch or retreat?

options.text: Pinch — I have F3 support behind me
options.feedback: Right read. If you win the race, O-zone stays alive. If you don't, F3 covers.

options.text: Retreat — I'm not sure I win the race
options.feedback: Also right. If you can't commit 100%, don't. A missed pinch beats a clean one.

options.text: Pinch without looking at my support
options.feedback: A pinch without knowing who's behind you is a gamble — not a hockey read.

options.text: Half-pinch and see what develops
options.feedback: A half-pinch is worse than either option. You're neither killing the play nor defending.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D is about to shoot from the point. The net-front is already occupied by your C. You're the second forward.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the primary screen is set, the second forward fills the slot for rebounds and second-shot chances. Two bodies at the net-front clogs your own shooter's lane.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Net-front full? Fill the slot.

question.prompt: D is shooting. Where do you fill?

feedback.correct: Slot. Rebounds that pop out find you. Second-shot threat created.
feedback.partial: High slot works as a trailer spot but the slot is a more direct rebound threat.
feedback.wrong: You're either in the way of the shot or out of the play entirely.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck on the breakout in your own zone. F1 is pressuring you on the boards. The glass above you has a clean rim lane.
U13 / Peewee.why: A rim up the strong-side wall gets the puck out of the zone and to a safe area. A chip in the middle gets picked off. Pick your outlet based on what's open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pressure on the wall? Rim up the strong-side.

question.prompt: F1 is on you. How do you get it out?

options.text: Rim up the strong-side wall to your LW
options.feedback: Clean exit. Your LW pins it and the zone is cleared.

options.text: Chip it up the middle of the ice
options.feedback: Middle chip gets blocked by the trailing F. That's an O-zone chance against.

options.text: Throw it around the boards the wrong way
options.feedback: Reverses the pressure but also hands possession to the forecheck. Rim strong-side was better.

options.text: Skate behind the net and hope for options
options.feedback: You had a rim. Skating the puck under pressure in your own zone invites more trouble.

U13 / Peewee.sit: 2-on-2 entering the zone. You have the puck. The strong-side D is on you, the weak-side D is tight on your linemate.
U13 / Peewee.why: 2-on-2s don't have a lot of space. Dumping the puck deep and using speed to forecheck wins most of these. Forcing a pass into two defenders is a recipe for a turnover the other way.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-2 with no space? Dump and chase beats forcing.

question.prompt: 2-on-2, no space. Best play?

options.text: Dump it deep and forecheck hard
options.feedback: Right read. Two forwards on a forecheck beats a forced pass through two defenders.

options.text: Cross-ice pass to C
options.feedback: Pass goes through two defenders at the blue line. High-turnover play.

options.text: Try to beat your D one-on-one
options.feedback: Sometimes works, but dumping the puck with a full line behind is safer offense.

options.text: Pull up and regroup
options.feedback: Fine if you have support behind — but if you have speed, dumping and chasing uses it better.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're at the red line with speed. Their D is at the blue line with a tight gap.
U13 / Peewee.why: Tight gap = chip-and-chase. No carry option. No reason to dump if you can chip behind them.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Tight D gap → chip behind.

question.prompt: Tight-gap D at the line. Best entry?

options.text: Chip the puck past them and race to recover.
options.feedback: Yes — denies their pinch, reclaims in the O-zone.

options.text: Try to carry through them.
options.feedback: You'll get stopped. Wasted rush.

options.text: Dump it in the corner.
options.feedback: Gives up possession. Chip is better if you have speed.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D retrieves behind the net with F1 closing hard. All wingers are covered.
U13 / Peewee.why: If all outlets are covered, the D's best read is to wheel — skate it out and beat F1 with speed.
U13 / Peewee.tip: No outlet + speed = wheel.

question.prompt: F1 closing, wingers covered. D does…

options.text: Wheel it out — skate up the strong side and beat F1.
options.feedback: Yes — wheel when outlets are covered.

options.text: Reverse to the other D.
options.feedback: Other D is locked down too. Wheel is the move.

options.text: Rim it hard around the boards.
options.feedback: Last-resort. Wheel keeps possession.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the weak-side D. A rush is developing on the strong side.
U13 / Peewee.why: Weak-side D stands up at the line to take away the cross-ice pass and keep gap on the late guy.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Weak-side D: hold the line, take the pass option.

question.prompt: Where do you hold?

feedback.correct: Yes — stand up, take the cross-ice pass away.
feedback.partial: You're closing the slot, which is fine, but holding the line is the primary job.
feedback.wrong: You're out of position. Hold the line.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team runs a 2-1-2 forecheck. F1 takes the puck, F2 reads second support. Where does F2 go?
U13 / Peewee.why: F2 takes the second pass option — the D's outlet. If F1 angles strong side, F2 kills the reverse.
U13 / Peewee.tip: F1 on the puck → F2 on the reverse/outlet.

question.prompt: F1 has strong side. F2 goes where?

feedback.correct: Yes — take away the reverse to the other D.
feedback.partial: Fine if you pressure next, but behind-the-net is the textbook cut.
feedback.wrong: That leaves the reverse wide open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is low. You're the D at the blue line and a winger is open in the high slot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Activating — sneaking down to the slot — creates scoring chances your opponents can't cover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Activating D = sneaky, not wild.

question.prompt: Low puck, winger high and open. D's move?

options.text: Slide down to the slot as a late option.
options.feedback: Yes — activate into the open ice for a shot.

options.text: Stay flat at the blue line.
options.feedback: That's what they expect. Activate when it's open.

options.text: Call for the puck back.
options.feedback: Sometimes. But activating creates a better shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The opposing winger has the puck in your corner. You're the strong-side D.
U13 / Peewee.why: Strong-side D: stick-on-puck, body between them and the net, keep them on the wall.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Deny the cut to the slot.

question.prompt: Their W on the wall with puck. You…

options.text: Pin them to the wall, keep them from cutting to the slot.
options.feedback: Yes — wall-play, no middle.

options.text: Stand off them waiting for help.
options.feedback: They'll cut to the slot and score.

options.text: Dive to the ice to block the pass.
options.feedback: Too early. Tie them up first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck-carrier on a 3-on-2. The strong-side D is skating backwards, the weak-side D is open.
U13 / Peewee.why: If the weak-side D is retreating, the puck-side shot is your best look. They're giving you the strong-side net.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shoot where the D isn't.

question.prompt: Strong-side D on you, weak-side D retreating. You…

options.text: Shoot to the strong-side short-corner.
options.feedback: Yes — they're not covering that net.

options.text: Drop pass and hope for a shot.
options.feedback: Drops kill momentum. Shoot.

options.text: Pass cross-ice.
options.feedback: Available, but the shot is there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team runs a 1-2-2 in the neutral zone. How should your D break it?
U13 / Peewee.why: 1-2-2 gives you the wings. Use the middle-lane speed to force them into tough reads.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Trap neutralizer = quick middle-lane reads + board support.

question.prompt: Break the 1-2-2 by…

options.text: Quick middle pass to the C with wall wingers ready.
options.feedback: Yes — exploit the middle, wings stretch wide.

options.text: Dump it in always.
options.feedback: You give them back-side recovery. Lose possession.

options.text: Cycle low to high.
options.feedback: Good later. First break is a middle pass.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're a PK forward. The puck moves from the strong-side point to the top. Who rotates?
U13 / Peewee.why: On a PK diamond, the top forward reads and the weak-side F fills behind. Wings cover points.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK = rotate, don't chase.

question.prompt: Puck to top. You're top F. You…

options.text: Slide to pressure the new puck-side point, stay in the lane.
options.feedback: Yes — rotate, don't chase.

options.text: Charge into the corner.
options.feedback: You just broke the box.

options.text: Drop to the slot.
options.feedback: Sometimes. First move is the rotation.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the weak-side forward. The puck is on the strong-side half-wall. What's your job?
U13 / Peewee.why: Weak-side F is the one-timer threat. Stay off the line to shoot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stand off the net post — off the goalie's line.

question.prompt: Where do you set up for the weak-side shot?

feedback.correct: Yes — weak-side faceoff dot, stick loaded.
feedback.partial: Slot's closer but you want a shooting angle — dot's cleaner.
feedback.wrong: Too far — no shot from there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate wins a puck in the corner with immediate pressure. What do you do as F2?
U13 / Peewee.why: F2 gives a short support over the puck-carrier's shoulder — never far away, never right on top.
U13 / Peewee.tip: F2 = 15 feet away, above the puck.

question.prompt: Where does F2 support?

feedback.correct: Yes — short support on the wall, above the puck.
feedback.partial: Slot works if you're screening. Short support is the first job.
feedback.wrong: Too far. F1 can't hit you.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're alone against a 3-on-1. Puck-carrier is in the middle. Two wingers drive wide.
U13 / Peewee.why: 3-on-1: stay middle, take the pass to the strong side (shooter's-left), force the weak-side shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cover middle, force low-% shot.

question.prompt: 3-on-1 against. Best play?

options.text: Stay centered, take the strong-side pass, let goalie play shot.
options.feedback: Yes — you can't cover everything; cover the most dangerous.

options.text: Charge the puck-carrier.
options.feedback: Now it's 2-on-0.

options.text: Drop to block.
options.feedback: Only if the shot is already coming.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're a D carrying the puck up the strong side. A F1 is bearing down. What do you do?
U13 / Peewee.why: Constantly scanning — head on a swivel — is what keeps the D a step ahead of the forecheck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Scan shoulder-to-shoulder before every touch.

question.prompt: F1 bearing down. You…

options.text: Pre-scan both sides, choose the support option.
options.feedback: Yes — scan, then decide.

options.text: Focus only on the puck.
options.feedback: Head down = bad pass or a hit.

options.text: Stop and wait for F1.
options.feedback: Dangerous. Move with a plan.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're skating the puck into the O-zone corner with a defender on your back.
U13 / Peewee.why: A cutback into space delays the defender and opens a cycle option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast.

question.prompt: Defender on your back in the corner. You…

options.text: Cutback into open ice, find a cycle partner.
options.feedback: Yes — create time, don't force a play.

options.text: Throw the puck to the net.
options.feedback: Throwaway. Possession matters.

options.text: Try to take the D 1-on-1.
options.feedback: Possible, but cutback is higher percentage.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A 2-on-2 rush is coming. You're F3, late back. What's your job?
U13 / Peewee.why: F3 takes the slot or the third attacker if one trails — don't duplicate what the D is already doing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: F3 covers what D can't.

question.prompt: Trailer in the slot. F3 does…

options.text: Take the trailer — stick on stick in the slot.
options.feedback: Yes — F3 covers the slot trailer.

options.text: Chase the puck-carrier.
options.feedback: The D already has them. Don't duplicate.

options.text: Drop to net-front.
options.feedback: Only if the D can't box out.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're open in the high slot. A pass just arrived. What's your first thought?
U13 / Peewee.why: Shoot first. Pass only if the shot isn't there. Too many U13s pass up shots.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shoot. The rebound is the next pass.

question.prompt: You're open in the slot with the puck. You…

options.text: Shoot immediately.
options.feedback: Yes — goalies hate quick releases from the slot.

options.text: Look for a pretty pass.
options.feedback: Classic U13 miss. Shoot.

options.text: Stickhandle to get a better angle.
options.feedback: Sometimes — but release speed matters more than angle.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Strong-side D has the puck with pressure. Weak-side D is open.
U13 / Peewee.why: D-to-D reverses the forecheck and resets the breakout — buys a second of space.
U13 / Peewee.tip: D-to-D changes the forecheck angle.

question.prompt: Pressure on your partner. Best play?

options.text: Open for a D-to-D, let them reverse to you.
options.feedback: Yes — reset the breakout.

options.text: Go help in the corner.
options.feedback: Now both Ds are in the corner and F1 has the middle.

options.text: Skate to the front of the net.
options.feedback: That's not a D-zone move.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A loose puck in the neutral zone. A defender's stick is in the lane.
U13 / Peewee.why: Stick-lift is how you steal pucks cleanly. Pokes miss. Lifts work.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Lift through their stick, not at the puck.

question.prompt: Contested loose puck. You…

options.text: Stick-lift their stick, then take the puck.
options.feedback: Yes — textbook puck-battle move.

options.text: Cross-check them to get position.
options.feedback: Penalty — you lose possession and a power play.

options.text: Poke at the puck.
options.feedback: Low-% move. Lifts work better.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're weak-side F. Your team cycles to the strong-side half-wall.
U13 / Peewee.why: The backdoor is the weak-side forward sneaking to the far post for a one-touch goal.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Backdoor = weak-side F hiding off the post.

question.prompt: Where do you hide for the backdoor?

feedback.correct: Yes — far post, off the goalie's sightline.
feedback.partial: Closer in is the play.
feedback.wrong: Out of range. Backdoor is the FAR side of the net.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A point shot is coming. You're a D. Their forward is between you and the net.
U13 / Peewee.why: Box-out: get a body on them and keep them from touching the rebound.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Tie up the stick, body in front.

question.prompt: Point shot coming. Forward in front. You…

options.text: Tie up their stick and body them to the outside.
options.feedback: Yes — no rebound goal.

options.text: Look at the shot.
options.feedback: You'll miss the forward and they'll bury the rebound.

options.text: Drop to block.
options.feedback: Only if you HAVE to. Box-out first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You pass to a teammate and want the puck back. How do you signal?
U13 / Peewee.why: Give-and-go works when the passer moves to space immediately after the pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pass and move — not pass and stand.

question.prompt: You just passed. You…

options.text: Skate to open ice and call for it back.
options.feedback: Yes — that's the 'go' in give-and-go.

options.text: Stand still watching.
options.feedback: Now your teammate has no return option.

options.text: Skate straight to the net.
options.feedback: Sometimes. Open ice gives a cleaner return.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A loose puck is heading to the offensive corner. Their D and you are neck-and-neck.
U13 / Peewee.why: First to the puck with leverage wins the race and the zone.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Short first steps. Beat them to the spot.

question.prompt: Race to a loose puck. Your move?

options.text: Lower my shoulder, box them off the lane.
options.feedback: Yes — legal leverage wins pucks.

options.text: Chop their stick early.
options.feedback: Slashing penalty. Bad trade.

options.text: Let them have it and cover back.
options.feedback: Too passive. Compete for it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Entry denied. Your team regroups. The D is at the red line with the puck. Where do you go?
U13 / Peewee.why: On a regroup, Fs curl to the strong side to accept the next pass with speed.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Regroup = curl back, get speed before reversing direction.

question.prompt: Where do Fs go on the regroup?

feedback.correct: Yes — curl wide into speed, strong side.
feedback.partial: Stay moving, but wider is better.
feedback.wrong: Too static. You need to generate speed.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Keep the puck alive on walls
U13 / Peewee.sit: A rim from the D is coming around the boards. You're the weak-side forward at the wall.
U13 / Peewee.why: Getting a piece of the rim to keep it alive in the zone is a simple, winnable puck-battle.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Angle to the wall, one-touch it down low.

question.prompt: Rim is coming at you. Best play?

options.text: Angle to the wall, one-touch it to the corner.
options.feedback: Yes — keep it alive in their zone.

options.text: Stand still and let it come.
options.feedback: You'll give the opposing D time to get there first.

options.text: Try to pick it clean with your stick.
options.feedback: Low-% under pressure. One-touch is better.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your line just got scored on. You're heading to the bench.
U13 / Peewee.why: Body language is leadership. Heads up, short shift, ready to go again.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Next shift matters more than last shift.

question.prompt: Scored against. You head to the bench. You…

options.text: Head up, ready for my next shift. Short memory.
options.feedback: Yes — great players reset fast.

options.text: Slam my stick on the boards.
options.feedback: Coaches notice. Teammates notice. Fix it next shift.

options.text: Blame the goalie under my breath.
options.feedback: Low move. Own the mistake, move on.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone at you. You're the lone D back. Where should your gap be?
opts: One stick length at the top of the circles to force a quick decision.
Back up to your own goal line to give yourself maximum time to react.
At the far blue line so you can close the distance with a big.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight gap at the top of the circles forces the attacker to make their move early. Too far back gives them time and space.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Gap control: top of the circles. Force their hand early.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier dumps the puck in and skates past you along the boards. They no longer have the puck. Should you finish the check?
opts: Yes — finishing the check discourages them from coming back for it.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Yes — hit every player near you regardless of where the puck went.
No — save your energy for the next play and let them go past you.
U15 / Bantam.why: Finishing the check on a player who just released the puck is legal and smart. It separates them from the play.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Just dumped it? Finish the check. Legal and effective.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D has the puck behind the net. The forecheck sends one player hard. You're the center. Where should you be?
opts: In the middle around the hashmarks providing a short central option.
In the corner on the same side helping the D win the board battle.
At the far blue line waiting for a long stretch pass over instead.
Behind the net with the D so you can take a direct handoff.
U15 / Bantam.why: The center's breakout job is to be the middle option at the hashmarks — short, safe, and transitions the puck quickly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Center on the breakout: hashmarks. Be the middle option.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team turns the puck over in the offensive zone. You're the high forward. The opposing center has the puck. What's your job?
opts: Pick up the most dangerous opposing player and skate with them back.
Chase the puck carrier from behind and try to poke check.
Skate to your bench for a change since fresh legs are more instead.
Stay in the offensive zone in case your team gets the puck back.
U15 / Bantam.why: The high forward's backchecking job is to pick up the most dangerous player. Chasing the puck carrier from behind rarely works.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Turnover? Pick up a man. Don't chase — cover the dangerous player.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is killing a penalty in a box formation. The PP moves the puck from the half-wall to the point. What does the box do?
opts: Shift up and toward the puck as a unit to pressure and close lanes.
Collapse to the net and let the point player shoot.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
U15 / Bantam.why: The PK box shifts as a unit toward the puck. When it goes to the point, the box shifts up to close shooting and passing lanes.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK box: shift as a unit toward the puck. Stay connected.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're F1 on the forecheck. The D picks up the puck and starts to skate behind their net. How do you approach?
opts: Angle your skating to cut off one side and force them toward your F2.
Skate directly at them at full speed to try to get a big hit.
Peel off and go back to the neutral zone since they have possession.
Slow down and wait to see what they do before committing to any.
U15 / Bantam.why: Angling as F1 takes away options. Cut off one side and force them into your support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F1 forecheck: angle and cut off one side. Force them into support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D and an opposing forward is setting up in front of your net for a screen or deflection. What do you do?
opts: Tie up their stick and box them out of the prime scoring area there.
Push them hard from behind with both hands to get them away.
Ignore them and focus only on the puck carrier since that's.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
U15 / Bantam.why: Net-front defense is about stick work and body position. Tie up their stick and box them out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent at your net front? Tie up stick. Box out. Protect goalie's eyes.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your coach runs a 2-1-2 forecheck. You're one of the two forwards going in first. How does this differ from a 1-2-2?
opts: You and your partner pressure together aggressively to create turnovers.
You go in alone first and your partner hangs back at the red line area.
You both stay at center ice and wait for them to bring the puck to you.
You pinch from the blue line while your partner forechecks alone there.
U15 / Bantam.why: In a 2-1-2, two forwards pressure together. More aggressive than 1-2-2 with immediate support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 2-1-2: two in, pressure together. More aggressive with immediate support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has the puck in the O-zone and it gets turned over. You're the nearest forward. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Apply immediate pressure on the new puck carrier to force a quick error.
Chase the puck carrier all the way back into your defensive zone alone.
Skate straight to the bench for a change since your offensive shift.
Stand still and watch what the other team does before you react.
U15 / Bantam.why: Immediate pressure on the turnover is the first line of defense. Force a quick decision before they break out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: OZ turnover? Pressure immediately. Force a bad decision.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're having a great shift creating chances. But you've been on the ice for over a minute. What do you do?
opts: Get off the ice for a change — tired players make costly mistakes.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Wait until the puck is in the O-zone and then stay on.
Keep playing until you score since you are the best option out.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shift discipline matters more than individual performance. Tired players make turnovers that cost goals.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Great shift but tired? Get off. Discipline over heroics.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier enters the neutral zone with speed. You're the D. They're fast. How does their speed change your gap?
opts: Slightly bigger gap so they can't blow past you with their speed.
Tighter gap because fast players need to be challenged before.
Same gap regardless of how fast the puck carrier is skating at.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a fast puck carrier, a slightly bigger gap prevents getting caught flat-footed.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Fast carrier? Slightly bigger gap. Don't get caught flat-footed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is killing a penalty. The opposing D has the puck in their zone setting up their PP breakout. What's your approach as the forechecker?
opts: Controlled pressure — take away the easy first pass without going deep.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK forechecking is controlled pressure. Take away the easy pass without overcommitting.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK forecheck: controlled pressure. Don't get caught deep.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has an offensive zone faceoff. The coach calls a set play — puck back to the point for a shot. What's everyone's job?
opts: Win the draw back, forwards set a screen, defenseman shoots through.
Everyone crashes the net immediately after the puck drops.
The center tries to score directly off the faceoff since that's instead.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: Set plays require execution from all five players. Win it back, screen, shoot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Faceoff set play: everyone has a job. Execute together.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has been running a 2-1-2 forecheck but the other team keeps breaking it with quick D-to-D passes. What adjustment should you suggest?
opts: Take away the D-to-D pass by positioning F1 between the two D when they have the puck.
Keep running the same 2-1-2 since eventually it will work if you just skate.
Switch to a 1-2-2 trap and don't forecheck at all since nothing seems to be working.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: If D-to-D passes are beating your forecheck, F1 needs to position between the two D to take that option away. Adjusting forecheck positioning is smarter than just skating harder.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D-to-D beating you? F1 positions between the two D. Take it away.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is breaking out cleanly. You're a D and the forwards have speed through the neutral zone. There's a trailer lane open. Should you jump into the rush?
opts: Yes — fill the trailer lane and give your team an extra option in the offensive zone attack.
No — both D should always stay back regardless of how clean the breakout looks.
Only jump if the other team has already committed all their players back into their zone.
Wait until the forwards dump the puck in and then join the forecheck as the fifth.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the breakout is clean and there's a trailer lane, an activating D creates an overload in the offensive zone. The key is reading when it's safe.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Clean breakout with a trailer lane? Jump in. Extra option in the attack.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's PP is set up in the zone. The PK is in a tight box. Every pass to the slot is getting intercepted. What do you do?
opts: Move the puck around the perimeter faster to force the box to shift and create the seam.
Keep forcing passes to the slot since eventually one will get through if you throw instead.
Pull the puck out of the zone and re-enter to reset since the formation isn't.
Have all five players crash the net at the same time to create chaos in front.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight PK box is beaten by fast puck movement around the perimeter. Force them to shift — the seam opens when they can't rotate fast enough.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tight PK box? Move the puck faster around the perimeter. Force them to shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're one of two D on a 3-on-2 rush. The three attackers are spread across the ice. The puck is in the middle. What's your read?
opts: You take the wide player on your side and trust your partner to take the other and play shot.
Both D collapse to the middle to take away the shot and leave the wide players for your.
Both D back up to the goal line together and let the goalie handle everything.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a 3-on-2, each D takes a wide player. The middle player (shooter) is the goalie's responsibility. Collapsing both D to the middle leaves two wide options open.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 3-on-2: each D takes a wide player. Goalie plays the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An opposing forward receives a pass in the slot with their head down, looking at the puck. You're the nearest D. What's the right play?
opts: Close quickly and deliver a clean check through their body to separate them from the puck.
Stand back and poke check from a distance since body contact in the slot is too risky.
Wait for them to shoot and then try to block the shot with your body as it comes instead.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
U15 / Bantam.why: A player with their head down in the slot is vulnerable. A clean check through the body separates them from the puck and sends a message.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Head down in the slot? Close and separate. Clean hit, big message.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D at the offensive blue line. The puck is dumped into the corner by your forward. The opposing D gets to it first. You see their winger starting to leak out. What do you do?
opts: Hold the blue line and be ready to close on the winger if they try to exit on your side.
Pinch into the corner to try to force a turnover since your forward needs help winning it.
Skate backward to center ice to give yourself a head start in case the puck comes back out.
Leave the blue line completely and go help your forward in the corner win the puck instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: With a winger leaking out, holding the line is critical. If you pinch and the puck gets to that winger, it's a breakaway.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Winger leaking out? Hold the line. Don't pinch into a breakaway against.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on the PP half-wall. The puck is at the point. You see the PK is cheating toward the point shooter. The back-door player on the far side is open. What do you call for?
opts: The cross-ice pass from the point to the far side for the one-timer since the PK shifted away.
A pass to you on the half-wall since you're in a better position to shoot from your.
A shot from the point through traffic since that's the highest percentage play from that instead.
The puck back to the D in the corner to reset and try a new play since this look isn't.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the PK shifts toward the point, the far side opens up. A cross-ice pass for a one-timer exploits that shift perfectly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK shifting to the point? Cross-ice one-timer on the far side. Exploit the shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The opposing team is cycling in your zone. The puck carrier is below the goal line. You're the weak-side D. Where should your eyes be?
opts: On the weak-side player and the slot — the most dangerous pass is coming to your side next.
Only on the puck carrier since you need to see what they do before you can react to.
On your goalie to make sure they're set and tracking the puck through all the traffic.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: The weak-side D must have eyes on the weak-side player and the slot. The cross-ice pass or back-door play is the most dangerous option when the puck is below the goal line.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Weak-side D during a cycle: eyes on the weak-side player and slot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's forecheck pressure has been working — you're getting turnovers. Entering the zone, a defender backs off and gives you space at the blue line. Do you carry or dump?
opts: Carry the puck in since the defender is backing off and giving you time and space to attack.
Dump it in deep because that's what the forecheck system calls for regardless of what.
Pass it back to your D and regroup since you're not sure what to do with the extra space given.
Stop at the blue line and wait for all five players to get set before you make any instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: When a defender backs off and gives you space, carrying the puck in is the right read. Dumping when you have space wastes a possession advantage.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defender giving you space? Carry it in. Don't dump when you have time.

U15 / Bantam.concept: Puck protection below the goal line
U15 / Bantam.sit: You have the puck below the goal line in the offensive zone. A defender is closing on you. You're not in a position to pass. What do you do?
opts: Use your body to shield the puck and buy time until a teammate gets open for a pass option.
Try to force a centering pass through the defender's legs since that might create a chance.
Skate behind the net at full speed to the other side since speed alone will beat.
Leave the puck against the boards and skate away since there's no play to be made.
U15 / Bantam.why: Below the goal line, puck protection is king. Use your body as a shield, keep the puck on your backhand, and buy time for support to arrive.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Below the goal line with pressure? Shield the puck. Buy time. Wait for support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In the defensive zone, your winger's man cuts across the ice toward your side. Your man stays put. Should you and your winger switch assignments?
opts: Yes — switch and communicate so each D-man stays in their zone and covers the nearest threat.
No — always stay with your original man regardless of where they go on the ice during.
Only switch if the coach specifically told you to switch before the game or during the last timeout.
Let the winger decide since they had the original assignment and it's their responsibility.
U15 / Bantam.why: Switching when opponents cross is smart defensive zone play. The key is communication — both players need to know who has who.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent crosses zones? Switch and communicate. Don't chase across the ice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You notice the opposing team always sends their center up the middle on breakouts. Your team's F2 isn't picking the center up. What's the adjustment?
opts: Have F2 take away the middle lane on the forecheck so the center can't receive the breakout pass.
Have F1 chase the D harder into the corner since that will prevent the breakout pass after that.
Pull all three forwards back to the neutral zone and stop forechecking completely to stay.
Ignore the center and focus on the wingers since the center usually isn't the most dangerous option.
U15 / Bantam.why: If the opposing center is consistently getting the middle breakout pass, F2 needs to take that lane away. Recognizing patterns and adjusting is systems hockey IQ.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Center always getting the middle pass? F2 takes the lane away. Adjust.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. You get the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are closing. Your only option is the glass. Do you use it?
opts: Yes — get the puck off the glass and out of the zone to relieve the immediate pressure safely.
No — always try to make a play with the puck even if that means holding it under heavy pressure.
Yes — but aim for a teammate at the blue line to turn the clear into a breakout opportunity.
No — freeze the puck and take a faceoff instead since that resets the play more effectively.
U15 / Bantam.why: Using the glass to exit the zone under heavy pressure is a smart play. It relieves pressure and gets the puck out safely.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Heavy pressure, no options? Use the glass. Get it out. Live to play another shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on the PK in the box. The PP moves the puck to the half-wall and the shooter looks like they're going to shoot. You're the nearest PK player. What do you do?
opts: Get your stick in the shooting lane to block or deflect the shot without fully committing out.
Charge at the shooter to close the distance and try to block the shot with your body.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Drop to both knees and slide toward the shooter to cover as much of the lane as you possibly.
U15 / Bantam.why: Stick in the lane without overcommitting keeps you in the PK structure while disrupting the shot. Charging out leaves a gap in the box.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: stick in the lane. Don't overcommit. Disrupt without leaving a gap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're forechecking and the D is going to the boards to play the puck. You arrive at the same time. How do you win this battle?
opts: Arrive with speed and pin them to the boards — use your body to separate them from the puck first.
Slow down before you arrive and try to poke check the puck away without making any body contact.
Skate past them and go behind the net to wait for the puck to come around the boards to you.
Stop before the boards and wait to see what they do with the puck before engaging in any.
U15 / Bantam.why: Winning the wall means arriving with speed and using your body. Pin them, separate them from the puck, and get possession.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Forechecking to the wall: arrive with speed. Pin. Separate. Win the puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D making a breakout pass. Your winger is open on the wall but skating at full speed. Where do you put the pass?
opts: Tape-to-tape — lead them slightly so the puck arrives in stride without them slowing down at all.
Dump it in and change immediately instead of pushing the transition.
Behind them so they have to turn around and come back for the puck.
Loft it high in the air so it goes over the forechecker's stick and lands near your winger up ahead.
U15 / Bantam.why: A breakout pass to a player in stride must lead them. Tape-to-tape in stride keeps the speed of the transition — a pass to their feet kills the momentum.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakout pass to a player in stride: lead them. Tape-to-tape. Keep the speed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up by one goal with two minutes left. The opposing team is pressing hard. You're the D. How does your gap change?
opts: Slightly tighter gap — force quick decisions and don't give them time or space to create anything.
Much bigger gap — play ultra-conservative and back up to the goal line to make sure nothing gets.
Same gap as always since you shouldn't change your defensive approach based on the game.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: With a lead late, slightly tighter gap forces the other team into quick, low-percentage decisions. Backing up too far invites pressure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Protecting a lead? Slightly tighter gap. Force quick decisions. Don't back up.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Their best player is coming at you 1-on-1. They're known for their speed and deking ability. They've beaten you before with a wide fake and cut back inside. How do you approach?
opts: Take away the inside lane and force them to the outside — accept the lower-percentage shot from the angle over a deke from slot.
Aggressively close your gap and try to force a turnover at the blue line before they can get up to speed and start their moves.
Back up to the goal line and give them space since they're too skilled to challenge one-on-one at any gap in the neutral.
Stand completely still at the top of the circles and wait for them to make the first move before reacting to anything they do.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a skilled deking player, taking away the inside lane and forcing them wide reduces their options. They have to beat you from a bad angle instead of the slot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Skilled deker? Take away inside. Force them wide. Bad angle beats slot deke.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An opposing forward establishes position in front of your net. They're screening your goalie and deflecting pucks. You've tried to box them out but they keep coming back. What's your next approach?
opts: Tie up their stick on every shot attempt and use your body to redirect their momentum away from the net front between whistles only.
Hit them as hard as you can from behind every time they set up in front since that's the only way to permanently remove them.
Give up on the net front and go cover someone else since if they want that position that badly there is nothing you can do to stop.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
U15 / Bantam.why: Net-front defense requires continuous effort. Tying up their stick prevents deflections, and using body position between whistles resets the battle. It's a war of attrition.
U15 / Bantam.tip: They keep coming back to the net front? Keep battling. Tie up stick on shots. Reset between whistles.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up by one with three minutes left. The coach calls for a 1-3-1 neutral zone trap. You're F1. How aggressive should you be on the forecheck?
opts: Controlled — angle and steer but don't overcommit, maintain the trap structure.
Very aggressive — pressure hard to force turnovers since a second goal would end the game and the best defense right now is a.
Improvise instead of following the system.
Ignore the trap call and play your normal game since changing your system with only three minutes left will confuse the team.
U15 / Bantam.why: Late-game trapping F1 is about controlled pressure. Angle and steer — don't overcommit. The trap works when F1 funnels the puck into the structure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Late-game trap: F1 controls the funnel. Angle, steer, don't overcommit.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone with full possession. Both wingers are below the dots cycling. Both D are at the blue line. The opposing team has dropped into a passive box. Should one D jump into the play?
opts: Yes — one D steps down to create a numerical advantage low while the other holds the line as safety in case of a turnover or breakout.
No — both D should always stay at the blue line since leaving the line is too risky regardless of how much possession your team has.
Yes — both D should jump into the play since you have full possession and there's no risk of a breakout when your team controls.
No — the D should pass the puck between each other at the blue line until a shooting lane opens up and then take a long-range.
U15 / Bantam.why: With full possession against a passive box, one D jumping down creates an overload. The key is one stays back as safety. Both going creates transition risk.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Passive box? One D steps down for the overload. Other holds the line.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're taking a defensive zone faceoff on the PK. Your team's faceoff play is to win it back to the D and clear it. You lose the draw cleanly to the PP center. What's the immediate priority?
opts: Sprint to cover the shooting lane and get in front of any shot — the PP will look to shoot immediately after a won O-zone faceoff draw.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Chase the center who won the draw and try to take the puck back from them before they can pass it to one of their power play instead.
Skate to the bench for a change since you lost the faceoff and a fresh penalty killer will be more effective than you right now.
U15 / Bantam.why: After losing a PK faceoff, the PP often looks for an immediate shot. Getting into the shooting lane quickly prevents the most dangerous opportunity.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Lost the PK faceoff? Sprint to the lane. Block the immediate shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An attacker has beaten you wide on a 1-on-1. They're now inside your zone with a step on you. Your goalie is set. What do you do to recover?
opts: Angle your recovery to take away the pass option while your goalie plays the shot — don't chase directly behind the puck carrier.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
Give up on the play since they've already beaten you and you'll only make things worse by being out of position chasing from behind.
Dive at their feet to try to trip them or knock the puck away since that's the only option left once you've been beaten on the rush.
U15 / Bantam.why: Recovery angle is critical. Don't chase directly behind — angle to take away the pass while your goalie plays the shot. Your goalie can handle the shooter; the pass is the danger.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Beaten wide? Angle your recovery to the pass lane. Goalie has the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're backchecking and an opposing forward is receiving a pass in the neutral zone at full speed. You're at an angle where you can deliver a legal body check. The puck is arriving in one second. What do you do?
opts: Time the hit to arrive just as the puck does — a clean check at the moment of reception separates them from the puck and kills the rush dead.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Skate past them and get ahead to set up your defensive positioning since the hit might not connect and then you'd be completely out of the play.
Wait until they have full possession and have made their first move before deciding whether to hit or play the puck on the defensive.
U15 / Bantam.why: Timing a hit to arrive with the puck is legal and devastating. It kills the rush and sends a message. The key is timing — too early is interference, too late is ineffective.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Hit timed with the puck arrival: legal, devastating, rush-killing. Timing is everything.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're killing a penalty. The opponent's PP runs a 1-3-1 with a one-timer option from the left circle. You've seen this three times. How do you adjust your PK?
opts: Overplay the one-timer side slightly and have the high PK player pressure the passer when the puck goes to the set-up spot on the left side.
Keep the box exactly the same since adjusting to one option will open up other options and the standard PK box is designed to handle.
Switch to a diamond PK formation since the 1-3-1 PP always beats the box and the diamond is designed specifically to counter the 1-3-1 offense.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: Recognizing the PP's go-to play and overplaying it is elite PK hockey IQ. You don't abandon the box — you adjust within it to take away their best option.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Seen their PP play three times? Overplay it. Adjust within your structure.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D with the puck in your zone. The forecheck is closing hard from both sides. Your outlets are covered. You have a split second. What creative option do you have?
opts: Use the back of the net as a shield to buy time — skate behind it and wait for the forecheck to commit before choosing your outlet play.
Try to split both forecheckers by skating between them since skilled D should be able to handle the puck under pressure in their own.
Shoot the puck directly at the forechecker and hope it bounces off them and goes to one of your teammates who can break out.
Dump it in and change immediately instead of pushing the transition.
U15 / Bantam.why: The back of the net is a tool. Using it as a shield buys time for outlets to open and forces the forecheck to commit to a side.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Outlets covered? Use the net. Shield the puck. Wait for the forecheck to commit.
