U15 / Bantam.sit: The opposing team dumps the puck in on every entry. They're fast and aggressive on the forecheck after the dump. How do you adjust your retrievals as a D?
opts: Communicate with your partner before each dump — one retrieves, one covers the net front. Move the puck quickly before their forecheckers arrive.
Both D go after every dump-in together since having two defensemen on the puck means you'll always win the race to the puck in the corner area.
Ignore the dump-in and let your goalie play the puck every single time since they're better at handling pucks behind the net than the D are at.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a dump-and-chase team, D communication on retrievals is critical. One goes, one covers. Quick puck movement before their speed arrives prevents the forecheck from working.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Dump-and-chase team? One D retrieves, one covers. Move the puck fast.

U15 / Bantam.concept: PK in the last minute of a penalty
U15 / Bantam.sit: There are 15 seconds left in your penalty kill. The PP is set up and looking dangerous. They're cycling the puck looking for the perfect play. What's the PK mindset?
opts: Stay disciplined in the box for 15 more seconds — don't chase, don't gamble, deny the high-danger shot and let the clock run out on the penalty.
Get aggressive and try to steal the puck since with only 15 seconds left you can take a risk to end the penalty kill early with a clear.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Pull one PK player off and play more passively since with only 15 seconds left the PP probably won't score and you can start transitioning to.
U15 / Bantam.why: 15 seconds of discipline ends the PK. Gambling or chasing with that little time left is unnecessary. Stay in structure, deny the dangerous play, and run the clock.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 15 seconds left on the PK? Stay disciplined. Don't chase. Run the clock.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just lost a tough game. The room is quiet. Some players are visibly upset. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Acknowledge the tough loss, point out something specific the team did well, and remind them of the next opportunity to compete and improve as a group.
Give a long speech about everything that went wrong so the team knows exactly what to fix before the next game and doesn't repeat the same.
Stay silent and let the coach handle everything since it's not your job to say anything after a loss the coaching staff's responsibility to.
Blame specific players for the loss so the team knows who was responsible and can hold them accountable for the mistakes that cost the game that instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: Post-loss leadership is about honesty and perspective. Acknowledging the loss, finding a positive, and looking forward gives the team something to build on.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tough loss? Acknowledge it. Find one positive. Point to the next one. That's leadership.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just got a new coach mid-season who runs a completely different system than you're used to. You're struggling to execute it. What's the right approach?
opts: Commit fully to learning the new system even if it feels uncomfortable — watch video, ask questions, and practice the details every single rep.
Keep playing the old system since it worked and the new coach should adapt to what the team already knows how to do instead of changing.
Only run the new system when the coach is watching and go back to the old one during games since the old system is what the team is.
Refuse to buy into the new system and tell your teammates it won't work since the old coach's system was better and the new one doesn't fit.
U15 / Bantam.why: Buying into a new system is a character test. Even when it's uncomfortable, committing fully — video, questions, practice — is how good players adapt.
U15 / Bantam.tip: New system? Commit fully. Watch video. Ask questions. Discomfort is growth.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck is below the goal line on your right side. The attacker is tight to the post. What's the correct post integration?
opts: RVH — pad stacked against the post, blocker and glove covering high to seal the entire post.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
VH with your lead leg up since that gives you more mobility to push across if they go.
U15 / Bantam.why: Below the goal line, RVH is the correct integration. It seals the bottom against the post while keeping blocker and glove covering high.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Below the goal line? RVH. Seal the bottom, cover the top.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck is dumped in behind your net. You leave to play it. A forechecker is closing. Your D is on the boards. What do you communicate?
opts: Call the play — 'Leave it!' if you have time, or 'Rim it!' to your D if pressure is too close.
Stay silent and make the play yourself since talking behind the net can distract your defensemen.
Yell 'Help!' to get all your teammates to come back behind the net and assist with the puck.
Don't communicate anything specific and let your D figure out on their own what the right call is.
U15 / Bantam.why: Goalie communication behind the net directs traffic. 'Leave it' tells the D you've got it. 'Rim' tells them you need them to take it. Clear calls prevent confusion.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Behind the net: direct traffic. 'Leave it' or 'Rim.' Be specific.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A point shot is coming and you see a forward in front with their stick on the ice, ready to tip. What do you prepare for?
opts: Expect the deflection — stay low in your stance with your stick on the ice and track the puck through the screen and forward's deflection.
Ignore the forward and focus only on the original shot from the point since the deflection might not happen and you can't prepare.
Stand up taller to see over the forward since a higher stance gives you a better view of the point shot as it comes through the traffic.
Drop into a full butterfly before the shot even arrives to cover the bottom of the net since deflections usually go low and you need to.
U15 / Bantam.why: A forward with their stick on the ice is a deflection setup. Stay low, track through, and expect the direction change. Deflections change angle and elevation.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Stick on the ice in front? Expect the tip. Stay low. Track through.

U15 / Bantam.sit: It's a 2-on-1. Your D is taking away the pass. The puck carrier is on the right side driving to the net. What's your positioning?
opts: Square to the shooter on the right side with good depth — trust your D on the pass and play the shot.
Cheat slightly to the far post since if the pass gets through you need to be ready for the instead.
Stay in the exact center of the net since you don't know if it's a shot or pass until it.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U15 / Bantam.why: When your D takes the pass, you play the shot. Square to the shooter with good depth — trust the D's job. If the pass gets through, that's a breakdown, not your read.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D takes the pass on 2-on-1? You play the shot. Square. Good depth. Trust.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Shooters in your league have started targeting your glove side high consistently. You've given up three glove-side goals in the last two games. How do you adjust?
opts: Work on your glove positioning in practice — start with the glove slightly higher and more visible to shooters to take away that visual cue for targeting.
Switch to catching with your blocker hand since your glove side is clearly a weakness and changing hands will confuse the shooters who have been instead.
Don't change anything since three goals is a small sample size and adjusting your technique based on a few goals will create new problems in other areas.
Tell your D to never let anyone shoot from your glove side since it's their job to take away that angle and any goal scored glove side high is a instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: Adjusting glove positioning is the right response. Starting slightly higher takes away the visual cue shooters are using. Address the weakness in practice.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Getting beaten glove side? Adjust the starting position. Work it in practice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is losing 1-0 with five minutes left. You've been solid all game. An attacker is coming on a breakaway. Do you change your approach?
opts: Stay patient and play your game — the same technique that's kept you in the game all night will give you the best chance to stop this breakaway.
Be way more aggressive than normal since you need to stop this at all costs and coming out past the circles is the only way to guarantee a.
Play more conservatively and back up deeper into the net since you absolutely cannot give up another goal and depth gives you more reaction.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U15 / Bantam.why: Game state doesn't change breakaway technique. The same patience and fundamentals that kept you in the game give you the best chance on any single save.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Losing and facing a breakaway? Same technique. Trust your game.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You gave up two goals in the second period — both on plays you'd normally save. The team is still in the game. What's your intermission routine?
opts: Reset mentally — review what went wrong briefly, then visualize making saves in the third. Focus on the next period, not the mistakes that happened.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Tell the team it was your fault and apologize repeatedly since your teammates need to know that you know the goals were soft and that you feel bad.
Don't think about hockey at all during the intermission — completely clear your mind and think about other things so you come out fresh.
U15 / Bantam.why: Brief review, then visualization of success. Dwelling causes doubt. Ignoring prevents learning. The balance is quick acknowledgment, then forward focus.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Bad period? Brief review. Visualize saves. Forward focus. Next period is new.

U15 / Bantam.sit: When your team is in the defensive zone with the puck, the first forward should immediately pinch the boards on every play.
U15 / Bantam.why: Pinching should be situational — only when you're sure you'll win the puck.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Pinch smart, not automatic.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Against a forward attacking at full speed, you should try to match their speed skating backwards.
U15 / Bantam.why: Backing up maintains gap and forces their decision.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Control the gap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On the penalty kill, the two forwards should focus on preventing zone entry, not creating counter-attacks.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK success depends on preventing entries and maintaining structure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK first: prevent zone entry.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A player should only demand accountability from teammates if they're having a great game themselves.
U15 / Bantam.why: Leadership is about effort and attitude, not perfection.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Lead by attitude and effort.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In transition, moving the puck quickly up ice is more important than finding the perfect pass.
U15 / Bantam.why: Quick transitions create odd-man advantages. Speed matters more than perfection.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition speed beats perfection.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You should avoid using your body on a defenseman because you'll be called for a penalty.
U15 / Bantam.why: Using your body properly is legal and encouraged. Penalties come from illegal contact.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Physical play is legal if positioned correctly.

U15 / Bantam.sit: When up 3-1 late, you should play defensively and avoid risks with the puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Protecting a lead means controlled possession and smart puck management.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Protecting lead = smart puck management.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of a controlled breakout in order.
items: Defenseman secures puck behind net
D creates space with lateral movement
Forward support arrives at hash marks
D makes crisp pass to forward in stride
Forward exits cleanly up board or middle
U15 / Bantam.why: Breakout requires D to secure, create space, pass when support ready, then exit cleanly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakout: secure, move, support, pass, exit.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of penalty kill entry prevention.
items: First forward pressures puck carrier
Second forward angles to boards
Together prevent the zone entry
Transition to short-handed counter if available
U15 / Bantam.why: PK requires engagement, angling, prevention, then counter opportunity.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: engage, angle, prevent, counter.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of setting up a 1-3-1 neutral zone trap in order.
items: F1 engages puck carrier
D establish blue line structure
F2/F3 move to passing lanes
If turnover, transition to offense
U15 / Bantam.why: Trap requires F1 engage first, then D set, then wings block lanes.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-3-1: F1 engages, D set, wings cover lanes.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of defending a 1-on-1 situation in order.
items: Read the attacker approach and speed
Maintain gap and body position
Force them to outside if possible
Stay on feet and make the play
U15 / Bantam.why: 1-on-1 requires reading, maintaining gap, forcing issue, then reacting.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-on-1: read, gap, force outside, stay up.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of executing a fast break in transition.
items: First pass breaks first line of defense
Second pass creates odd-man advantage
Final pass sets up scoring opportunity
Take the shot or create best play
U15 / Bantam.why: Fast breaks build in rhythm: break line, create advantage, position, score.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Fast break: break line, advantage, position, finish.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of a proper slot attack sequence.
items: Recognize the open slot area
Move into slot with purpose
Receive pass in sweet spot
Shoot immediately without hesitation
U15 / Bantam.why: Slot play requires reading area, getting in, receiving clean, then shooting fast.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Slot: read, move, receive, shoot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the communication steps during defensive play.
items: Call out opponent assignments
Confirm coverage with partner
Make callouts as play develops
Reset communication after play
U15 / Bantam.why: Communication starts with clear assignments, confirms coverage, continues, then resets.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defense talk: assign, confirm, continue, reset.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of executing a zone cycle.
items: Enter zone with support arriving
Cycle puck around perimeter
Create rebound opportunity in slot
Maintain possession or create scoring chance
U15 / Bantam.why: Cycling builds time and opportunity: enter, move, create, finish.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Cycle: enter, move, create, finish.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You skate forward to challenge early instead of backing up. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have stayed deeper.
You overcommitted — closing gap early gives speed advantage and scoring chance.
You didn't use your stick effectively.
You angled them wrong.
U15 / Bantam.why: Overcommitting closes gap and removes ability to react.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Maintain gap first. Challenge when sure.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In transition at hash marks with no support, you try to deke through two defenders. What's the mistake?
opts: Deking is never right.
You forced play instead of letting transition develop — support arrives if you buy time.
You should have shot.
You didn't protect puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Forcing plays in transition kills momentum. Support arrives if you buy time.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition: buy time, let support arrive.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On PK, you chase the puck instead of maintaining coverage, leaving your player open. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have predicted the pass.
You left your man open — coverage matters more than chasing puck.
You moved too slowly.
You should have blocked the pass.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK is about structure and coverage. Chasing pucks breaks assignments.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: maintain coverage assignment.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You receive pass in slot and hesitate to adjust puck. Goalie sets and saves. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have passed.
You hesitated — in slot with open lane, shoot immediately.
The pass was late.
The goalie got lucky.
U15 / Bantam.why: Slot opportunities are high-percentage only if you shoot fast.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Slot: receive and shoot immediately.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In a 1-3-1 trap, F1 leaves the puck carrier to help F2 block a passing lane. What's the mistake?
opts: F1 should support behind the play.
F1 abandoned their primary responsibility — engaging the puck carrier is the foundation of the trap.
F2 should handle the puck carrier.
The trap setup was wrong.
U15 / Bantam.why: Trap fails if F1 doesn't maintain pressure on puck carrier.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Trap: F1 engagement is foundation.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You see a gap opening but don't call it out to your partner, who gets caught out of position. What's the mistake?
opts: Your partner should have seen it.
You didn't communicate — calling gaps keeps partner informed and alert.
Your partner should have moved.
The play was too fast.
U15 / Bantam.why: Defense communication prevents coverage breakdowns.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Talk constantly on defense.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Against a rushing attacker, you use your body too aggressively and get called for a check from behind. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have let them score.
You overcommitted with your body — positioning and angling work better than aggressive contact.
The ref was wrong.
You should have pushed harder.
U15 / Bantam.why: Effective physical play uses positioning and angle, not aggression.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Positioning beats aggression.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Down 2-1 with 30 seconds left, you throw the puck away trying a risky pass instead of shooting. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have passed.
You were reckless — in desperation, shot > risky pass. You need a goal.
The goalie made a save.
You needed more skill.
U15 / Bantam.why: Late-game deficit requires goal-scoring focus, not playmaking.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Down late: create shots, not chances for others.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Breaking out at hash marks, opposing forward approaches. What next?
opts: Dump it in deep before defender closes.
Cut toward middle and look for support threading through.
Protect puck and continue up board.
Pass back to trailing defenseman.
U15 / Bantam.why: Support arriving through middle creates odd-man advantage.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition: use support in middle.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Backing up maintaining gap, attacker cuts toward middle. What next?
opts: Stay in middle to cut pass.
Follow them — stay tight.
Shuffle across staying between them and net.
Keep backing straight — let partner help.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shuffle across (not turn back). Stay gap-conscious.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Shuffle with them. Stay gap-conscious.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You shoot from the point. Goalie makes the save and rebound bounces loose. What next?
opts: Back off — give the goalie space.
Push hard to first rebound and follow up with another shot.
Pass to open teammate.
Let the play develop.
U15 / Bantam.why: Loose rebounds are second chances. Push hard and be ready to shoot again.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Rebound: push hard and be ready.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on PK and gain possession. You have a 3-on-2 short-handed break. What next?
opts: Conservative pass and reset.
Attack with speed — convert to scoring chance.
Dump it in and retreat.
Wait for teammates to get back.
U15 / Bantam.why: Short-handed breaks are premium opportunities. Attack with speed.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Short-handed break: attack with speed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Applying a hard forecheck, the D passes it to a forechecker's wing. What next?
opts: Back off the pressure.
Immediately transition to defense — they may break through.
Keep pressing.
Let the wing figure it out.
U15 / Bantam.why: When forecheck backfires, transition quickly to defense.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Forecheck failure = quick transition.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up 2-1 with 5 minutes left. Opponent scores to tie 2-2. What next?
opts: Play more aggressive to retake lead.
Play controlled and create even-strength chances.
Go into full defensive mode.
Push for overtime win.
U15 / Bantam.why: Tied late, controlled play creates chances without risk of odd-man rush.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tied late: controlled possession.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your defensive partner is beat on their side and the forward is heading to the net. What next?
opts: Go back to check on them.
Be ready to support and cover the assignment.
Let them handle it.
Change immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Supporting your partner's mistake prevents the goal. Communication and position matter.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Back up your partner on D.

U15 / Bantam.sit: 2-on-1 down the middle. You have the puck on the left. Your teammate is on the right. The lone D is shading toward your teammate.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a 2-on-1, the D's body language tells you what to do. D leaning off you = shoot, because the goalie has to respect you and can't cheat to the pass. Pass too early and the D reads it.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D leans off? Shoot. D takes you? Pass. Read, don't guess.

question.prompt: D is leaning toward your teammate. What's the play?

options.text: Shoot low glove-side and look for a rebound
options.feedback: D leaning off you = shoot. Goalie can't cheat to the pass, so you're shooting into a full opening.

options.text: Pass across early to the weak-side winger
options.feedback: Early pass lets the D read it and get the stick in the lane. You haven't forced the goalie to respect you.

options.text: Delay and wait for the trailing center
options.feedback: Delay gives the back-checker time to catch up. If you've got a trailer close, maybe — but the 2-on-1 advantage disappears fast.

options.text: Cut across in front to try a wraparound
options.feedback: Wraparound on a 2-on-1 gives up the shot lane and lets both the D and goalie reset. No angle, no play.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Opponent is going behind the net about to rim the puck around the boards. F1 and F2 are on the forecheck. You're F3 — the high forward supporting the play.
U15 / Bantam.why: F3's job on a rim is to kill the puck on the strong-side boards before it clears. Cheating to the middle lets the puck skip past you up the wall and out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F3 on a rim? Support the strong-side wall, not the middle.

question.prompt: Where do you sit as F3 to kill the rim?

feedback.correct: Strong-side wall. You kill the rim before it clears, and you're in a spot to either chip it back deep or start the cycle.
feedback.partial: High slot is the right general area but you need to be tight to the wall — not in the middle — to actually stop the rim.
feedback.wrong: That spot lets the puck skip right past you and out of the zone. F3 has to be on the strong-side boards to kill the play.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You have the puck at the point. The defender is aggressive, the shot lane is blocked, but you can walk laterally to change the angle.
U15 / Bantam.why: Walking the line opens a new shot lane by shifting the goalie and the defender. Good D walk before they shoot — they don't fire the first thing they see.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Shot lane blocked? Walk, don't force.

question.prompt: Shot lane is blocked. What do you do?

options.text: Walk the line toward the middle and shoot
options.feedback: Textbook. New angle, goalie has to move, net-front screens now live in the shot lane.

options.text: Shoot blindly through the lane
options.feedback: Blocked shot = short-handed rush the other way. Don't feed the PK-style look.

options.text: D-to-D and let your partner deal with it
options.feedback: Safe but you had a better option. Walking creates offense; the reset doesn't.

options.text: Dump it back to the corner for a cycle
options.feedback: Dumping from the point back to the corner on your own retrieval gives up possession.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your teammate is on the half-wall with time. You're the weak-side forward. Where do you set up to receive a seam pass?
U15 / Bantam.why: The seam receiver doesn't stand still. You arrive late into the high-slot-to-slot gap as the pass comes — any earlier and the D closes on you, any later and the pass doesn't find you.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Seam receiver? Arrive late into the slot.

question.prompt: Where do you arrive for the seam pass?

feedback.correct: Slot. Receive, turn, shoot. That's the whole play.
feedback.partial: Close, but the slot is the money spot. High slot is a setup — not the release zone.
feedback.wrong: That spot can't release a seam pass into a scoring chance. You're out of the play.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're retrieving a puck behind your net. You look up — your winger has a step on their defender through the neutral zone.
U15 / Bantam.why: A stretch pass to a fast winger with a step is one of the highest-percentage offensive plays in hockey. The D backs off, the winger gets speed, and you bypass the forecheck entirely.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Winger with a step? Stretch it.

question.prompt: Your RW has a step. What's the play?

options.text: Stretch pass up the weak-side to the RW
options.feedback: Bypass the forecheck, create a breakaway look. This is the play all day.

options.text: D-to-D for a slower breakout
options.feedback: Safe but you just skipped a breakaway setup. When the stretch is there, take it.

options.text: Rim it up the strong-side boards
options.feedback: A rim is fine but you had a better option. Rims are for when the stretch isn't there.

options.text: Skate it out yourself
options.feedback: Skating out when a stretch is open wastes the best offensive play available.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On the penalty kill. The other team has the puck behind your net. Your PK box has to decide how to react.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK boxes collapse tight to the net-front when the puck is behind. You don't chase — you deny the wrap and the seam pass by shrinking the box.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK + puck behind net? Collapse, don't chase.

question.prompt: Where do you sit as the slot PK forward?

feedback.correct: Slot. You deny the seam pass and the shot — that's the PK forward's job.
feedback.partial: Too close to the net. You give up the slot lane where their PP will try to one-time from.
feedback.wrong: You've left the most dangerous area uncovered. Stay in the slot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your line has overloaded the strong side with three forwards low. The puck comes to you on the half-wall. The weak side is empty.
U15 / Bantam.why: Overloads work because they pull defenders to one side. The release is the cross-ice pass to the weak-side point or a late-arriving weak-side F — that's where the open ice is.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Overload? The release is always weak-side.

question.prompt: Overload loaded on your side. What's the release?

options.text: Cross-ice to the LD at the weak-side point
options.feedback: That's the overload pay-off. Weak-side shot with no coverage = grade-A chance.

options.text: Cycle to the low corner again
options.feedback: Keeps the overload alive but doesn't release it. Eventually you have to move it weak-side.

options.text: Shoot from the half-wall
options.feedback: Too many bodies in the way and no release to the weak side. Waste a shot.

options.text: Pass back behind the net
options.feedback: Behind the net resets the overload — you just passed on the release.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On the power play with an umbrella setup. Two F's are low, one D at the top, two forwards flanking. You're F3 — the bumper. Where do you sit?
U15 / Bantam.why: The bumper sits in the mid-slot, ready for tips, one-timers, and short passes from the flanks. Going too low clogs the net-front F; going too high makes you a second point-man.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PP bumper? Mid-slot, always available.

question.prompt: Where does the bumper sit on the umbrella?

feedback.correct: Perfect bumper spot. Tips from the point shot, one-timer off the flank pass.
feedback.partial: Too high. The bumper release comes from mid-slot — high slot is the point's lane.
feedback.wrong: You're not in the umbrella. The bumper has a specific spot — the slot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're coming into the slot off a cross-seam pass. You have time to receive, but a quick one-touch redirect is also available.
U15 / Bantam.why: One-timers beat goalies when the pass is crisp and the goalie is still moving. But if the pass is slightly behind you or off your blade, a controlled shot-pass to a teammate or a quick tip is higher-percentage than fanning.
U15 / Bantam.tip: One-timer only if the puck is on your blade. Otherwise, settle and release.

question.prompt: Pass is coming to you in the slot. What's the read?

options.text: One-timer if the pass lands on my blade
options.feedback: Right. The pass has to be crisp and to your stick — then the one-timer beats the goalie every time.

options.text: Settle the puck, turn, shoot — every time
options.feedback: Safer but slower. You give the goalie time to square up. The one-timer is the better read if possible.

options.text: Always one-time it no matter what
options.feedback: One-timing a bad pass is how you fan and lose possession. Read the pass first.

options.text: Pass it back — too risky
options.feedback: You're in the slot off a cross-seam. Give up that chance only if the defender is on you already.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your line has been cycling low for 15 seconds. The defenders are collapsed deep. The point man is open at the top.
U15 / Bantam.why: A cycle doesn't score from the cycle — it scores when you release to the point or seam while the D is deep and tired. Timing the release before the D resets is the whole point.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Cycle sets up the release. Don't forget to release.

question.prompt: D's are collapsed. What's the release?

options.text: Pass to RD at the point for a shot
options.feedback: That's the release. Low-to-high before the D resets. Bodies at the net screen and tip.

options.text: Keep cycling — stay patient
options.feedback: Patience is good but over-patience means the D re-establishes and your chance disappears.

options.text: Try another wrap-around
options.feedback: Solo plays when a point release is open wastes the cycle's whole setup.

options.text: Pass to the LW for a low shot
options.feedback: Can work but the point shot is more dangerous with the D down low.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D is retrieving a puck behind your net. The opposing forecheck is sending two deep. You're the low forward.
U15 / Bantam.why: The low F's job on a retrieval is to give the D a safe outlet option — usually up the strong-side boards — and also to be ready for a reverse. Don't skate away from your D.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D on a retrieval? Be their outlet, not their headache.

question.prompt: Where do you set up as the low F?

feedback.correct: Strong-side boards. Your D can rim it to you or hit you with a quick outlet.
feedback.partial: Left corner is close but lower — your D can't rim safely below the hash. Boards is better.
feedback.wrong: You've left your D with no outlet. The forecheck forces a turnover in the worst spot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On a 1-2-2 forecheck. F1 is pressuring the D. You're F2 on the strong side. The puck is being retrieved by their strong-side D.
U15 / Bantam.why: F2 in a 1-2-2 sits near the top of the circle on the strong side. You're ready to jump the strong-side outlet (winger on the boards) or drop back if the D reverses.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-2-2 F2? Top of the strong-side circle.

question.prompt: Where do you sit as F2 in a 1-2-2?

feedback.correct: Top of the strong-side circle. You jump the outlet or back off to the neutral zone.
feedback.partial: Close but not quite — top of the circle is the sweet spot for F2 reads.
feedback.wrong: You're out of position for 1-2-2. F2 stays strong-side, near the top of the circle.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Their team has just gained their O-zone with a 5-man unit. Your team runs 1-2-2. Where's F2?
U15 / Bantam.why: F2 in a 1-2-2 pressures the first pass — usually the strong-side wall. Deny the up.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F2 kills the wall, lets F1 finish.

question.prompt: F2 position?

feedback.correct: Yes — kill the wall option as F1 pressures.
feedback.partial: Close — but strong-side wall is the textbook.
feedback.wrong: That's F3's job. You're F2.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D has an outlet and a winger is streaking through centre. What's the best pass?
U15 / Bantam.why: Stretch passes to moving wings catch the D flat-footed and create partial breakaways.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Stretch when the weak-side W has speed.

question.prompt: Your W flying through centre. D's best pass?

options.text: Stretch to the moving W.
options.feedback: Yes — speed-on-speed, catches their D cold.

options.text: Safe up to a stationary winger.
options.feedback: Safe but slower — you miss the stretch.

options.text: Carry it out himself.
options.feedback: If the stretch is there, use it.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're the weak-side D. Puck is on the strong-side half-wall. Their F is trying to chip it past your partner.
U15 / Bantam.why: Weak-side D reads the chip attempt — if their F is winning the battle, hold the line. If your D has it, pinch.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Pinch only when your partner has control.

question.prompt: Your partner's battling on the wall. You…

options.text: Hold the line, ready to collapse if they lose it.
options.feedback: Yes — don't get beaten both sides.

options.text: Pinch aggressively to help.
options.feedback: If they win, you're caught. Hold.

options.text: Skate to the slot.
options.feedback: Too far from the play.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D has the puck in the neutral zone. F1 on the other team is pressuring. You're the centre.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a regroup trigger, C curls back to support low and gives the D a second outlet.
U15 / Bantam.tip: C curls back to support the D.

question.prompt: Where does C support on the regroup?

feedback.correct: Yes — low support for the D, gives a second outlet.
feedback.partial: That supports but doesn't create a clean outlet. Low is better.
feedback.wrong: Too deep — D can't hit you.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team cycles high-to-low. You're the high-slot F. Where do you move?
U15 / Bantam.why: On a high cycle, the high-slot F drops toward the cycler to give a one-timer option.
U15 / Bantam.tip: High cycle = drop to support, not hover.

question.prompt: High F's move on the cycle?

feedback.correct: Yes — drop into the shooting lane for a one-timer.
feedback.partial: Hovering doesn't help the cycle.
feedback.wrong: Too high. Drop lower.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's PP runs an umbrella. First pass comes to the high-point F. What's the read?
U15 / Bantam.why: Umbrella PP: high F scans left-wall and right-wall options. Reads the PK rotation.
U15 / Bantam.tip: High F is the distributor, not the shooter (usually).

question.prompt: Umbrella, puck on high-F. Read?

options.text: Scan wall-to-wall, pick the open half-wall.
options.feedback: Yes — high F distributes, reads PK.

options.text: Shoot every time.
options.feedback: Only when it's truly open. Distribute first.

options.text: Pass to net-front.
options.feedback: Dangerous — often covered. Half-wall first.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A forward is about to pick up a puck along the wall. You're closing.
U15 / Bantam.why: Finishing the check legally takes them out of the play and establishes the wall.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Angle in, finish through — not at — them.

question.prompt: They're reaching for the puck on the wall. You…

options.text: Finish the check legally — shoulder-to-body.
options.feedback: Yes — take them out, reclaim possession.

options.text: Hit their head.
options.feedback: Major penalty. You just lost the team 5 minutes.

options.text: Coast through the contact.
options.feedback: Soft — you left a free puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D backing off on a rush. The puck-carrier slows down at your blue line.
U15 / Bantam.why: When a carrier slows, close the gap hard — don't give them time to survey.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Slow carrier → aggressive gap.

question.prompt: Carrier slowed down at your line. You…

options.text: Close the gap hard — stick on puck.
options.feedback: Yes — a slow carrier is inviting pressure.

options.text: Stay back and wait for the rush.
options.feedback: That gives them time to set up plays.

options.text: Retreat to the hashmarks.
options.feedback: Too passive.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck goes into your team's strong-side corner. You're F3 (C usually).
U15 / Bantam.why: F3 in support means covering the high slot as an outlet, not drifting to the point.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F3 = high slot anchor.

question.prompt: Where's F3?

feedback.correct: Yes — high slot, available for the up.
feedback.partial: You drifted. Anchor at high slot.
feedback.wrong: That's not F3's job.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A pass is coming to you in transition. Defender behind you is closing.
U15 / Bantam.why: Pre-scanning before reception gives you a plan — turn up-ice without hesitation.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Scan 3 times: before, during, after reception.

question.prompt: Pass coming. Defender behind. You…

options.text: Pre-scan, receive open, turn up-ice in one motion.
options.feedback: Yes — pros-in-training. Pre-scan wins possession.

options.text: Focus on the puck only.
options.feedback: You'll catch it but get hit immediately.

options.text: Call for a different pass.
options.feedback: Too late — the pass is on its way.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Puck goes up the strong side. Weak-side D is the nearest support. What's the rotation?
U15 / Bantam.why: D-pair rotation: one pressures, one supports. Never both pressure, never both support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: One on the puck, one reading the ice.

question.prompt: Weak-side D's job here?

options.text: Read the play, slide middle, cover the slot.
options.feedback: Yes — D pair always has one reading, one pressuring.

options.text: Pinch on the weak side.
options.feedback: Now both Ds are out of position.

options.text: Skate back to your own net.
options.feedback: Too deep — you need to support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Puck is below the goal line. Your team has numbers. How do you layer the attack?
U15 / Bantam.why: Layered attack = low cycle + mid-slot support + weak-side cover. Three threats, not one.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Three layers beat any defense.

question.prompt: You're the mid-layer. Where do you set?

feedback.correct: Yes — mid-slot support, ready for the shot.
feedback.partial: Too low. Mid-slot is the layer.
feedback.wrong: You're on the low layer — duplicate coverage.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You carry the puck at a wall with no entry open. A teammate is trailing.
U15 / Bantam.why: Delay behind the net creates time for the trailer to arrive — called a delay game.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Delay = buy time for the trailer.

question.prompt: No entry, trailer coming. You…

options.text: Delay behind the net, drop to the trailer.
options.feedback: Yes — classic delay game.

options.text: Force the entry anyway.
options.feedback: Turnover and transition against.

options.text: Dump and change.
options.feedback: If delay is available, use it. Dump is a last resort.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You just cleared a puck on the PK, shorthanded. Which clear is better?
U15 / Bantam.why: On a PK clear, rim it hard if PK time is ticking; ice it only if you can't reach the boards clean.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Rim > ice on the PK — keeps pressure off.

question.prompt: PK clear — best option?

options.text: Rim it hard around the boards to the far corner.
options.feedback: Yes — rim gets it deep without icing.

options.text: Dump it up the middle of the ice.
options.feedback: Picked off — PP goal.

options.text: Ice it.
options.feedback: Only if you can't rim. Icing brings the draw back in.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Their winger enters your zone on the weak side. Your strong-side D is caught up.
U15 / Bantam.why: Strong-side F3 drops low to pick up the winger — a rotation, not a scramble.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D in trouble → F3 drops low.

question.prompt: D out of position. F3…

options.text: Drops low to cover the puck-carrier.
options.feedback: Yes — rotation, not panic.

options.text: Stays high at the point.
options.feedback: The winger has a clear lane.

options.text: Skates to the opposite corner.
options.feedback: Unrelated. Cover the puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You receive a pass at the top of the circles with a window to shoot.
U15 / Bantam.why: Quick releases — before the goalie can square — are how U15+ scorers actually score.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Don't stickhandle. Release.

question.prompt: Pass received, window open. You…

options.text: One-timer or snap shot immediately.
options.feedback: Yes — before the goalie squares up.

options.text: Stickhandle to get a better angle.
options.feedback: You just gave the goalie time.

options.text: Walk in toward the net.
options.feedback: Sometimes. But release speed is higher value.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You lose a puck battle along the wall. What do you do next?
U15 / Bantam.why: Second-effort plays — not giving up — are the difference between U15 and rep-level compete.
U15 / Bantam.tip: First loss is normal. Second loss is a habit.

question.prompt: You lost the battle. Now you…

options.text: Re-engage immediately — second effort, stick-lift, disrupt.
options.feedback: Yes — pros make second efforts.

options.text: Glide back defensively.
options.feedback: You just gave up on the play.

options.text: Call for help.
options.feedback: Only if you can't second-effort.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A pass is coming off the wall to you on the off side.
U15 / Bantam.why: Receiving on your off side requires pre-scanning and quick hands — not turning your back.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Receive open, don't turn your back.

question.prompt: Off-side wall pass. You…

options.text: Open my body up-ice, receive with forehand, scan.
options.feedback: Yes — pro habit. Body open = vision open.

options.text: Turn my back to the defender.
options.feedback: Now you can't see anything. Bad habit.

options.text: Catch it on the backhand.
options.feedback: Workable, but forehand-open is cleaner.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The other team just reset through their D. Where are you looking next?
U15 / Bantam.why: Reads after the reset — the next pass option — are what the best U15s see two steps ahead.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Anticipate, don't chase.

question.prompt: After reset. You scan for…

options.text: The next pass option — usually the weak-side wing or a backdoor.
options.feedback: Yes — anticipate the pattern.

options.text: The D's shot.
options.feedback: Too late — react to the next puck, not this one.

options.text: The puck only.
options.feedback: You'll be a step behind.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your teammate's check is in the slot unmarked. What do you do?
U15 / Bantam.why: Communication wins games. Call it out — your teammate covers.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Talk is the cheapest edge in hockey.

question.prompt: Their F is open in the slot and nobody's on them. You…

options.text: Yell to your teammate — 'slot, slot!' — they cover.
options.feedback: Yes — talking saves goals.

options.text: Skate over and cover yourself.
options.feedback: Now you've left your check.

options.text: Assume someone's on it.
options.feedback: That's how open-net goals happen.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your coach shows video of the opposing team's breakout. Their D always goes behind the net and passes to the winger on the left wall. How do you use this in the game?
opts: Position your forecheck to take away the left wall pass and force the D into an unfamiliar play.
Ignore the scouting since video from a previous game doesn't predict what they'll do.
Tell your teammates about the tendency but don't adjust your forecheck system based on instead.
Watch for it in the game but don't change anything until you see them actually do it multiple.
U18 / Midget.why: Pre-scout information is most valuable when it directly changes your positioning. Taking away their go-to play forces them into uncomfortable decisions.
U18 / Midget.tip: Scouting shows a tendency? Position to take it away. Force them into Plan B.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team is trying to carry the puck through the neutral zone. Your team is in a 1-2-2. You're one of the two forwards at the red line. What's your job?
opts: Clog the middle of the ice and force the puck carrier to the outside where they have less options.
Chase the puck carrier aggressively regardless of where they are since speed solves every.
Back up to your own blue line and let them enter the zone since the neutral zone doesn't instead.
Move to the same side as the other forward at the red line so you can double-team the puck.
U18 / Midget.why: The two forwards in a 1-2-2 clog the middle. Forcing the carrier outside reduces their options — wide entries are lower percentage than middle-lane attacks.
U18 / Midget.tip: 1-2-2 middle forward: clog the center. Force them wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's game day against the first-place team. Your team has lost to them twice this season. As a leader, how do you approach the pre-game room?
opts: Bring calm confidence — acknowledge the challenge but focus on specific things your team does well that can win this game tonight against them.
Give an emotional speech about how important this game is and how everyone needs to play their best game of the season or the season is.
Don't say anything since the coach handles pre-game talks and players should just focus on their own individual preparation without at this level.
Talk about how the other team isn't that good and you should have beaten them both times since overconfidence will motivate your team to play.
U18 / Midget.why: Pre-game leadership is about calm confidence. Acknowledge the challenge, focus on specific strengths, and set a tone of preparation, not panic.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big game? Calm confidence. Specific strengths. Set the tone.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D has the puck in the corner and the forecheck is closing. You're the winger. Where do you need to be?
opts: Up the wall on your side — be the outlet and give the D a clear, safe passing option up the ice.
In the slot area in front of the net since that's always the most dangerous area to receive.
At the far blue line waiting for a stretch pass since that creates the biggest transition.
Behind the D to provide close physical support in case they need help winning the board.
U18 / Midget.why: The winger's wall position is the primary breakout outlet. Getting up the wall fast and being ready for the pass is your most important job on the breakout.
U18 / Midget.tip: D in the corner? Get up the wall. Be the outlet. Fast.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team leads by one with five minutes left. The other team is pressing. What's the team-wide approach?
opts: Smart clears, no turnovers in the neutral zone, and take what the defense gives you without risk.
Turtle completely — dump the puck out every time you touch it and don't try to create any.
Play the exact same way you've played all game since changing your approach shows you're scared.
Get ultra-aggressive and try to score another goal to put the game away with an insurance tally.
U18 / Midget.why: Protecting a lead late means smart hockey — clean exits, no neutral zone turnovers, and taking what's given without unnecessary risk. It's not turtling, it's managing.
U18 / Midget.tip: Lead with five left? Smart clears. No turnovers. Take what they give you.

U18 / Midget.sit: You notice the opposing D keeps pinching aggressively on your breakouts. There's open ice behind them every time they pinch. How do you exploit it?
opts: Use stretch passes behind the pinching D to create breakaway chances for your fastest forwards.
Ignore it since taking advantage of other teams' mistakes is unsportsmanlike and not how you play.
Tell the other team's coach about their D's pinching habit so the game stays fair for both.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing and exploiting defensive mistakes is not unsportsmanlike hockey IQ. Stretch passes behind an over-aggressive D create the highest-danger chances.
U18 / Midget.tip: Their D pinching too much? Stretch behind them. Exploit the mistake.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team is running a neutral zone trap. Your team keeps turning the puck over trying to carry through it. What's the adjustment?
opts: Use D-to-D movement and east-west passes to pull the trap apart before trying to carry through it.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Dump the puck every single time and never try to carry through any trap formation.
Have your best player try to beat the trap alone since they're the only one skilled enough.
U18 / Midget.why: Traps are beaten by east-west movement. D-to-D passes and lateral puck movement pull the trap apart and create lanes.
U18 / Midget.tip: Facing a trap? East-west. D-to-D. Pull it apart. Don't skate into it.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D is behind the net with the puck. The forecheck has overloaded the strong side — both forecheckers went to the same side. What breakout option does this create?
opts: Reverse the puck to the weak side where the ice is wide open since the forecheck left it vacant.
Pass up the strong side anyway since that's the standard breakout and you should stick to.
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for the forecheck to back off before making any breakout.
Shoot the puck off the glass on the strong side to get it past the forecheckers and out.
U18 / Midget.why: An overloaded forecheck leaves the weak side open. Reversing the puck behind the net exploits their aggressiveness and creates a clean exit.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forecheck overloaded one side? Reverse. The weak side is open.

U18 / Midget.sit: You've taken two penalties this game. The coach benched you for a shift as a message. How do you respond when you go back out?
opts: Play disciplined but don't change your compete level — show the coach you can be physical and smart.
Play the same way regardless of the game score or time.
Play the exact same way that got you the penalties since you were just unlucky with the officials.
Play the same way regardless of the game score or time.
U18 / Midget.why: The right response to being benched for penalties is disciplined compete. Physical and smart. Not timid, not reckless — the balance in between.
U18 / Midget.tip: Benched for penalties? Come back disciplined but competitive. Show you learned.

U18 / Midget.sit: You have the puck at the top of the left circle. The goalie is set and square to you. There's traffic in front. What's the best shot selection?
opts: Low shot through traffic — the goalie can't see through the screen and a low shot creates a rebound chance off pads or deflects off traffic in front.
High shot over the traffic since going over the screen eliminates the chance of the puck being blocked by bodies and gives you a clear look at the net.
Hold the shot and try to walk to the middle of the ice for a better angle since the left circle isn't a high enough percentage area to shoot.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
U18 / Midget.why: With traffic and a set goalie, low through traffic creates rebounds and deflections. The screen makes the goalie's job harder. High shots over the screen are often caught.
U18 / Midget.tip: Traffic in front, goalie set? Shoot low through it. Screen + rebound = goals.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has lost four games in a row. Morale is low. Players are pointing fingers. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Refocus the team on process — work ethic, preparation, and executing the details in the next game.
Call out the specific players who have been making the most mistakes so they can be held accountable.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Have a team party to boost morale since the problem is clearly that the team isn't having enough.
U18 / Midget.why: Losing streaks are broken by refocusing on process, not results. Work ethic, preparation, and details. Teams that focus on the next game instead of the last four recover.
U18 / Midget.tip: Losing streak? Process over results. Work ethic. Preparation. Next game.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're entering the offensive zone with possession. The D is backing up and giving you the blue line. What type of entry should you make?
opts: Carry the puck in with speed and possession since the D is conceding the blue line to you.
Dump it in hard to the corner since every zone entry should be a dump to protect against turnovers.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your entire team to catch up before you enter the zone.
Pass the puck backward to your D and let them carry it in since D entries are more effective.
U18 / Midget.why: When the D gives you the blue line, carry the puck in. A possession entry against a retreating D creates more offense than a dump-in.
U18 / Midget.tip: D backing up? Carry it in. Possession entry. Don't give up the puck for free.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're a forward sprinting up ice on a stretch play. Your D has the puck. The opposing D is flat-footed at the blue line. When should the stretch pass arrive?
opts: Timed so you receive it in stride at the blue line — not too early to be offside, not too late to stop.
As early as possible so you have maximum time with the puck before you reach the defensive zone.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
It doesn't matter when it arrives as long as it's in the general area and you can skate to go get.
U18 / Midget.why: Stretch pass timing is critical. In stride at the blue line maximizes speed and minimizes offside risk. Too early forces offside; too late kills momentum.
U18 / Midget.tip: Stretch pass: in stride at the blue line. Timing is everything.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one goal entering the third period. The other team plays a tight defensive system. What's the overall approach?
opts: Increase offensive zone time through sustained pressure and cycling — force turnovers in their zone to create chances rather than rushing the attack up the ice.
Pull the goalie immediately at the start of the third to get an extra attacker for the full twenty minutes since you need to score and this maximizes your.
Keep playing the same way a lucky bounce since changing your approach when you're down usually makes things worse and creates more turnovers.
Play ultra-aggressive with no regard for defense since you need a goal and the best defense when trailing is a good offense that puts constant pressure on.
U18 / Midget.why: Trailing by one requires sustained offensive zone pressure, not desperation. Cycling, winning puck battles, and creating chances through possession is more effective than rush attacks against a tight system.
U18 / Midget.tip: Down one in the third? Sustained pressure. Cycle. Win battles. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: You've noticed the opposing center always cheats to the right on faceoffs. They win draws to their forehand consistently. You're taking the next draw against them. What do you do?
opts: Counter their forehand by positioning your stick to block the right-side win and use your body to gain inside leverage for the draw at the dot against them.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Let the opposing center win the draw and focus on the puck battle after since faceoffs aren't important enough to spend time strategizing about before.
Ask your winger to line up where the puck usually goes on their forehand win so at least you'll have the puck battle covered if they win it to their preferred.
U18 / Midget.why: Reading an opponent's faceoff tendency and countering it is a competitive advantage. Blocking their forehand win and using body leverage directly addresses their strength.
U18 / Midget.tip: Know their faceoff tendency? Counter it. Block the forehand. Use leverage.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your coach wants to run a 1-3-1 trap in the neutral zone to slow down a fast team. You're the center in the middle of the three. What's your specific role?
opts: Control the middle of the ice — take away the center lane and force everything wide where your D can close on the puck carrier as they approach your blue line.
Chase the puck carrier wherever they go since the center in the 1-3-1 should always be closest to the puck regardless of where it is on the ice at all.
Stand at center ice and don't move since the 1-3-1 is a passive system where everyone stays in their spot and waits for the opponent to make a mistake first.
Drop back to the blue line and play as a third defenseman since the neutral zone isn't where the trap works and the real defense starts at your own blue line.
U18 / Midget.why: The center in a 1-3-1 controls the middle lane. You're the key to the trap — take away the center and funnel everything wide where the structure has advantages.
U18 / Midget.tip: Center in the 1-3-1: own the middle lane. Force everything wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team just iced the puck. You can't change lines. The other team sends out their top line for the offensive zone faceoff. What do you focus on?
opts: Win the draw and get a clean zone exit on the first touch since a tired team on an icing can not afford to spend time in the D-zone against a fresh top line.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Don't worry about it since icings happen and whatever happens on the faceoff is fine since you can't control whether you're tired or not on this particular.
Argue with the referee about the icing call to try to get a few extra seconds of rest before the faceoff is taken since every second of rest counts when you're.
U18 / Midget.why: After an icing, the priority is a clean zone exit on the first touch. Your team is tired and can't change — spending time in the D-zone against their fresh top line is dangerous.
U18 / Midget.tip: Icing, tired team: win the draw. Get out clean. First touch matters most.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has a new player who's talented but hasn't bought into the team system. They freelance on the ice and it's creating problems. As a veteran leader, what do you do?
opts: Have a private conversation explaining how the system works and why buying in makes everyone better including them personally — welcome them and show them the culture.
Ignore it since they'll either figure it out or the coach will bench them and it's not your responsibility to integrate new players into the team culture and system.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Tell the coach to bench them until they agree to play within the system since that's the most effective way to force compliance from talented players who won't.
U18 / Midget.why: Integrating new players through private conversation is leadership. Welcoming them, explaining the culture, and showing why the system helps everyone — including them — builds buy-in.
U18 / Midget.tip: New player not buying in? Private conversation. Welcome them. Show the 'why.'

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in a tight game. The coach calls a timeout after a goal against. You have 30 seconds. What's the most productive way to use this time as a player?
opts: Listen to the coach, catch your breath, and focus on one specific adjustment for the next shift to execute — don't try to process everything at once or change too many things.
Drink water and zone out since the timeout is for the coach to talk and you just need physical rest not tactical information during these short breaks in the game flow.
Talk over the coach to share what you've been seeing on the ice since your perspective is more important than theirs and the team needs to hear your observations.
Use the time to argue about the goal that was just scored and assign blame for the breakdown since accountability during timeouts prevents the same mistake from happening.
U18 / Midget.why: Timeout productivity: listen, breathe, focus on one adjustment. Trying to fix everything at once during a 30-second break creates confusion. One focus, one fix.
U18 / Midget.tip: Timeout: listen, breathe, one focus. Don't overload. One adjustment at a time.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one with 90 seconds left. You have a faceoff in the offensive zone. The coach signals to pull the goalie for the extra attacker. What's the team's approach?
opts: Set up the 6-on-5 with the extra player at the net front or point — use the overload to create a shot through traffic or a cross-ice play with the numerical advantage.
Panic and shoot the puck every time you touch it since you need a goal and shot volume is the only thing that matters when the goalie is pulled and time is.
Have the extra player roam around the zone without a specific assignment since having an extra body creates natural chaos that the defending team can not handle.
Don't pull the goalie since an empty-net goal against ends the game and the risk outweighs the reward of having six attackers when there's still 90 seconds left to.
U18 / Midget.why: 6-on-5 requires structure. The extra player takes a specific position — net front or point — to create the overload. Random movement wastes the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: 6-on-5: extra player takes a specific spot. Net front or point. Structure wins.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your teammate turns the puck over in the neutral zone. The other team has a 3-on-2 rush coming at your defense. You're the forward closest to the play. What's your job?
opts: Backcheck hard and take away the trailing player — reduce the 3-on-2 to a 2-on-2 by picking up the third attacker coming late with speed from behind the play.
Chase the puck carrier since taking away the puck is the most direct way to stop the rush and your speed should be enough to catch them from behind on this play.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Stay in the neutral zone in case the rush fails and the puck comes back since being in position for a counter-attack is more valuable than backchecking.
U18 / Midget.why: Backchecking to take away the trailer turns a 3-on-2 into a 2-on-2. Your D handle two — you eliminate the third. That's the highest-value backcheck play.
U18 / Midget.tip: 3-on-2 against? Backcheck and take the trailer. Turn it into 2-on-2.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're F1 on the backcheck. Your team just turned the puck over. The opposing D has the puck and their center is open in the middle. What's your assignment?
opts: Take the center away — get in the middle lane and prevent the pass to the most dangerous player in their breakout since the center pass starts their transition cleanly.
Chase the D who has the puck since taking the puck away directly is always better than covering a passing lane and your speed should be enough to catch them from here.
Skate to the far winger's side since they're the most open player on the ice and a long pass to the winger creates a bigger offensive threat than the center in the middle.
Go to the bench for a change since backchecking F1 should be a fresh player and you've been on the ice too long to be effective in a footrace with the opposing center.
U18 / Midget.why: F1 backcheck takes away the center. The middle lane is the most dangerous breakout pass — eliminating it forces the D to use the boards, which is slower and easier to defend.
U18 / Midget.tip: F1 backcheck: take the center away. Eliminate the middle lane.

U18 / Midget.sit: In the defensive zone, the opposing team passes the puck from the corner to the point. Your winger was covering the point but got caught low. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Rotate up to cover the point man since your winger can't get there — communicate so the coverage gap is filled before the point player can shoot or pass it down to the slot.
Stay in the slot since that's always the center's responsibility and if the winger got caught low that's their mistake to fix and not your responsibility to cover for them.
Follow the puck to the corner since the puck is what scores goals and being near the puck is always the right decision regardless of where your coverage assignment is.
Don't rotate and let the point man have the shot since shots from the point are low percentage and your goalie can handle a point shot without any defensive help from you.
U18 / Midget.why: Defensive coverage is fluid. When a winger gets caught, the center rotates to fill the gap. Communication prevents confusion. Rigid assignments without rotation create open shooters.
U18 / Midget.tip: Winger caught low? Rotate to the point. Fill the gap. Communicate.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has a PP offensive zone faceoff with a set play designed: draw back to the point for a one-timer. The PK knows this play — they've seen it. What adjustment do you make to the play?
opts: Run the same draw but add a secondary option — if the PK overplays the point shot, have the center fake the draw-back and go directly to the net with the puck instead.
Run the exact same play without any adjustment since execution beats scouting and if you win the draw cleanly the PK can't stop a clean point shot regardless of what.
Abandon the set play entirely and just try to win the draw forward aggressively for a quick shot since set plays don't work when the other team knows what you're instead.
Skip the faceoff entirely — don't even try to win it and focus all your energy on defending the PK's counter-attack since their scouting has made your PP faceoff.
U18 / Midget.why: Adding a secondary option to a scouted play keeps the PK guessing. If they overplay the point shot, the center going direct to the net exploits their adjustment.
U18 / Midget.tip: PK knows your play? Add a secondary option. Keep them guessing.

U18 / Midget.sit: There are 10 seconds left in the second period. Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. What's the smart play?
opts: Get the puck deep into the offensive zone and create a scoring chance — a late-period goal changes momentum going into the intermission break between periods.
Hold the puck at center ice and let the clock run out since there isn't enough time to create anything meaningful and risking a turnover at the buzzer could.
Dump the puck in and go for a change since the period is essentially over and there's no point in trying to create a scoring chance with only 10 seconds.
Shoot the puck on net from center ice since any shot from anywhere is a good shot when time is running out and you might score a lucky goal from long distance at.
U18 / Midget.why: 10 seconds is enough to get a shot. Getting the puck deep and creating a late-period chance can shift momentum. Wasting the opportunity is a missed chance.
U18 / Midget.tip: 10 seconds left with possession? Get it deep. Create a chance. Momentum matters.

U18 / Midget.concept: Supporting the rush through the NZ
U18 / Midget.sit: Your D is carrying the puck through the neutral zone. You're a forward and there's open ice on the right side. But another forward is already filling that lane. Where do you go?
opts: Fill the middle or left lane — don't double up in the same lane as your teammate and give the rush three distinct options across the ice to attack with maximum width and numbers.
Go to the right side anyway since being close to the puck carrier is always better than being in a different lane and double coverage on one side creates an overload the D.
Stop skating and let the rush develop without you since three forwards in the rush is too many and one forward should always stay back as a safety valve in case of a after that.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
U18 / Midget.why: Rush support requires lane discipline. Three distinct lanes (left, middle, right) give the puck carrier maximum options. Doubling up in a lane wastes a player.
U18 / Midget.tip: Teammate already in a lane? Take a different one. Three lanes, three options.

U18 / Midget.concept: Penalty kill strategy late in game
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one with four minutes left and takes a penalty. The opposing PP is strong. What's the PK strategy?
opts: Ultra-disciplined box — collapse to the net, block shots, and clear every puck. Deny the high-danger shots and force them to score from the perimeter if they can at all.
Aggressive PK — pressure the puck carrier at every opportunity since taking the puck away ends the power play instantly and that's better than sitting back and.
Play a normal PK without any adjustment for the game situation since your PK should be the same regardless of the score or the time remaining on the clock in the game.
Send a player after the PP puck carrier every time to try to force a shorthanded chance since the best PK is one that scores a goal and that would seal the game.
U18 / Midget.why: Late-game PK with a lead: ultra-disciplined. Collapse, block, clear. The goal is to survive the two minutes — not create offense. Force them to score from low-percentage areas.
U18 / Midget.tip: Leading late, PK? Ultra-disciplined. Collapse. Block. Clear. Survive the two minutes.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your PP has been in the zone for a full minute. The PK is tired but still in structure. You have the puck at the point. The PK is sagging to block shots. What's the next-level read?
opts: Walk the puck lower to change the shooting angle and draw the PK out of their collapsing position, creating a lane for a pass to the slot or a higher-percentage shot from a new spot.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Pass the puck back and restart the PP setup since the current setup isn't working and cycling back will give the PK a chance to reset and you can try a completely new approach this.
Hold the puck at the point for as long as possible since the PK is tired and if you just wait long enough they'll eventually break down and give you a wide open look at the net.
U18 / Midget.why: Walking the puck lower changes the geometry. A sagging PK is beaten by changing the angle — it draws them out and creates passing lanes that didn't exist from the point.
U18 / Midget.tip: PK sagging at the point? Walk lower. Change the angle. Draw them out.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your forward has the puck in the neutral zone but is getting pressured. You're the D behind them. They're about to lose the puck. What do you do?
opts: Get close enough to be an outlet for a drop pass — give them an escape option before the pressure forces a turnover in a dangerous area of the neutral zone on the play.
Stay back at your own blue line since if they lose the puck you need to be in defensive position already and moving forward would leave the back end completely.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
U18 / Midget.why: Being an outlet for a drop pass when your forward is pressured prevents the turnover. Close support gives them an escape option in a dangerous area.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forward under pressure in NZ? Get close. Be the drop-pass outlet.

U18 / Midget.sit: The other team has been forechecking aggressively for the last three shifts. Your team has been pinned in the D-zone. You finally get the puck. What's the priority?
opts: Make a quick, smart pass to exit the zone — don't try to be a hero with the puck since one clean exit can change the momentum of the entire game for your team.
Hold the puck and try to deke through the forecheck since after three shifts of pressure your team needs a highlight play to change the momentum and fire up the bench.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Pass the puck back to the goalie and have them freeze it for a faceoff every single time since faceoffs are the only reliable way to reset when you're under after that.
U18 / Midget.why: After sustained pressure, one clean exit changes everything. Quick, smart pass — don't try to be a hero. Clean exits relieve pressure and shift momentum.
U18 / Midget.tip: Pinned for three shifts? One clean exit changes everything. Quick, smart pass.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in 3-on-3 overtime. You have possession. There's a ton of open ice. What's the strategic approach?
opts: Control the puck and wait for the right moment to attack — don't force anything since a turnover in 3-on-3 creates a high-danger chance the other way with open ice everywhere.
Attack immediately every time you get the puck since 3-on-3 is about scoring and hesitation gives the defense time to set up and take away the open ice that overtime creates.
Pass the puck between your three players at center ice without attacking since the other team will eventually make a mistake and give you a perfect 2-on-0 or breakaway.
Dump the puck in every time and forecheck since dump-and-chase works in 3-on-3 just like it works in 5-on-5 and it's the safest way to generate offensive zone time in after that.
U18 / Midget.why: 3-on-3 is about controlled aggression. Control the puck, read the play, and attack when the opening appears. Forcing the play creates turnovers — and turnovers in OT are breakaways.
U18 / Midget.tip: 3-on-3 OT: controlled aggression. Wait for the moment. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing coach calls a timeout. When play resumes, you notice they've changed their forecheck from a 1-2-2 to a more aggressive 2-1-2. What do you do with this recognition?
opts: Adjust your breakout immediately — use the weak side and quick D-to-D passes since a 2-1-2 overloads one side and leaves the other side more open for a clean breakout exit.
Ignore it since trying to identify and adjust to mid-game tactical changes is overthinking and you should just run your standard breakout regardless of what they're.
Panic and call your own timeout since the opponent has changed tactics and your team needs time to discuss and plan a completely new strategy to counter their aggressive.
Slow the game down and hold the puck in your own zone to waste time and prevent the new forecheck from being effective since if you don't have the puck they can't.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing a mid-game tactical change and adjusting immediately is elite hockey IQ. A 2-1-2 overloads one side — the weak side is the outlet.
U18 / Midget.tip: They changed their forecheck after a timeout? Recognize it. Adjust. Use the weak side.

U18 / Midget.concept: NZ faceoff with game on the line
U18 / Midget.sit: Neutral zone faceoff with 10 seconds left. Game is tied. Your coach wants you to win the draw and get a shot. The opposing center is one of the best faceoff players in the league. What's your approach?
opts: Study their stance and hand position — adjust your technique to counter their strength. Have your wingers ready to jump on a loose puck if the draw isn't won cleanly by either side.
Take the draw the same way you always do since adjusting your technique at the last second is more likely to cause you to lose the draw than to win it against a strong faceoff.
Let them win the draw and focus on defense since with 10 seconds left it's better to play for overtime than to risk a turnover that gives them a chance to win it in regulation.
Cheat on the draw and try to get the puck before it hits the ice since the referee probably won't kick you out with 10 seconds left and the advantage of getting the puck first.
U18 / Midget.why: Big faceoffs require preparation and support. Study the opposing center, counter their technique, and have your wingers ready for a loose puck. It's not just the center's battle.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big faceoff: study them. Counter their technique. Wingers ready for the loose puck.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's the championship game. Pre-game nerves are high. Some teammates look tight. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Keep the room loose but focused — remind the team to play their game, trust the preparation, and enjoy the moment since this is what you've worked for the entire season.
Give an intense, emotional speech about how this is the most important game of everyone's life and anyone who doesn't perform at their best will regret it for the rest.
Don't say anything since every player handles pressure differently and any pre-game talk might make the nervous players feel worse and the confident players feel.
Tell everyone to pretend it's a regular game since acknowledging the significance of the championship will add to the pressure and the team should treat it like any.
U18 / Midget.why: Championship leadership: loose but focused. Trust the preparation, play your game, enjoy the moment. Over-intensity creates tightness. Dismissing the moment loses its power.
U18 / Midget.tip: Championship game? Loose but focused. Trust preparation. Enjoy the moment.

U18 / Midget.concept: Managing emotions after a bad hit
U18 / Midget.sit: An opponent delivers a questionable hit on your linemate. No penalty is called. Your linemate is shaken up. The bench is furious. What do you do on the next shift?
opts: Play hard and physical but within the rules — channel the emotion into compete level, not retaliation, since a penalty hurts your team more than it helps your linemate.
Go after the player who delivered the hit and fight them since your teammate needs to know you have their back and physical retaliation is the only language some players.
Take a run at the nearest opposing player to send a message to the other team that they can't get away with hits like that without consequences from your team in the next shift.
Ignore it completely and play passively since getting emotional after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants and the best response is to pretend nothing happened at all.
U18 / Midget.why: Channeling emotion into compete, not retaliation, is elite emotional management. A penalty after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants. Play harder, not dirtier.
U18 / Midget.tip: Bad hit on a teammate? Channel the emotion into compete. Don't give them the penalty.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your PK just killed a big penalty and your bench is energized. You're the first player on the ice after the penalty expires. What do you do with the momentum?
opts: Channel the energy into a strong, aggressive shift — the PK momentum carry-over often leads to scoring chances if the team capitalizes on the emotional shift immediately.
Calm down and play conservatively since the PK was tiring and the team needs to recover their energy before pushing the pace again so you should ease into the next.
Call a timeout to celebrate the kill and let the momentum sink in since stopping play after a big PK allows the team to savor the moment and build even more emotional.
Ignore the momentum since hockey is played in individual shifts and what happened on the PK has no bearing on what happens in the next five-on-five shift for your.
U18 / Midget.why: PK momentum is real. The first shift after a big kill is often the best chance to capitalize — the team is energized and the opponent is frustrated their PP failed.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big PK? First shift after: channel the energy. Momentum is real. Capitalize.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're a D with the puck behind your net. The forecheck is closing hard from both sides. You have maybe one second. Your outlets are: winger on the wall (covered with pressure coming), center at the hashmarks (partially open), or glass. What do you choose?
opts: Quick pass to the center at the hashmarks since it's partially open and the center can handle some pressure — it starts the breakout while the winger option risks a board turnover.
Pass to the winger on the wall even though they're covered since at U18 your winger should be skilled enough to handle a pass under pressure and fight through the coverage easily.
